Lumbar puncture before imaging risks delay and is not the priority; mass effect or hemorrhage should be excluded first, and CSF can be obtained later to evaluate for inflammatory/infectious arteriopathy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage; subtle loss of gray-white differentiation in the left basal ganglia
MRI brain (DWI): acute infarct in the left lenticulostriate territory involving the caudate and putamen
MRA head: focal narrowing and beading of the supraclinoid left internal carotid artery and proximal M1
CBC: WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.6 g/dL, Plt 290 ×10³/µL
ESR 28 mm/hr, CRP 1.4 mg/dL
VZV PCR (CSF): pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the clinical course and imaging, what is the most likely underlying mechanism of this stroke?
options: Cardioembolism from an undiagnosed congenital heart defect
Focal cerebral arteriopathy of childhood
Moyamoya disease with chronic collateral formation in this clinical context
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Unilateral basal ganglia infarction with focal narrowing and beading of the distal ICA/proximal M1 weeks after varicella is the classic picture of focal cerebral arteriopathy (post-varicella arteriopathy), a leading cause of childhood arterial ischemic stroke. VZV-induced inflammation of the vessel wall produces this monophasic, often progressive stenosis. Cardioembolism typically lacks focal vessel-wall arteriopathy and the exam/echo would point elsewhere. Moyamoya shows bilateral terminal ICA stenosis with a 'puff of smoke' collateral network developing chronically, not an acute post-varicella unilateral lesion. Venous sinus thrombosis causes venous (often hemorrhagic, non-arterial-territory) infarcts and would show sinus filling defects, not arterial beading.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which antithrombotic strategy is most appropriate for acute and secondary prevention in this child?
options: Therapeutic warfarin targeting INR 2-3 as monotherapy
Intravenous tPA followed by mechanical thrombectomy
No antithrombotic therapy; corticosteroids alone
Aspirin 3-5 mg/kg/day for secondary prevention
stages.explanation: For childhood arterial ischemic stroke due to non-cardioembolic, non-dissection arteriopathy, daily aspirin (3-5 mg/kg/day) is the standard antithrombotic for secondary prevention per pediatric stroke guidelines. Anticoagulation with warfarin is reserved for cardioembolic sources or dissection, not focal arteriopathy. Thrombolysis/thrombectomy are not standard in young children with established basal ganglia infarct presenting outside well-defined criteria and carry uncertain pediatric evidence, especially with arteriopathy. Withholding antithrombotics is inappropriate given recurrence risk; corticosteroids may be considered adjunctively for inflammatory arteriopathy but do not replace antiplatelet therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond antiplatelet therapy, what additional treatment is most reasonable to consider for this post-varicella arteriopathy?
options: Immediate surgical EC-IC bypass as the immediate next step
Plasma exchange as the immediate next step
Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel
A course of corticosteroids with consideration of antiviral therapy
stages.explanation: Post-varicella focal cerebral arteriopathy is an inflammatory vessel-wall process; a course of corticosteroids is often used to limit progressive stenosis, and antiviral therapy (acyclovir) is considered when active VZV reactivation is suspected or CSF VZV is positive. Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy is not standard in children and increases bleeding risk without clear benefit. EC-IC bypass is a treatment for moyamoya, not monophasic post-varicella arteriopathy. Plasma exchange has no established role here and is used in other neuroinflammatory conditions. Serial vascular imaging is also recommended to monitor for progression or stabilization.

cases.diagnosis: Pediatric arterial ischemic stroke due to post-varicella focal cerebral arteriopathy
pearls: Acute focal deficits in a child are a stroke until proven otherwise; obtain emergent CT plus vessel imaging.
Post-varicella arteriopathy classically causes unilateral basal ganglia infarcts with distal ICA/proximal M1 stenosis weeks after chickenpox.
Aspirin 3-5 mg/kg/day is first-line secondary prevention for non-cardioembolic childhood arterial ischemic stroke.
Consider corticosteroids and antivirals for the inflammatory component, and follow vessel imaging serially for progression.

cases.title: Found Down in the Morning With a Weak Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Left-sided weakness and neglect discovered this morning after she was last normal the night before.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her husband at 8:00 AM after he found her confused with a drooping left face and a weak left arm. She was last seen completely normal at 11:00 PM the previous night when she went to bed; the husband woke and found her this way, so her last known well is roughly 9 hours ago by the clock, but symptoms were first witnessed only this morning. She has hypertension (amlodipine 10 mg daily), type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily), and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation for which she admits she frequently forgets her apixaban. She last took apixaban 'a few days ago.' No prior stroke. No recent surgery, GI bleeding, or trauma. No known drug allergies. She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago) and drinks alcohol socially. Family history includes a brother with atrial fibrillation. On arrival the time from last known well is now approximately 16 hours given evaluation and transport delays; she cannot give further history due to neglect and inattention.
cases.exam: Awake, inattentive with left hemispatial neglect and gaze preference to the right. Left homonymous hemianopia by confrontation. Dense left central facial droop, left arm plegic (0/5), left leg antigravity briefly then falls (2/5). Left-sided sensory extinction to double simultaneous stimulation. Dysarthria present; comprehension intact. NIHSS calculated at 16. Irregularly irregular pulse without murmur. No carotid bruit. No meningismus, no signs of trauma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a last known well of 16 hours ago, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Administer IV alteplase immediately based on the NIHSS as the first step today
Obtain MRI brain only and defer any vascular imaging as the first step today
Obtain CT angiography
Admit for supportive care; no acute intervention is possible beyond 6 hours
stages.explanation: At 16 hours from last known well, IV thrombolysis is outside the accepted time window, but extended-window mechanical thrombectomy may still benefit selected patients. The key is identifying a large vessel occlusion and a favorable perfusion mismatch, so CTA (for LVO) and CT perfusion (for core/penumbra mismatch) are the correct next steps. IV alteplase at 16 hours is contraindicated. MRI alone without vascular imaging would miss the LVO and delay triage; perfusion imaging is needed to apply DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria. Declaring no intervention possible is incorrect because extended-window thrombectomy trials demonstrated benefit up to 16-24 hours in selected patients.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT: hyperdense right MCA sign; ASPECTS 8; no hemorrhage
CTA: occlusion of the right M1 segment; patent cervical carotids
CT perfusion: ischemic core 18 mL; Tmax >6 s volume 110 mL; mismatch ratio ~6
Glucose 142 mg/dL
INR 1.1; aPTT 30 s; Platelets 232 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; eGFR >60

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the perfusion data, does this patient meet criteria for endovascular thrombectomy in the extended window?
options: No, because thrombectomy is only proven within 6 hours as the primary diagnosis
No, because the core infarct must be 0 mL to qualify as the primary diagnosis
Yes, the small core with a large perfusion mismatch meets DAWN/DEFUSE-3 selection criteria
Yes, but only after IV alteplase is given first as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: DEFUSE-3 (6-16 h) and DAWN (6-24 h) established that patients with a small ischemic core and a substantial clinical-imaging or perfusion mismatch benefit from thrombectomy in the extended window. A core of 18 mL with a Tmax>6s volume of 110 mL gives a large mismatch, clearly favorable. A 0 mL core is not required; qualifying cores can be up to ~70 mL with appropriate mismatch/age criteria. Thrombectomy is proven beyond 6 hours precisely because of these trials. IV alteplase is not a prerequisite and is contraindicated here given the time window, so thrombectomy should proceed directly.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging thrombectomy, how should her blood pressure of 168/92 mmHg be managed?
options: No blood pressure monitoring is needed until after recanalization
Lower aggressively to below 120/80 mmHg before the procedure as monotherapy
Start a nitroprusside drip targeting 140/90 mmHg as initial therapy
Permit permissive hypertension
stages.explanation: Before reperfusion, cerebral perfusion depends on collateral flow, so permissive hypertension is maintained; for thrombectomy candidates BP is generally kept below 185/110 mmHg but not lowered aggressively, preserving penumbral perfusion. Her 168/92 is acceptable and needs no immediate treatment. Driving BP below 120/80 risks extending the infarct by reducing collateral flow. A nitroprusside drip to 140/90 is overly aggressive pre-reperfusion and nitroprusside can raise intracranial pressure. BP must be monitored closely throughout, especially after recanalization when targets tighten to reduce reperfusion hemorrhage risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful thrombectomy with TICI 3 reperfusion, what is the most appropriate post-procedure plan regarding her atrial fibrillation?
options: Admit to a stroke/neuro ICU, monitor with repeat imaging,
Discharge home the same day on aspirin alone with appropriate follow-up
Start a heparin drip immediately for full anticoagulation with appropriate follow-up
Resume apixaban immediately in the angiography suite with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Post-thrombectomy patients require close neuro-ICU monitoring for reperfusion hemorrhage and hemodynamic management. Although she has cardioembolic AF requiring long-term anticoagulation, restarting it is timed to infarct size and bleeding risk (the '1-3-6-12 day' principle), guided by follow-up imaging, rather than started immediately. Resuming apixaban in the suite or starting a heparin drip immediately after a large reperfused infarct risks hemorrhagic transformation. Same-day discharge on aspirin alone is unsafe after a large stroke with an LVO and does not address her need for anticoagulation and monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Right MCA (M1) large vessel occlusion treated with extended-window mechanical thrombectomy
pearls: Wake-up and late-presenting strokes can still be treated: image for LVO and perfusion mismatch rather than excluding by clock alone.
DEFUSE-3 (6-16 h) and DAWN (6-24 h) select thrombectomy candidates by small core and large mismatch.
Maintain permissive hypertension (<185/110 mmHg) before reperfusion to preserve penumbral collateral flow.
After recanalization, time anticoagulation for cardioembolic AF to infarct size and hemorrhagic risk.

cases.title: Sudden Weakness With a Cold, Pulseless-Feeling Neck Vessel
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden right arm and leg weakness with difficulty speaking that started 90 minutes ago.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents to the ED 90 minutes after the abrupt onset of right-sided weakness and slurred, effortful speech that began while he was eating breakfast. His wife called EMS immediately. He has poorly controlled hypertension (lisinopril 40 mg daily, intermittently taken), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg daily), and a 40 pack-year smoking history; he continues to smoke. He reports several episodes of transient right hand clumsiness over the past two weeks that he ignored. No prior stroke, no atrial fibrillation, no recent trauma or surgery. He had a colonoscopy 6 months ago that was normal. No known drug allergies. He drinks two beers nightly. Family history is significant for a father who died of myocardial infarction at 58 and a brother with peripheral arterial disease. He works as a long-haul truck driver and has not seen a physician in three years. On arrival he is anxious and aware of his deficits, with last known well clearly documented at 90 minutes prior.
cases.exam: Alert with non-fluent dysarthria and mild aphasia; comprehension largely intact. Left gaze preference. Right central facial droop. Right arm plegic (0/5), right leg 2/5. Right-sided sensory loss. Right hyperreflexia with extensor plantar response. NIHSS 15. A left carotid bruit is heard; the left carotid upstroke feels diminished compared with the right. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur. No meningismus, no head trauma, no papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: At 90 minutes from onset with NIHSS 15 and BP 192/104, what is the most appropriate immediate management while imaging is obtained?
options: Start a heparin drip empirically for presumed cardioembolism in this clinical context
Lower blood pressure to <185/110 mmHg
Withhold all therapy until carotid ultrasound is completed as the first step today
Give IV alteplase regardless of the blood pressure as the first step today
stages.explanation: He is within the thrombolysis window with a disabling deficit. Eligibility for IV alteplase requires BP below 185/110 mmHg, so controlled lowering (e.g., labetalol or nicardipine) is appropriate while non-contrast CT excludes hemorrhage and CTA evaluates for large vessel occlusion. Withholding therapy for carotid ultrasound wastes the treatment window. Giving alteplase at 192/104 violates BP criteria and increases hemorrhage risk. Empiric heparin is not indicated acutely, raises bleeding risk, and does not address the urgent reperfusion question; mechanism is undetermined and his bruit suggests large-artery atherosclerosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT: no hemorrhage; ASPECTS 9; hyperdense left MCA
CTA neck: complete occlusion of the cervical left internal carotid artery at its origin
CTA head: occlusion of the left M1 segment (tandem lesion)
CT perfusion: core 12 mL; penumbra 95 mL; large mismatch
Glucose 138 mg/dL; INR 1.0; Platelets 245 ×10³/µL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this combination of cervical ICA occlusion plus intracranial M1 occlusion best characterized?
options: Cardioembolic occlusion of a single vessel as the primary diagnosis
Carotid dissection with no intracranial involvement as the primary diagnosis
Isolated intracranial atherosclerotic disease as the primary diagnosis
A tandem occlusion involving both the extracranial cervical carotid
stages.explanation: A tandem occlusion is the simultaneous occlusion of the extracranial cervical carotid (often atherosclerotic) and an intracranial artery (here the M1), and is present in roughly 10-20% of anterior-circulation LVO strokes. His stereotyped TIAs, bruit, diminished upstroke, and atherosclerotic risk factors fit an atherosclerotic carotid origin lesion that embolized intracranially or caused flow failure. Isolated intracranial disease would not show a cervical occlusion. Dissection is possible but less likely given no trauma and heavy atherosclerotic burden, and it would not explain the M1 clot alone. The sinus rhythm and clinical picture argue against a purely cardioembolic single-vessel event.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After IV thrombolysis is given, what is the most appropriate endovascular strategy for this tandem occlusion?
options: Carotid endarterectomy in the OR before any intracranial intervention
Acute carotid stenting plus mechanical thrombectomy of the intracranial occlusion
Thrombectomy of the intracranial clot only, ignoring the cervical occlusion
Medical management alone with no endovascular therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Tandem occlusions are managed endovascularly by addressing both lesions: mechanical thrombectomy recanalizes the intracranial M1, and the cervical carotid is typically treated with angioplasty/stenting to restore inflow and prevent re-occlusion. Treating only the intracranial clot leaves a high-grade proximal lesion that can re-occlude and limits access. Acute carotid endarterectomy is not the standard emergent approach in this setting and delays intracranial recanalization. Medical management alone forgoes proven thrombectomy benefit for a large mismatch with disabling LVO. Periprocedural antiplatelet therapy is needed when stenting, balanced against recent thrombolysis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After successful stenting and thrombectomy with TICI 2b reperfusion, what is the most important early antithrombotic consideration given the new carotid stent?
options: No antiplatelet therapy is needed because thrombolysis was already given as the leading concern
Balance dual antiplatelet therapy to maintain stent patency against hemorrhagic risk after recent tPA
Start full-dose warfarin immediately as the leading concern
Begin triple therapy with aspirin, clopidogrel, and a DOAC at once as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A freshly placed carotid stent requires antiplatelet therapy to prevent acute in-stent thrombosis, but the patient just received IV alteplase and underwent reperfusion of a sizable infarct, raising hemorrhage risk. Management individualizes timing and intensity of dual antiplatelet therapy (often with careful loading and follow-up imaging) to balance stent patency against intracranial bleeding. Giving no antiplatelet risks stent thrombosis and re-occlusion. Immediate full-dose warfarin is inappropriate and does not protect the stent acutely. Triple therapy markedly increases bleeding risk and is not warranted here without a separate anticoagulation indication.

cases.diagnosis: Tandem occlusion (cervical left ICA plus intracranial left M1) treated with carotid stenting and thrombectomy
pearls: Suspect tandem occlusion when an anterior LVO is accompanied by a cervical carotid occlusion, a bruit, or diminished carotid upstroke.
Tandem occlusions are treated by recanalizing the intracranial clot and addressing the cervical lesion, often with acute stenting.
Maintain BP <185/110 mmHg to remain alteplase-eligible while pursuing CTA and perfusion imaging.
After acute carotid stenting plus tPA, individualize antiplatelet timing to balance stent patency against hemorrhagic transformation.

cases.title: New Chest Strain and a Weak Side After Collapse
cases.chiefComplaint: Right-sided weakness and inattention with a rising troponin noted on arrival to the ICU.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman was admitted to the neuro ICU after presenting with sudden left-sided weakness, gaze deviation, and profound inattention. CT angiography showed a right M2 occlusion and she underwent successful thrombectomy 6 hours earlier; she now has a large right insular and frontoparietal infarct. Several hours after the procedure the bedside monitor showed ST-segment changes and her troponin returned elevated. She has hypertension (hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily) and osteoporosis (alendronate weekly). No prior coronary disease, no diabetes. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. Two days before this event her husband of 45 years died suddenly, and she has been acutely grief-stricken. No known drug allergies. Family history is unremarkable for early cardiac disease. She lives independently. On transfer she remains hemodynamically borderline with intermittent hypotension and is being monitored for cardiac and neurologic deterioration.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable. Left central facial droop, left arm plegic, left leg with minimal movement; left neglect and right gaze preference persist. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia, a soft S3 gallop, and bibasilar crackles. Jugular venous pressure mildly elevated. Extremities cool with delayed capillary refill. No murmurs of valvular disease. Abdomen soft. No focal cardiac chest wall tenderness; she cannot fully report chest symptoms due to neglect and drowsiness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the elevated troponin, ST changes, and new heart failure signs after a large insular stroke, what is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Start thrombolytics for myocardial infarction as the first step today
Immediate cardiac catheterization and full-dose anticoagulation for presumed STEMI
Obtain an ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram to characterize ventricular function and wall motion
Attribute the troponin to the stroke alone and take no further cardiac action in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The combination of troponin elevation, ST changes, and acute heart failure after a large insular stroke warrants prompt characterization with ECG and echocardiography to assess ejection fraction and regional wall motion, distinguishing neurogenic stunned myocardium/Takotsubo from an acute coronary occlusion. Rushing to catheterization with full anticoagulation in a patient hours after a large reperfused infarct risks hemorrhagic transformation and may be unnecessary if there is no obstructive coronary lesion. Ignoring the troponin misses a treatable, hemodynamically significant cardiac process. Thrombolytics are absolutely contraindicated after recent large stroke and reperfusion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Troponin I 4.8 ng/mL (ref <0.04), trending up then plateauing
ECG: sinus tachycardia with diffuse T-wave inversions and QT prolongation (QTc 510 ms)
TTE: left ventricular ejection fraction 35%; akinesis of the apex with hyperdynamic base (apical ballooning)
BNP 820 pg/mL
Coronary angiography (performed): no obstructive coronary artery disease
Basic metabolic panel: K 3.4 mEq/L, Mg 1.7 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What cardiac diagnosis best explains the apical ballooning with non-obstructive coronaries in this patient?
options: Neurogenic stunned myocardium / Takotsubo cardiomyopathy
Acute anterior STEMI from LAD occlusion
Chronic ischemic cardiomyopathy as the primary diagnosis
Infective endocarditis with embolic stroke
stages.explanation: Apical akinesis with a hyperdynamic base ('apical ballooning'), reduced EF, troponin elevation, marked QT prolongation, and non-obstructive coronaries, in the setting of an acute insular stroke and intense emotional stress (her husband's death), is classic for neurogenic stunned myocardium/Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. The insular cortex modulates autonomic output; insular infarction triggers a catecholamine surge causing this reversible wall-motion pattern. An LAD STEMI would show an occluded vessel on angiography. Endocarditis would have vegetations and an embolic mechanism with fever/murmur. Chronic ischemic cardiomyopathy would not present acutely with reversible apical ballooning and clean coronaries.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hypotensive (98/62) with EF 35% and pulmonary congestion. What is the most appropriate hemodynamic management priority?
options: Start a high-dose epinephrine infusion to raise the blood pressure as initial therapy
Aggressive beta-blockade and high-dose diuresis regardless of pressure as initial therapy
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation immediately for the apical akinesis as initial therapy
Supportive care with cautious fluids/inotropes as needed, correct electrolytes, monitor the QT,
stages.explanation: Takotsubo is usually reversible, so management is supportive: careful hemodynamic support (judicious fluids; inotropes only if truly needed), correction of hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, and close QT monitoring to prevent torsades. Catecholaminergic agents like epinephrine can worsen the underlying catecholamine-driven stunning and are generally avoided; mechanical support is preferred if shock is refractory. Aggressive beta-blockade and diuresis in a hypotensive patient can precipitate cardiogenic collapse. Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation for apical akinesis is risky soon after a large reperfused infarct; LV thrombus risk is weighed against hemorrhagic transformation, often with imaging surveillance first.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the expected course and appropriate follow-up for her cardiomyopathy?
options: Urgent coronary artery bypass grafting with appropriate follow-up
Permanent severe systolic dysfunction requiring immediate ICD placement
Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy as for coronary stenting with appropriate follow-up
Typically reversible over days to weeks
stages.explanation: Takotsubo/neurogenic stunned myocardium typically recovers over days to weeks once the catecholamine surge resolves, so follow-up echocardiography is arranged to document normalization of EF and wall motion. Immediate ICD placement is not indicated for a reversible cardiomyopathy, though QT and arrhythmia monitoring are essential during the acute phase. Dual antiplatelet therapy as for coronary stenting is inappropriate because there is no obstructive coronary lesion or stent. CABG is not indicated with non-obstructive coronaries. The neurologic injury and overall prognosis are usually driven by the stroke rather than the transient cardiomyopathy.

cases.diagnosis: Neurogenic stunned myocardium (Takotsubo cardiomyopathy) complicating a large right insular stroke
pearls: Insular cortex infarcts disrupt autonomic control and can trigger a catecholamine surge causing Takotsubo cardiomyopathy.
Apical ballooning with non-obstructive coronaries, troponin elevation, and a long QT after stroke or acute stress is the hallmark.
Management is supportive and reversible-focused; avoid catecholamines, correct electrolytes, and watch the QT for torsades.
Confirm recovery with repeat echocardiography; the stroke, not the transient cardiomyopathy, usually drives prognosis.

cases.title: New Weakness on the Table During a Neck Artery Procedure
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute left-sided weakness developing during a right carotid revascularization procedure.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man is undergoing elective right carotid artery stenting for a symptomatic 80% right internal carotid stenosis discovered after a recent right-hemisphere TIA (transient left arm weakness two weeks ago). He has hypertension (amlodipine 10 mg, losartan 100 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (rosuvastatin 20 mg daily), and coronary artery disease with prior PCI. He was loaded with aspirin 325 mg and clopidogrel 600 mg before the procedure and is on heparin intraoperatively with an appropriate ACT. He has a 50 pack-year smoking history. No known drug allergies. Family history includes a brother with carotid disease. He was selected for stenting rather than endarterectomy due to a high surgical neck and cardiac comorbidity. During deployment of the stent across the lesion and post-dilation angioplasty, the awake patient suddenly develops slurred speech and left facial and arm weakness, prompting immediate intraoperative assessment.
cases.exam: Awake on the angiography table. New dysarthria with a left central facial droop. Left arm drift to plegia within seconds, left leg mildly weak (4/5). Left-sided neglect emerging. Bradycardic to the low 50s during balloon inflation. No chest pain. Right groin access site without hematoma. Pupils equal and reactive. The interventionalist notes the deficit appeared during post-stent balloon angioplasty.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most likely mechanism of this acute neurologic deficit occurring during stent post-dilation?
options: A new lacunar infarct unrelated to the procedure as the leading concern
Contrast-induced encephalopathy as the leading concern
Hyperperfusion syndrome from restored blood flow as the leading concern
Distal embolization of atherosclerotic/thrombotic debris into the intracranial circulation
stages.explanation: Acute focal deficits arising during stent deployment and balloon post-dilation are most often due to distal embolization, when manipulation of the plaque releases atheromatous or thrombotic debris that lodges in the intracranial vessels. Hyperperfusion syndrome typically presents hours to days later with headache, seizures, or hemorrhage, not instantly during balloon inflation. Contrast-induced encephalopathy can cause cortical signs but usually with cortical blindness/encephalopathy and a characteristic time course, not an immediate hemiparesis tied to manipulation. A coincidental unrelated lacune is far less likely than procedure-related embolization given the precise temporal association with plaque manipulation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The patient also became bradycardic during balloon inflation. What is the best immediate management of the bradycardia and the new deficit?
options: Reverse heparin with protamine and terminate all intervention as the first step today
Treat the carotid sinus-mediated bradycardia , maintain blood pressure,
Stop the procedure and extubate immediately for a CT scan only as the first step today
Give IV alteplase on the table without imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: Balloon inflation across the carotid bulb stimulates the carotid sinus and causes reflex bradycardia/hypotension, treated with anticholinergics (atropine/glycopyrrolate) and BP support to maintain cerebral perfusion. Simultaneously, because the patient already has angiographic access, immediate cerebral angiography can localize the embolic occlusion for possible aspiration/thrombectomy. Stopping for a CT only forfeits the opportunity for immediate angiographic rescue. IV alteplase is contraindicated intra-arterially in a heparinized, recently instrumented patient and is not the on-table answer. Reversing heparin with protamine promotes thrombosis on the fresh stent and is dangerous.

stages.label: ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Cerebral angiogram: new filling defect in the right M2 (inferior division) branch consistent with embolic occlusion
Right ICA stent: widely patent, good apposition, no residual stenosis
No extravasation or vessel perforation
ACT 280 seconds (therapeutic)
No embolic protection device filter was deployed during the case

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a documented right M2 embolic occlusion and a patent stent, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Observe and reassess in 24 hours as initial therapy
Proceed with intra-arterial mechanical thrombectomy/aspiration of the M2 occlusion
Administer protamine to reverse anticoagulation as initial therapy
Remove the carotid stent as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An identified distal embolic occlusion causing an acute disabling deficit, with access already established, is best treated by immediate intra-arterial thrombectomy/aspiration to recanalize the M2 branch and limit infarction. Observing for 24 hours allows the penumbra to infarct. Removing the stent is technically inappropriate and unnecessary since it is patent and well-apposed. Reversing anticoagulation with protamine would promote thrombosis on the fresh stent and the embolic clot, worsening ischemia. Time is brain: the on-table angiographic finding should prompt prompt mechanical retrieval while perfusion is maintained.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: This complication highlights the role of which device in reducing periprocedural stroke during carotid artery stenting?
options: Pulmonary artery catheter as the immediate next step
Intra-aortic balloon pump as the immediate next step
Embolic protection device
Inferior vena cava filter as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Embolic protection devices, either distal filters that catch debris downstream or proximal flow-reversal/occlusion systems, are used during carotid artery stenting to capture atheromatous debris liberated during plaque manipulation and reduce periprocedural embolic stroke. This case had no protection device deployed, increasing embolic risk during post-dilation. An intra-aortic balloon pump supports cardiac output, not cerebral embolic protection. An IVC filter prevents pulmonary embolism from lower-extremity venous thrombi, irrelevant to arterial cerebral embolization. A pulmonary artery catheter monitors hemodynamics and offers no embolic protection.

cases.diagnosis: Periprocedural embolic stroke during carotid artery stenting (distal embolization)
pearls: Deficits appearing during stent deployment or post-dilation are usually distal embolization of plaque/thrombus, not hyperperfusion.
Balloon inflation at the carotid bulb triggers carotid sinus reflex bradycardia/hypotension; treat with anticholinergics and BP support.
With access in place, an on-table embolic occlusion is treated by immediate intra-arterial thrombectomy/aspiration.
Embolic protection devices (distal filters or proximal flow reversal) reduce periprocedural embolic stroke during carotid stenting.

cases.title: Another Stroke Despite Taking My Aspirin
cases.chiefComplaint: Transient slurred speech and right hand numbness one week ago in a patient already on aspirin.
cases.history: A 69-year-old woman presents to the stroke clinic after an episode of slurred speech and right hand numbness lasting 20 minutes one week ago, which fully resolved. This is her second cerebrovascular event: eight months ago she had a small left subcortical ischemic stroke and was started on aspirin 81 mg daily, which she takes reliably. She has hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg, lisinopril 20 mg daily), type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily, A1c 8.2%), and hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg daily). She does not smoke and does not drink. No palpitations or syncope. No prior atrial fibrillation documented on a 24-hour Holter done after her first stroke. No known drug allergies. Family history includes a mother with stroke at age 75. She is adherent with all medications and confirms she has not missed aspirin doses. She is frustrated that she had another event 'on a blood thinner' and wants to understand why. Her LDL at last visit was 95 mg/dL.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, fluent speech with no current dysarthria. Cranial nerves intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout with no drift. Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick. Reflexes symmetric, plantar responses flexor. Coordination and gait normal. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmurs; no carotid bruits appreciated. No residual deficits from the prior stroke on today's exam. Blood pressure mildly elevated in clinic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After a recurrent ischemic event on aspirin, what is the most important first step before changing antithrombotic therapy?
options: Re-investigate the stroke mechanism, including vessel imaging, prolonged cardiac monitoring,
Double the aspirin dose to 325 mg daily as the first step today
Immediately switch to warfarin without further workup as the first step today
Add a DOAC empirically on top of aspirin as the first step today
stages.explanation: Recurrent ischemic stroke on antiplatelet therapy ('aspirin failure') should prompt a systematic reassessment of mechanism rather than reflexive drug changes. Vessel imaging (carotid/intracranial), prolonged cardiac monitoring for occult atrial fibrillation, and review of risk-factor control (BP, A1c, LDL) identify the true driver and the correct therapy. Switching straight to warfarin without an anticoagulation indication is inappropriate. Doubling aspirin does not improve efficacy and increases bleeding. Empirically adding a DOAC to aspirin without a documented cardioembolic source increases bleeding risk without proven benefit. The mechanism dictates whether antiplatelet intensification or anticoagulation is appropriate.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
MRI brain: new punctate left corona radiata infarct plus the prior subcortical lacune
Carotid duplex and CTA neck: <30% bilateral carotid stenosis, no significant intracranial stenosis
30-day ambulatory cardiac monitor: no atrial fibrillation; rare PACs
Transthoracic echo: normal LV function, no thrombus, no PFO on bubble study
Hemoglobin A1c 8.2%; LDL 95 mg/dL
BP log (home): averaging 150/90 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on this workup, what is the most likely mechanism of her recurrent strokes?
options: Small-vessel disease driven by poorly controlled hypertension
Large-artery atherosclerosis with high-grade carotid stenosis
Cardioembolism from paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as the primary diagnosis
Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Recurrent small deep (subcortical/corona radiata) infarcts with no significant large-artery stenosis, no atrial fibrillation on 30-day monitoring, and a normal echo point to small-vessel (lacunar) disease. Her uncontrolled hypertension (home BP ~150/90), diabetes (A1c 8.2%), and suboptimal LDL drive lipohyalinosis of penetrating arteries. Cardioembolism is unlikely given negative prolonged monitoring and normal echo. Large-artery atherosclerosis is excluded by <30% stenosis on imaging. PFO-related paradoxical embolism is unlikely with a negative bubble study and is typically considered in younger patients with cortical embolic patterns, not recurrent deep lacunes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given lacunar mechanism and prior aspirin 'failure,' what is the most appropriate change to antithrombotic and risk-factor management?
options: Intensify risk-factor control
Stop all antithrombotic therapy as initial therapy
Begin indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus clopidogrel as initial therapy
Switch to long-term anticoagulation with apixaban as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For small-vessel disease, the highest-yield intervention is aggressive risk-factor control: tighter blood pressure, better glycemic control, and high-intensity statin to lower LDL, alongside continued single antiplatelet therapy. Long-term anticoagulation has no benefit without a cardioembolic source and increases hemorrhage risk. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin plus clopidogrel) raises bleeding risk over time (per SPS3, harmful for long-term lacunar secondary prevention) and is reserved for short courses after minor stroke/TIA or specific stenoses. Stopping antithrombotics entirely would remove proven secondary-prevention benefit. The 'failure' largely reflects undertreated vascular risk factors.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She asks whether switching aspirin to clopidogrel monotherapy would have prevented this. What is the best evidence-based response?
options: Only anticoagulation prevents lacunar strokes as the immediate next step
Clopidogrel is dramatically superior to aspirin and should always replace it as the immediate next step
Antiplatelet choice is irrelevant in all stroke subtypes as the immediate next step
Single antiplatelet agents have broadly similar efficacy
stages.explanation: Single antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) have broadly comparable efficacy for secondary prevention; clopidogrel monotherapy is a reasonable alternative after an event on aspirin, but it is not dramatically superior, and the dominant lever in small-vessel disease is risk-factor optimization. Claiming clopidogrel is always superior overstates the evidence. Anticoagulation is not indicated for lacunar disease without a cardioembolic source. Saying antiplatelet choice is irrelevant in all subtypes is false, since choice and combinations matter in settings like recent minor stroke/TIA (short-term DAPT) or symptomatic intracranial stenosis. Tailoring therapy to mechanism is key.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent lacunar (small-vessel) stroke from uncontrolled vascular risk factors, not true antiplatelet failure
pearls: Recurrent stroke 'on aspirin' should trigger a mechanism workup before reflexively escalating antithrombotics.
Recurrent deep lacunes with clean vessels, no AF, and a normal echo point to hypertensive/diabetic small-vessel disease.
Long-term dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding without benefit in lacunar secondary prevention (SPS3).
Aggressive BP, glucose, and LDL control outweighs antiplatelet switching for preventing small-vessel strokes.

cases.title: A Whisper-Thin Neck Artery Found on a Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: Two brief episodes of transient left arm weakness with an alarming carotid finding on imaging.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is referred to the stroke clinic after two episodes of transient left arm and hand weakness lasting several minutes each over the past month, both fully resolved. His primary care physician ordered a CTA that showed a severely narrowed right internal carotid artery. He has hypertension (chlorthalidone 25 mg, amlodipine 10 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 80 mg daily, started recently), and a 45 pack-year smoking history; he still smokes. No diabetes. No prior stroke or cardiac disease. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily, started after the first TIA. No known drug allergies. He drinks one or two beers on weekends. Family history includes a father with stroke and a brother with carotid stenosis. He is otherwise active and works part-time. He is anxious about the imaging report describing a 'string sign' and wants to know whether he needs surgery. He has had no events in the past two weeks since aspirin was started and his statin was increased.
cases.exam: Alert and fully oriented with fluent speech and no dysarthria. Cranial nerves intact. Full strength throughout with no pronator drift. Sensation normal. Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor. No current focal deficit. A faint right cervical bruit is heard, though high-grade lesions can be silent. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur. Gait normal. Funduscopic exam without Hollenhorst plaques.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CTA describes a 'string sign.' What does this finding represent?
options: A normal-caliber carotid with a calcified plaque as the primary diagnosis
A near-occlusion with a trickle of flow through a severely collapsed, narrowed distal lumen
An acute carotid dissection with intramural hematoma as the primary diagnosis
Complete chronic occlusion of the carotid with no flow as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The 'string sign' describes carotid near-occlusion: a critically high-grade stenosis with only a thin, threadlike trickle of contrast through a collapsed distal internal carotid lumen, often with distal vessel narrowing from low flow. It is not a complete occlusion (some flow persists) and distinguishing the two is critical because management differs. A normal-caliber vessel with calcified plaque would not show a threadlike lumen. Acute dissection shows an intimal flap or crescentic intramural hematoma with a tapered ('flame') narrowing, a different appearance and clinical context. Recognizing near-occlusion changes the risk-benefit calculus for intervention.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CTA neck: right ICA near-occlusion with a 'string sign'; threadlike distal lumen and reduced distal ICA caliber
Left ICA: 40% stenosis, smooth plaque
Digital subtraction angiography (confirmatory): delayed, threadlike opacification of the right ICA consistent with near-occlusion, distal reconstitution present
MRI brain: small scattered right-hemisphere cortical DWI lesions (embolic pattern)
LDL 70 mg/dL on high-intensity statin

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the central management controversy for symptomatic carotid near-occlusion (string sign)?
options: Whether to use aspirin or no antithrombotic at all as initial therapy
Whether to perform bilateral simultaneous endarterectomy as initial therapy
Whether revascularization offers benefit over intensive medical therapy, given near-occlusions were largely excluded from or underpowered in the major trials
Whether to anticoagulate with warfarin versus a DOAC as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The core controversy is whether revascularization (endarterectomy or stenting) benefits symptomatic near-occlusion compared with intensive medical therapy. Near-occlusions were excluded from or underrepresented in the landmark trials (NASCET/ECST), and subgroup data did not show clear surgical benefit, partly because the collapsed distal vessel and low flow lower the embolic stroke risk and complicate intervention. The choice between aspirin and no therapy is not the dilemma; antiplatelet therapy is standard. Anticoagulation (warfarin vs DOAC) is not the issue for atherosclerotic carotid disease. Bilateral simultaneous endarterectomy is not standard and is unsafe; the contralateral 40% lesion does not warrant surgery.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given recurrent symptoms now controlled on therapy, what is the most reasonable initial approach for this near-occlusion?
options: No treatment and routine follow-up in one year as the immediate next step
Urgent emergency revascularization regardless of symptom status as the immediate next step
Intensive medical therapy with close follow-up, reserving revascularization for refractory or recurrent symptoms
Immediate carotid occlusion with coils as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Because trial evidence does not clearly favor revascularization for near-occlusion and the low-flow collapsed segment lowers embolic risk, intensive medical therapy is a reasonable first-line strategy: antiplatelet therapy, high-intensity statin, blood pressure control, and especially smoking cessation, with close clinical follow-up. Revascularization is considered for patients with recurrent or refractory symptoms despite optimal medical therapy. Emergency revascularization regardless of symptoms overtreats a lesion with uncertain surgical benefit. Deliberate carotid occlusion is not appropriate. Doing nothing with only annual follow-up undertreats a symptomatic patient who needs aggressive risk-factor modification and monitoring.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which single intervention will most reduce this patient's future stroke and vascular risk?
options: Adding a second antihypertensive only
Switching aspirin to clopidogrel
Smoking cessation
Increasing the statin beyond high-intensity dosing
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation is the single highest-impact intervention for this active smoker with carotid atherosclerosis, substantially lowering stroke, myocardial infarction, and progression of vascular disease. His BP is already reasonably controlled and an additional agent offers smaller incremental benefit. Switching aspirin to clopidogrel provides at best marginal, unproven added benefit in this context. He is already on high-intensity statin with an LDL of 70; pushing the dose higher yields diminishing returns and is not the dominant lever. Counseling and supporting cessation (with pharmacotherapy if needed) is the most powerful modifiable factor here.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic carotid near-occlusion (string sign) managed with intensive medical therapy
pearls: The 'string sign' is carotid near-occlusion: a threadlike trickle of flow through a collapsed distal lumen, distinct from full occlusion.
Near-occlusions were excluded/underpowered in NASCET-era trials, so revascularization benefit over medical therapy is uncertain.
Intensive medical therapy is a reasonable first line, reserving CEA/stenting for refractory or recurrent symptoms.
Smoking cessation is the highest-yield modifiable intervention in carotid atherosclerotic disease.

cases.title: Repeated Spells of a Dead Arm in One Day
cases.chiefComplaint: Four episodes of sudden right arm weakness and speech difficulty in the past 24 hours.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man comes to the ED reporting four discrete episodes over the past 24 hours, each with sudden right arm weakness and difficulty getting words out, lasting 5 to 15 minutes and resolving completely. The episodes are stereotyped, occurring with increasing frequency, the most recent two hours ago. Between episodes he feels completely normal. He has hypertension (hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily) and hyperlipidemia (simvastatin 20 mg daily) but admits poor adherence. He has a 35 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. No prior stroke, no atrial fibrillation, no recent illness or trauma. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes a father with stroke at 60. He is currently asymptomatic and worried, asking whether he can just go home since the spells keep resolving. He takes no antiplatelet agent at present. He has not seen a physician in two years and his blood pressure is usually untreated for long stretches.
cases.exam: Currently asymptomatic, alert, fully oriented with fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact. Motor 5/5 throughout with no pronator drift. Sensation intact. Reflexes symmetric, plantar responses flexor. Coordination and gait normal. A left carotid bruit is auscultated. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur. No residual deficits between spells. Blood pressure elevated; no signs of trauma or meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Multiple stereotyped, increasingly frequent TIAs in 24 hours describe which clinical syndrome, and what is the correct disposition?
options: Conversion disorder; reassure and discharge as the first step today
Crescendo TIA, a neurologic emergency warranting urgent admission
Benign recurrent vertigo; discharge with outpatient follow-up
Complex partial seizures; start an antiepileptic and discharge
stages.explanation: Multiple stereotyped TIAs that are increasing in frequency over a short period define crescendo TIA, which signals an unstable, often high-grade arterial lesion or active thromboembolic source and a high short-term risk of completed stroke. It is a neurologic emergency requiring urgent admission and expedited evaluation, not discharge. Recurrent vertigo would present with prominent dizziness, not stereotyped hemiparesis/aphasia. Conversion disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion and inappropriate given vascular risk factors and a carotid bruit. Complex partial seizures usually involve automatisms or altered awareness with positive phenomena, not stereotyped negative motor/speech deficits tied to vascular territory.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage, no established infarct
MRI brain (DWI): tiny punctate left frontal cortical infarct
CTA neck: 85% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery with ulcerated plaque
Glucose 110 mg/dL; LDL 158 mg/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
ABCD2 score: 6 (high risk)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely mechanism of these crescendo TIAs?
options: Global hypoperfusion from dehydration as the primary diagnosis
Cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation as the primary diagnosis
Artery-to-artery thromboembolism from a high-grade ulcerated left carotid plaque
Small-vessel lacunar disease as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An 85% ulcerated left internal carotid stenosis ipsilateral to recurrent right-sided/aphasic events, with a small left cortical DWI lesion and normal sinus rhythm, indicates artery-to-artery thromboembolism from the unstable carotid plaque. The ulcerated, high-grade lesion sheds emboli intermittently, producing stereotyped, escalating spells. Cardioembolism is unlikely with sinus rhythm and a clear culprit carotid lesion. Small-vessel lacunar disease causes deep, not cortical, infarcts and would not explain the carotid findings or cortical signs like aphasia. Global hypoperfusion would cause watershed, bilateral, or non-stereotyped symptoms and is not supported by the focal, lateralized pattern and a specific stenotic culprit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive treatment, what is the most appropriate immediate medical management?
options: Begin warfarin for stroke prevention as initial therapy
Aggressively lower the blood pressure to normal and start a heparin drip as initial therapy
Start antiplatelet therapy , high-intensity statin,
Withhold all antithrombotics until after surgery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For high-risk TIA with high-grade symptomatic stenosis, initiate antiplatelet therapy (short-term dual antiplatelet, e.g., aspirin plus clopidogrel, is supported for high-risk TIA/minor stroke) and a high-intensity statin, while avoiding aggressive blood pressure reduction because the tight stenosis makes perfusion pressure-dependent. A heparin drip is not standard for atherosclerotic TIA and adds bleeding risk; aggressive BP normalization can precipitate stroke in a flow-limiting lesion. Withholding all antithrombotics until surgery leaves the patient unprotected during the highest-risk window. Warfarin is for cardioembolic sources, not symptomatic carotid atherosclerosis, and offers no advantage here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given a symptomatic 85% carotid stenosis with crescendo TIAs, what is the recommended definitive treatment and timing?
options: Lifelong medical therapy with no revascularization with appropriate follow-up
Immediate IV thrombolysis with appropriate follow-up
Early carotid revascularization, ideally within about 2 weeks of symptom onset
Carotid revascularization (endarterectomy or stenting) electively in 3 months
stages.explanation: Symptomatic high-grade (70-99%) carotid stenosis warrants carotid revascularization, and guidelines favor early intervention, ideally within about two weeks of the index event, because stroke risk is highest in that early window, particularly with crescendo symptoms. Waiting three months forgoes the greatest benefit and exposes the patient to interval stroke. Medical therapy alone is insufficient for a symptomatic 85% stenosis with unstable, escalating TIAs. IV thrombolysis treats acute ischemic stroke within the time window, not a patient who is currently asymptomatic between TIAs with a fixed treatable stenosis; it does not address the underlying lesion.

cases.diagnosis: Crescendo TIA from a high-grade ulcerated symptomatic carotid stenosis
pearls: Crescendo TIAs (multiple stereotyped, increasingly frequent spells) are a neurologic emergency heralding impending stroke.
An ulcerated high-grade carotid stenosis ipsilateral to symptoms suggests artery-to-artery thromboembolism.
Avoid aggressive BP lowering with a flow-limiting stenosis; start antiplatelet (often short-term DAPT) and high-intensity statin.
Symptomatic 70-99% carotid stenosis warrants early revascularization, ideally within about two weeks.

cases.title: Double Vision and a Clumsy Eye in a Middle-Aged Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden horizontal double vision and a sensation of imbalance starting three hours ago.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents to the ED with sudden horizontal double vision and mild imbalance that began three hours ago while at work. He denies headache, weakness, or numbness. He has hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg daily, often missed), hyperlipidemia (untreated), and a 30 pack-year smoking history; he continues to smoke. He has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 500 mg twice daily, with poor follow-up. No prior stroke, no prior demyelinating disease, no recent infection or trauma. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends. Family history includes a father with myocardial infarction and a mother with hypertension. He works as an electrician and noticed the double vision was worse when looking to one side. He is otherwise well and denies vertigo at rest, dysphagia, or limb weakness. He has never had visual symptoms before and is concerned he cannot drive home safely.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented with fluent speech. On rightward gaze, the left eye fails to adduct past midline while the right (abducting) eye shows horizontal nystagmus. Convergence is preserved. No ptosis; pupils equal and reactive. Remaining extraocular movements intact, no vertical gaze palsy. Mild gait unsteadiness but no limb ataxia, no facial weakness, no hemiparesis, no sensory loss. Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor. No dysarthria. Funduscopy normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Failure of the left eye to adduct on rightward gaze with abducting nystagmus of the right eye and preserved convergence is characteristic of what finding?
options: Left third nerve (oculomotor) palsy as the primary diagnosis
Myasthenia gravis with fatigable diplopia as the primary diagnosis
Left internuclear ophthalmoplegia from a medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion
Right sixth nerve (abducens) palsy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Impaired adduction of the eye ipsilateral to the lesion with abducting nystagmus of the contralateral eye, and preserved convergence, defines internuclear ophthalmoplegia from a lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF). The MLF connects the abducens nucleus to the contralateral oculomotor (medial rectus) subnucleus; here the left MLF is affected, so the left eye fails to adduct. A third nerve palsy would cause ptosis, a 'down-and-out' eye, and pupillary involvement, with impaired convergence too. A sixth nerve palsy impairs abduction, not adduction. Myasthenia can mimic INO but is fatigable, often with ptosis, and lacks a fixed vascular MLF localization.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a 54-year-old with vascular risk factors and acute INO, what is the most appropriate immediate evaluation?
options: Emergent stroke workup with brain imaging and vascular assessment
Reassure and discharge as a benign ocular muscle strain as the first step today
Order an acetylcholine receptor antibody panel and discharge pending results
Start IV steroids for presumed multiple sclerosis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Acute INO in an older patient with multiple vascular risk factors is most likely an ischemic brainstem (pontine/midbrain) lesion of the MLF and warrants an emergent stroke workup, with MRI being far more sensitive than CT for small brainstem infarcts, plus vascular imaging and risk-factor evaluation. Discharging as ocular strain misses a stroke. Ordering antibody tests and discharging delays diagnosis of an acute vascular event; myasthenia is less likely given the fixed localization. Empiric steroids for presumed MS are inappropriate without imaging; MS-related INO is typical of younger patients and bilateral INO, and demyelination must be confirmed before immunotherapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain (DWI): small acute infarct in the dorsal pons involving the left medial longitudinal fasciculus
MRA head/neck: mild basilar artery atherosclerosis, no occlusion
Non-contrast CT: no hemorrhage
Glucose 168 mg/dL; LDL 162 mg/dL; A1c 8.6%
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
Lumbar puncture: not performed (imaging consistent with infarct)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the imaging, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's INO?
options: Brainstem neoplasm as the primary diagnosis
Multiple sclerosis demyelination as the primary diagnosis
Brainstem ischemic stroke from small-vessel/basilar atherosclerotic disease
Wernicke encephalopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A discrete DWI-positive infarct in the dorsal pons at the MLF in a 54-year-old with hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and smoking, plus basilar atherosclerosis, indicates an ischemic brainstem stroke from small-vessel or basilar atherosclerotic disease. MS demyelination typically affects younger patients, often causes bilateral INO, and shows demyelinating plaques rather than a single acute DWI infarct with a vascular risk profile. Wernicke encephalopathy causes ophthalmoplegia with confusion, ataxia, and a nutritional/alcohol history, with characteristic mammillary/periaqueductal changes, not a focal MLF infarct. A neoplasm would show a mass lesion, not a small acute diffusion-restricting infarct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate secondary prevention strategy for this brainstem ischemic stroke?
options: Long-term anticoagulation with warfarin as initial therapy
Disease-modifying therapy for multiple sclerosis as initial therapy
Thiamine repletion alone as initial therapy
Antiplatelet therapy plus high-intensity statin
stages.explanation: Secondary prevention for an atherosclerotic/small-vessel brainstem infarct is antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin or clopidogrel; short-term DAPT if a high-risk minor stroke/TIA) combined with a high-intensity statin and aggressive management of hypertension, diabetes, and smoking cessation. Long-term warfarin is reserved for cardioembolic sources, absent here with sinus rhythm and no cardiac source. MS disease-modifying therapy is inappropriate because the lesion is ischemic, not demyelinating. Thiamine repletion treats Wernicke encephalopathy, which is not the diagnosis. Optimizing all vascular risk factors, particularly his markedly elevated LDL and A1c and ongoing smoking, is essential to prevent recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Brainstem (dorsal pontine) ischemic stroke causing internuclear ophthalmoplegia from an MLF infarct
pearls: INO = impaired adduction ipsilateral to the lesion plus abducting nystagmus of the other eye, with preserved convergence, localizing to the MLF.
Acute INO in an older patient with vascular risk factors is a brainstem stroke until proven otherwise; MRI beats CT for the brainstem.
Bilateral INO and a younger patient suggest MS; unilateral INO with vascular risk factors suggests ischemia.
Treat ischemic brainstem stroke with antiplatelet therapy, high-intensity statin, and aggressive vascular risk-factor control.

cases.title: Worst Headache, Vomiting, and a Sinking Level of Consciousness
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe headache and left-sided weakness progressing to decreased responsiveness.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man was brought to the ED with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and left-sided weakness, then transferred to the ICU as his level of consciousness declined. He has long-standing poorly controlled hypertension (amlodipine 10 mg daily, frequently missed; he ran out of medication two weeks ago). He has hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg) and a 25 pack-year smoking history. No anticoagulant use, no known coagulopathy, no recent trauma. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol heavily on weekends. Family history includes a mother with hemorrhagic stroke. He works in construction. On arrival his blood pressure was markedly elevated. Over the next two hours in the ICU he became progressively drowsy, then difficult to arouse, with worsening headache and new upgaze difficulty, prompting urgent reassessment of his airway, intracranial status, and the need for neurosurgical intervention.
cases.exam: Stuporous, opens eyes only to pain, follows no commands. Left hemiparesis (arm and leg 1/5) with left facial droop. Eyes show impaired upgaze and small poorly reactive pupils; setting-sun appearance noted. Right-sided sensory response to pain present. Reflexes brisk on the left with an extensor plantar. Bradycardia with irregular respirations raises concern for rising intracranial pressure. No meningismus initially; neck not manipulated given concern for ICP.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A hypertensive patient with sudden headache, left hemiparesis, vomiting, and declining consciousness most likely has what, and what is the first diagnostic step?
options: Ischemic stroke; give IV thrombolysis immediately
Migraine with aura; give triptans as the first step today
Spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage
Meningitis; perform immediate lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Sudden severe headache, vomiting, focal deficit, and declining consciousness in a patient with markedly uncontrolled hypertension strongly suggest spontaneous (hypertensive) intracerebral hemorrhage, and the immediate step is an emergent non-contrast CT head to confirm the bleed, localize it, and assess for intraventricular extension and hydrocephalus. Thrombolysis would be catastrophic if given before excluding hemorrhage. Migraine does not cause progressive obtundation with hemiparesis and is a diagnosis of exclusion here. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when raised intracranial pressure or a mass lesion is suspected, given herniation risk; CT comes first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: 25 mL right thalamic hemorrhage with extension into the lateral and third ventricles
Intraventricular hemorrhage with dilation of both lateral ventricles and the third ventricle (acute obstructive hydrocephalus)
No cerebellar hemorrhage; effaced perimesencephalic cisterns
INR 1.0; Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
Glucose 150 mg/dL
GCS now 8 (E2 V2 M4)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What complication of this thalamic hemorrhage explains his rapid neurologic deterioration and upgaze palsy?
options: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as the primary diagnosis
Subarachnoid hemorrhage from an aneurysm as the primary diagnosis
Hemorrhagic transformation of an ischemic stroke
Intraventricular hemorrhage causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus
stages.explanation: The thalamic hemorrhage has extended into the ventricular system; intraventricular hemorrhage obstructs CSF outflow (third ventricle/aqueduct), producing acute obstructive hydrocephalus, which explains the rapidly worsening consciousness, the upgaze palsy/setting-sun sign (dorsal midbrain compression), and signs of rising ICP. This is not hemorrhagic transformation of an ischemic infarct, which would show an underlying infarct pattern. Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage centers in the basal cisterns/subarachnoid space rather than a primary thalamic parenchymal bleed with IVH, though the clinical picture can overlap. Venous sinus thrombosis causes venous infarcts/hemorrhages with sinus filling defects, not this deep hypertensive bleed with IVH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With GCS 8, acute obstructive hydrocephalus, and rising ICP, what is the most appropriate neurosurgical intervention?
options: Lumbar drain placement as initial therapy
Placement of an external ventricular drain to relieve hydrocephalus
Decompressive hemicraniectomy as initial therapy
Immediate craniotomy to evacuate the thalamic clot as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Acute obstructive hydrocephalus from intraventricular hemorrhage with a depressed GCS is treated by placing an external ventricular drain, which diverts CSF and blood, relieves hydrocephalus, and allows continuous ICP monitoring and management. Open craniotomy to evacuate a deep thalamic clot is generally not beneficial and risks further injury to eloquent deep structures. A lumbar drain is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a mass effect because it can precipitate downward herniation. Decompressive hemicraniectomy addresses malignant supratentorial swelling/large lobar lesions, not the hydrocephalus driving this deterioration; the EVD directly addresses the obstructed CSF pathways.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The EVD frequently clogs with clot and ventricular blood clears slowly. What adjunctive therapy has been studied to address persistent intraventricular hemorrhage?
options: Therapeutic systemic anticoagulation as the immediate next step
Intraventricular administration of a thrombolytic via the EVD to accelerate clot clearance
Oral nimodipine to dissolve the clot as the immediate next step
Systemic IV thrombolysis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Intraventricular thrombolysis, delivering a low-dose thrombolytic such as alteplase directly through the EVD, has been studied (e.g., CLEAR III) to accelerate clearance of intraventricular blood, maintain catheter patency, and reduce hydrocephalus burden; it improves clot resolution though its effect on functional outcome is modest and patient selection matters. Systemic IV thrombolysis is contraindicated in active intracerebral hemorrhage. Systemic anticoagulation would expand the bleed. Nimodipine is used to reduce vasospasm-related ischemia after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage and does not dissolve intraventricular clot. Intraventricular thrombolytic dosing is local, low-dose, and given specifically to address the clot obstructing CSF flow.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive thalamic intracerebral hemorrhage with intraventricular extension causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Deep (thalamic/basal ganglia) hemorrhage in an uncontrolled hypertensive patient is the classic hypertensive bleed; get an emergent non-contrast CT.
Intraventricular extension can obstruct CSF flow, causing acute hydrocephalus, upgaze palsy/setting-sun sign, and rapid decline.
An external ventricular drain relieves obstructive hydrocephalus and enables ICP monitoring; avoid lumbar drains with obstruction.
Intraventricular thrombolysis (CLEAR III) speeds clot clearance and maintains EVD patency, with modest functional benefit.

cases.title: Sudden Spinning and an Unsteady Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute onset of continuous room-spinning dizziness with inability to walk unaided.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents to the ED two hours after the abrupt onset of severe vertigo while gardening. The spinning is constant rather than positional and is accompanied by nausea, two episodes of vomiting, and a sense that she cannot walk a straight line. She denies headache, tinnitus, hearing loss, chest pain, palpitations, dysarthria, diplopia, facial droop, or limb weakness. She had a similar but milder, self-limited episode three days ago that resolved in 20 minutes. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily (35 pack-years) and drinks alcohol rarely. She works as a librarian. Family history is notable for ischemic stroke in her father at age 62 and atrial fibrillation in her mother. She has never had vertigo of this severity before and is frightened by her inability to stand.
cases.exam: Alert, anxious, and uncomfortable, repeatedly vomiting into a basin. Speech is clear with no dysarthria. There is spontaneous left-beating horizontal nystagmus that does not change direction with gaze. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact; face is symmetric, hearing is grossly normal, and there is no Horner syndrome. Strength is 5/5 throughout with normal sensation. Finger-to-nose testing is mildly dysmetric on the left. She is unable to sit unsupported or walk without falling toward the left, far out of proportion to a benign cause. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside maneuver is most useful for distinguishing a dangerous central cause from benign peripheral vertigo in this patient with acute, continuous vertigo and nystagmus?
options: Romberg test as the first step today
Fukuda stepping test in this clinical context
HINTS examination
Dix-Hallpike maneuver in this clinical context
stages.explanation: In acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo with nystagmus and gait instability), the HINTS exam outperforms early MRI for identifying a central lesion. A reassuring peripheral pattern is an abnormal (corrective saccade) head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, and no skew. A central pattern—normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew deviation—mandates stroke workup. Dix-Hallpike is for positional (BPPV) vertigo, not continuous vertigo, and can be misleading here. Romberg and Fukuda stepping tests are nonspecific for balance and do not localize central versus peripheral disease. The marked truncal ataxia and inability to sit already raise concern for a central process.

stages.label: HINTS EXAM & BEDSIDE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Head impulse test: NORMAL bilaterally (no corrective saccade)
Nystagmus: left-beating, direction-fixed in primary gaze but right-beating on right gaze (direction-changing)
Test of skew: small left hypertropia on alternate cover testing (skew present)
Finger-nose: mild left dysmetria
Gait: cannot stand without falling to the left
Fingerstick glucose: 142 mg/dL

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How should this HINTS examination be interpreted?
options: Consistent with benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
Reassuring peripheral pattern consistent with vestibular neuritis
Nondiagnostic because nausea limits the exam in this setting
Central pattern concerning for posterior circulation stroke
stages.explanation: A 'central' HINTS pattern is defined by any one of: normal/negative head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew deviation. This patient has all three—an INFARCT pattern—strongly predicting a central lesion such as PICA-territory cerebellar infarction. A reassuring peripheral pattern requires an abnormal head impulse (corrective saccade) with unidirectional nystagmus and no skew, which she does not have. The exam is fully interpretable despite nausea. BPPV produces brief, fatigable, positional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike, not continuous direction-changing nystagmus with skew. The central pattern plus disproportionate truncal ataxia demands emergent neuroimaging.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study is most appropriate to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast head CT alone as the primary diagnosis
Transthoracic echocardiogram as the primary diagnosis
Carotid duplex ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging plus CT angiography of head
stages.explanation: MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging is the gold standard for acute cerebellar/brainstem infarction; non-contrast CT misses up to 80% of early posterior fossa ischemia because of beam-hardening artifact and small infarct size. CT angiography of the head and neck identifies the culprit vessel (here likely vertebral or PICA) and screens for dissection. Carotid duplex evaluates the anterior circulation and would not assess the posterior fossa. Echocardiography is part of the later embolic source workup but does not establish the acute diagnosis. Note a normal initial CT cannot exclude posterior circulation stroke and should never be reassuring in this setting.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage; subtle hypodensity in the left cerebellar hemisphere
MRI brain DWI: acute restricted diffusion in the left posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) territory
Fourth ventricle: patent, no mass effect
CT angiography: focal occlusion of the left PICA; vertebral arteries patent without dissection
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is now 3.5 hours from symptom onset with a confirmed left PICA-territory cerebellar infarct and BP 168/92. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate suboccipital decompressive craniectomy as initial therapy
Discharge home with meclizine and outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Start aspirin and discharge with vestibular rehabilitation as initial therapy
Administer IV alteplase if no contraindications, then admit to a stroke unit with serial neuro checks
stages.explanation: She is within the 4.5-hour window with a disabling, confirmed ischemic stroke and no listed contraindication, so IV thrombolysis is indicated; her BP is below the 185/110 threshold. Admission to a monitored stroke unit with serial neurologic checks is essential because cerebellar infarcts can swell over 2–4 days and cause life-threatening fourth-ventricle compression and hydrocephalus. Discharge with meclizine or treating this as peripheral vertigo would be dangerous given the confirmed infarct. Aspirin alone forgoes a time-sensitive reperfusion opportunity. Decompressive craniectomy is reserved for malignant posterior fossa edema with brainstem compression, which is not present now but must be watched for.

cases.diagnosis: Left posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) territory cerebellar infarction presenting as acute vestibular syndrome
pearls: Disproportionate truncal ataxia (inability to sit or stand) with acute vertigo is a red flag for cerebellar stroke, not peripheral vertigo.
A 'central' HINTS pattern—normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew deviation—outperforms early MRI for detecting posterior circulation stroke.
Non-contrast CT misses most acute cerebellar infarcts; MRI-DWI is required to confirm and CTA to find the culprit vessel.
Admit cerebellar infarcts for serial neuro checks because delayed posterior fossa swelling can compress the fourth ventricle and brainstem.

cases.title: A Drowsy Patient After a Cerebellar Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive drowsiness and worsening headache two days after admission for a cerebellar stroke.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man was admitted to the neuro-ICU 48 hours ago after a large right cerebellar infarction confirmed on MRI. He initially presented with vertigo, vomiting, and gait ataxia and was started on aspirin and high-intensity statin. Over the past 6 hours nursing staff note he has become progressively more difficult to arouse, with a new occipital headache and repeated vomiting. Past medical history includes poorly controlled hypertension and a 30-pack-year smoking history. He takes amlodipine 10 mg daily and now aspirin 81 mg daily; he was not a thrombolysis or thrombectomy candidate on arrival due to a late presentation window. He has no known allergies. He drinks alcohol socially and works as a long-haul truck driver. Family history includes hypertension and early coronary disease. His wife at bedside reports he was conversational and joking this morning but is now barely opening his eyes and seems confused when he does respond. He has not had a fever.
cases.exam: Somnolent, opens eyes only to loud voice and localizes to pain (GCS 11: E2 V4 M5). Pupils are 3 mm and reactive. There is new right-sided limb dysmetria and persistent gaze-evoked nystagmus. He has a right facial weakness sparing the forehead is absent; rather facial sensation and movement are symmetric. There is no neck stiffness. Plantar responses are now extensor bilaterally. Respirations are slow and slightly irregular. The combination of declining consciousness, bradycardia, and hypertension is concerning for rising intracranial pressure with posterior fossa mass effect.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for this patient's acute deterioration on day 2 after a large cerebellar infarct?
options: Aspirin-induced metabolic encephalopathy as the first step today
Recurrent embolic stroke in the anterior circulation as the first step today
Hemorrhagic transformation causing seizure as the first step today
Cytotoxic edema of the infarct producing posterior fossa mass effect with fourth-ventricle compression
stages.explanation: Cerebellar infarcts swell maximally between days 2 and 4 as cytotoxic edema peaks. In the rigid posterior fossa, swelling compresses the fourth ventricle, causing obstructive hydrocephalus and direct brainstem compression—classically producing declining consciousness, new headache, vomiting, and the Cushing response (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) seen here.
