stages.explanation: A top-of-basilar embolus in a patient with a family history of AF and vascular risk factors raises strong suspicion for a cardioembolic source; documenting paroxysmal AF would shift secondary prevention from antiplatelet to anticoagulation, the single change with the largest impact. LDL and A1c optimization matter for global risk but do not alter the antithrombotic decision. Carotid duplex evaluates the anterior circulation and is essentially irrelevant to a posterior-circulation embolic stroke. Prolonged monitoring (e.g., extended Holter or implantable loop recorder) is therefore the highest-yield investigation for guiding therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Artery of Percheron infarct (bilateral paramedian thalamic infarction from top-of-basilar embolism)
pearls: Acute coma + vertical gaze palsy + bilateral medial thalamic signal = think artery of Percheron occlusion.
A single perforator variant off one P1 segment supplies both paramedian thalami, so one occlusion infarcts both sides.
It is frequently embolic and tied to top-of-basilar disease, so pursue cardioembolic workup and thrombectomy evaluation.
Early CT can look near-normal; the hyperdense basilar tip and subtle medial thalamic hypodensity are key clues.

cases.title: Droopy Eyelid and a Weak Opposite Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden double vision, a drooping eyelid, and weakness of the left arm and leg.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to the ED 70 minutes after abrupt onset of double vision, a drooping right eyelid, and clumsy weakness of his left arm and leg while watching television. His wife noted his right eye 'turned out.' There was no headache, loss of consciousness, or speech difficulty. PMH includes long-standing hypertension (chlorthalidone 25 mg daily, losartan 100 mg daily), type 2 diabetes (insulin glargine 30 units nightly), and hyperlipidemia (rosuvastatin 20 mg daily). He had a TIA 3 years ago and takes aspirin 81 mg daily. NKDA. He is an active smoker (40 pack-years) and drinks 2 beers most evenings. He is a retired electrician. Family history includes stroke in his mother at age 70. He denies recent trauma, neck manipulation, or fever. Last meal was 2 hours ago. He uses no anticoagulation. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no vertigo, dysphagia, or hearing change.
cases.exam: Alert and fully oriented with fluent speech. Right eye is deviated 'down and out' with complete ptosis; the right pupil is 5 mm and poorly reactive. The left eye moves fully. There is binocular diplopia in most directions of gaze on the right. Left lower facial droop is present with preserved forehead. Left arm and leg show 3/5 weakness with upgoing left plantar response; right side is normal. Sensation is intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. No dysarthria, no neglect, no visual field cut. Gait not tested due to weakness. No nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The combination of a right 'down-and-out' eye with ptosis and a dilated pupil, plus left hemiparesis, localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Left internal capsule as the immediate next step
Right lateral medulla as the immediate next step
Right cavernous sinus as the immediate next step
Right ventral midbrain
stages.explanation: The crossed picture of an ipsilateral CN III palsy (down-and-out eye, ptosis, mydriasis) with contralateral hemiparesis localizes to the ventral midbrain, where the CN III fascicle exits adjacent to the corticospinal fibers in the cerebral peduncle—Weber syndrome. A cavernous sinus lesion would give a CN III palsy but no contralateral hemiparesis and often involves CN IV, VI, and V1/V2. A left internal capsule lesion causes contralateral (right) hemiparesis without an ipsilateral III palsy. The lateral medulla (Wallenberg) produces crossed sensory loss, Horner syndrome, ataxia, and dysphagia—not CN III palsy with hemiparesis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step in the ED?
options: Non-contrast head CT followed by CT angiography of the head
Tensilon (edrophonium) test for ocular myasthenia
Outpatient MRI brain within 48 hours as the first step today
Carotid duplex ultrasound at the bedside in this clinical context
stages.explanation: This is a hyperacute brainstem stroke syndrome, so emergent non-contrast CT (to exclude hemorrhage) plus CTA (to assess the basilar and posterior cerebral/perforator vessels) is the correct first step and preserves reperfusion options. Carotid duplex evaluates the anterior circulation and is irrelevant to a midbrain stroke. A Tensilon test addresses myasthenia gravis, which spares the pupil and never causes contralateral hemiparesis, so it does not fit. Deferring to an outpatient MRI abandons the acute treatment window for a disabling stroke and is inappropriate.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: No hemorrhage; no early established hypodensity.
CT angiography: Patent basilar artery; attenuated right P1 perforator origin without large-vessel occlusion.
Glucose: 156 mg/dL
INR: 1.0
Platelets: 232 ×10³/µL
MRI (DWI, obtained after thrombolysis decision): Acute restricted diffusion in the right paramedian midbrain involving the cerebral peduncle
NIHSS: 9

stages.label: ACUTE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is 95 minutes from onset, BP 182/98, glucose 156, INR 1.0, platelets normal, and CT shows no hemorrhage. What is the best acute treatment?
options: Withhold thrombolysis because brainstem strokes are too small to treat
Lower BP rapidly to below 140/90 then give thrombolysis
Give therapeutic enoxaparin and admit for observation in this setting
Administer IV thrombolysis after gently lowering BP to below 185/110
stages.explanation: He is within the time window with no contraindication, so IV thrombolysis is indicated; the only barrier is BP, which must be brought below 185/110 (e.g., IV labetalol or nicardipine) before lytic administration. Withholding therapy because the lesion is small is wrong—the deficit is disabling and small-vessel/perforator strokes still benefit. Driving BP below 140/90 is excessive and risks hypoperfusing the ischemic penumbra; the validated pre-lytic threshold is 185/110. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not an acute stroke treatment and increases hemorrhagic transformation risk.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: His deficits stabilize. Given a lacunar/perforator midbrain infarct with extensive small-vessel risk factors, what is the most appropriate long-term antithrombotic and risk-factor plan?
options: No antithrombotic therapy; risk-factor control alone before discharge planning
Warfarin with INR goal 2-3 as the immediate next step
Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus clopidogrel indefinitely
Single antiplatelet therapy with aggressive BP, glucose, lipid control,
stages.explanation: A small-vessel (lacunar) midbrain infarct is best managed with a single antiplatelet agent plus intensive vascular risk-factor modification—BP control, statin to LDL goal, diabetes optimization, and smoking cessation—which drives the majority of recurrence reduction. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding without long-term benefit; short-course DAPT is reserved for minor stroke/high-risk TIA in the first weeks only. Warfarin is for cardioembolic sources, not small-vessel disease. Omitting antithrombotic therapy entirely is substandard for a non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke, where antiplatelet therapy clearly reduces recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Weber syndrome (ventral midbrain infarct: ipsilateral CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis)
pearls: Weber = ipsilateral CN III palsy (down-and-out eye, ptosis, mydriasis) + contralateral hemiparesis from ventral midbrain CN III fascicle and cerebral peduncle.
A 'crossed' brainstem syndrome localizes to the brainstem at the level of the affected cranial nerve.
Pupil-involving CN III palsy demands urgent attention; in stroke it reflects fascicular midbrain involvement, distinct from a compressive aneurysm.
Perforator (lacunar) brainstem strokes are managed with single antiplatelet therapy plus aggressive small-vessel risk-factor control.

cases.title: Crossed Weakness with a Wandering Eye and Facial Droop
cases.chiefComplaint: New right facial weakness, inability to turn the right eye outward, and left-sided body weakness.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man admitted two days ago for hypertensive urgency and chest pain (ruled out for MI) develops acute new neurologic deficits during morning rounds. He reports sudden horizontal double vision worse on right gaze, drooping of the right side of his face, and weakness of the left arm and leg. PMH includes poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily), hyperlipidemia, and obstructive sleep apnea. Home meds include amlodipine 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and atorvastatin 80 mg daily; aspirin 81 mg daily was held the morning of admission for a planned procedure. NKDA. He is a current smoker (30 pack-years) and former heavy drinker. He works as a long-haul truck driver. Family history includes hypertension and stroke. No recent trauma or infection. He has had no headache, vertigo, or hearing loss. Inpatient telemetry has shown sinus rhythm. Glucose this morning was 188 mg/dL.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, fluent. Right eye fails to abduct past midline with horizontal diplopia maximal on right gaze; left eye moves fully. Complete right peripheral-pattern facial weakness involving the forehead, with inability to close the right eye and flattened right nasolabial fold. Hearing grossly intact. Left arm and leg 3/5 with increased tone and an upgoing left toe; right limbs full strength. Sensation intact bilaterally to pinprick. No dysarthria beyond facial weakness, no dysphagia, no tongue deviation, no nystagmus, no limb ataxia. Pupils equal and reactive.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Ipsilateral CN VI palsy and peripheral CN VII palsy with contralateral hemiparesis localizes the lesion to which region?
options: Ventral (basal) pons
Cerebellopontine angle
Dorsal midbrain (tectum)
Medial medulla in this setting
stages.explanation: Millard-Gubler syndrome arises from a ventral (basal) pontine lesion that damages the CN VI fascicle, the CN VII fascicle, and the corticospinal tract before its decussation—yielding ipsilateral lateral rectus palsy, ipsilateral peripheral facial palsy (forehead involved), and contralateral hemiparesis. The dorsal midbrain governs vertical gaze and pupils (Parinaud), not CN VI/VII with hemiparesis. The medial medulla causes contralateral hemiparesis with ipsilateral tongue weakness (CN XII), not CN VI/VII findings. A cerebellopontine angle lesion affects CN V, VII, VIII and causes ataxia, not a crossed hemiparesis with abducens palsy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step for this inpatient with acute focal deficits?
options: Order a routine MRI for the next available slot as the first step today
Start IV antibiotics for presumed Bell palsy with otitis as the first step today
Activate a stroke alert with stat non-contrast head CT
Obtain an urgent ophthalmology consult for isolated sixth-nerve palsy
stages.explanation: Acute, simultaneous multifocal deficits in a vascular-risk patient mandate an in-hospital stroke alert with stat non-contrast CT (to exclude hemorrhage), CTA (to assess the basilar/pontine perforators), and immediate glucose check to exclude a mimic. A routine MRI delays time-critical decisions. Bell palsy is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not explain abducens palsy or contralateral hemiparesis, so antibiotics are inappropriate. The deficit is not an isolated sixth-nerve palsy—the crossed hemiparesis and facial palsy point to a brainstem stroke requiring neurology, not ophthalmology, as the lead service.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Fingerstick glucose: 184 mg/dL
Non-contrast head CT: No hemorrhage; no established infarct.
CT angiography: Patent basilar artery; small pontine perforator (paramedian basilar branch) not well visualized.
MRI brain (DWI): Acute restricted diffusion in the right ventral/paramedian pons
INR 1.0, platelets 245 ×10³/µL
NIHSS: 8

stages.label: ACUTE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Deficits began roughly 40 minutes ago. CT shows no hemorrhage, but aspirin was the only antithrombotic and he is otherwise eligible. What is the best acute treatment?
options: Begin a heparin drip to prevent clot propagation before discharge planning
Withhold thrombolysis because he is already hospitalized in this setting
Defer treatment and repeat imaging in 24 hours as the immediate next step
Administer IV thrombolysis after confirming BP is controlled below 185/110
stages.explanation: An in-hospital stroke within the time window with no hemorrhage and no contraindication should receive IV thrombolysis once BP is below 185/110; prior low-dose aspirin is not a contraindication. Being hospitalized is irrelevant to eligibility—time from onset and contraindications govern the decision. A heparin drip is not an acute reperfusion therapy and raises hemorrhagic transformation risk in acute infarction. Deferring and re-imaging in 24 hours forfeits the treatment window for a disabling brainstem stroke and is inappropriate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After treatment his deficits partially improve. Which complication of his facial palsy most requires proactive prevention before transfer to rehab?
options: Contralateral hearing loss with appropriate follow-up
Hyperacusis requiring ear protection with appropriate follow-up
Exposure keratopathy of the right eye from incomplete lid closure
Loss of taste on the anterior two-thirds of the tongue
stages.explanation: With a complete peripheral facial palsy and inability to close the right eye, the cornea is unprotected, so exposure keratopathy is the immediate, vision-threatening risk and is prevented with lubricating drops, ointment, and a moisture chamber/taping at night. Hyperacusis (stapedius involvement) and taste loss can occur with facial-nerve dysfunction but are not sight-threatening and need no urgent intervention. Contralateral hearing loss is not a consequence of a unilateral pontine facial fascicle lesion. Protecting the eye is the highest-priority bedside measure.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: Telemetry shows persistent sinus rhythm and the infarct is a single pontine perforator lesion. What secondary-prevention regimen is most appropriate?
options: Single antiplatelet therapy plus intensive BP, glucose,
Indefinite aspirin plus clopidogrel dual therapy as the immediate next step
Carotid endarterectomy of the dominant carotid as the immediate next step
Apixaban for presumed occult atrial fibrillation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A paramedian pontine perforator infarct is small-vessel disease; single antiplatelet therapy combined with aggressive risk-factor control (BP, diabetes, statin, smoking cessation) provides the greatest recurrence reduction. With documented sinus rhythm and no cardioembolic source, empiric anticoagulation is not justified. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding without sustained benefit and is reserved as a short course only after minor stroke/high-risk TIA. Carotid endarterectomy treats anterior-circulation carotid stenosis and has no role for a posterior-circulation lacunar stroke.

cases.diagnosis: Millard-Gubler syndrome (ventral pontine infarct: ipsilateral CN VI and VII palsies with contralateral hemiparesis)
pearls: Millard-Gubler = ventral pons lesion: ipsilateral CN VI palsy + ipsilateral peripheral CN VII palsy + contralateral hemiparesis.
Peripheral (forehead-involving) facial weakness plus an abducens palsy localizes the facial lesion to the pontine fascicle, not the cortex.
Always protect the eye in any complete peripheral facial palsy to prevent exposure keratopathy.
Pontine perforator strokes are small-vessel disease: treat with single antiplatelet plus aggressive risk-factor control.

cases.title: Weak in Both Shoulders After the Heart Stopped
cases.chiefComplaint: Bilateral arm weakness noted after resuscitation from cardiac arrest.
cases.history: A 73-year-old man is in the cardiac ICU on hospital day 3 after an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest from ventricular fibrillation. Downtime was approximately 12 minutes with prolonged hypotension during and after resuscitation, including a documented period of systolic BP in the 60s requiring vasopressors. He was successfully revascularized with a stent to the LAD. As sedation is weaned, the team notes he cannot lift either arm against gravity but moves his legs more normally and follows commands. PMH includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease, and chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL). Home meds were aspirin, atorvastatin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. NKDA. Former smoker. Family history of coronary disease. He has bilateral known carotid disease noted on a prior duplex (moderate stenosis). No prior stroke. There is no fever or new sedating medication to explain the deficit. Renal function is at baseline; he is hemodynamically stable now on no pressors.
cases.exam: Awake, follows commands, oriented to self and place. Speech intact. Cranial nerves grossly normal; visual fields full to confrontation. Striking bilateral proximal arm weakness (2/5 deltoids and biceps) with relatively preserved hand grip and 4+/5 strength in both legs—a 'man-in-a-barrel' pattern. Sensation grossly intact. Reflexes symmetric, plantar responses flexor. No aphasia, no neglect, no facial asymmetry. Coordination limited by weakness. Cardiac exam regular; lungs clear. No carotid bruit appreciated today.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The bilateral proximal arm-predominant weakness with relatively spared hands and legs ('man-in-a-barrel') most suggests injury to which region?
options: Bilateral anterior cerebral artery cortical territories
Bilateral medial medulla as the immediate next step
Bilateral anterior-posterior cortical borderzones
Cervical spinal cord central gray in this setting
stages.explanation: Global hypoperfusion damages the watershed (borderzone) regions farthest from the arterial sources. The cortical ACA-MCA borderzone overlies the motor strip representing the proximal arm and shoulder, producing the characteristic bilateral proximal arm weakness with spared face, hands, and legs—'man-in-a-barrel.' Isolated bilateral ACA infarcts cause leg-predominant weakness, the opposite pattern. Bilateral medial medullary infarction is rare and produces quadriparesis with tongue weakness, not selective proximal arm loss. Central cord syndrome causes arm-greater-than-leg weakness but follows trauma, not a hypotensive arrest, and typically includes a sensory dissociation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain (DWI): Symmetric restricted diffusion in bilateral cortical ACA-MCA borderzone regions; additional scattered internal (subcortical) borderzone foci.
MRA head/neck: Bilateral moderate (50-69%) internal carotid stenosis without occlusion.
Troponin trending down post-PCI
Creatinine 1.7 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: Given the imaging, which mechanism best explains the bilateral borderzone infarcts?
options: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as the immediate next step
Cardioembolism showering both hemispheres as the immediate next step
Bilateral carotid dissection as the immediate next step
Systemic hypoperfusion during cardiac arrest superimposed on bilateral carotid stenosis
stages.explanation: Borderzone infarcts in a symmetric, bilateral distribution after a documented hypotensive arrest reflect global hypoperfusion, with fixed bilateral carotid stenosis reducing distal perfusion reserve and exaggerating injury at the watershed. Cardioembolism typically causes territorial (wedge-shaped) cortical infarcts in named-artery distributions, not symmetric borderzone lesions. Bilateral simultaneous carotid dissection is exceedingly rare and was not seen on imaging. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis produces non-arterial-territory infarcts, often hemorrhagic, with venous distribution—inconsistent with symmetric arterial borderzones and the clinical context of arrest-related hypotension.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important immediate management priority to prevent further borderzone injury?
options: Emergent bilateral carotid stenting before stabilization as initial therapy
Maintain adequate mean arterial pressure
Permissive hypotension to reduce reperfusion injury as initial therapy
Immediate full-dose anticoagulation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because the injury is hemodynamic, the priority is optimizing cerebral perfusion: maintain an adequate MAP, ensure euvolemia, avoid further hypotension, and correct contributing anemia—all of which protect the vulnerable watershed. Permissive hypotension is exactly wrong and would extend the infarcts. Emergent bilateral carotid revascularization in an unstable post-arrest patient carries high risk and is not the acute hemodynamic fix; revascularization decisions come after stabilization and assessment of recovery. Full-dose anticoagulation does not address a perfusion-failure mechanism and adds hemorrhage risk, especially after recent PCI.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: Once stable, how should his bilateral moderate carotid stenosis be addressed for long-term prevention?
options: Stop all antiplatelet therapy to avoid bleeding as the immediate next step
Anticoagulate indefinitely with a direct oral anticoagulant as the immediate next step
Intensive medical therapy with antiplatelet
Bilateral carotid endarterectomy regardless of stenosis severity as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: His infarcts were hemodynamic (global hypoperfusion), not embolic from a tight, symptomatic plaque, so the moderate (50-69%) bilateral stenoses are best managed as asymptomatic disease with intensive medical therapy: antiplatelet agent, high-intensity statin, BP control, and smoking cessation. Routine bilateral endarterectomy for moderate stenosis is not indicated and carries operative risk. Stopping antiplatelet therapy raises ischemic risk in a patient with coronary and carotid disease. Anticoagulation is for cardioembolic sources, not atherosclerotic carotid stenosis or watershed hypoperfusion, and would add unnecessary bleeding risk.

cases.diagnosis: Watershed (borderzone) infarction from global hypoperfusion after cardiac arrest
pearls: Bilateral proximal arm-predominant weakness with spared hands/legs ('man-in-a-barrel') signals cortical ACA-MCA borderzone ischemia.
Watershed infarcts come from global hypoperfusion; fixed proximal stenosis worsens distal perfusion reserve.
Management is hemodynamic: maintain MAP and euvolemia, avoid hypotension, and correct anemia.
Moderate carotid stenosis causing hemodynamic (not embolic) infarcts is generally treated medically, not surgically.

cases.title: Sudden Right-Sided Weakness and an Irregular Pulse
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden inability to move the right arm and slurred speech.
cases.history: A 78-year-old woman is brought to the ED by ambulance after her daughter found her with a facial droop, garbled speech, and a limp right arm that began suddenly 50 minutes ago during breakfast. The deficit was maximal at onset and has not progressed. PMH includes hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily), and osteoarthritis. She is not on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet. She has never been told she has an irregular heartbeat. NKDA. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks rarely, and lives independently. Family history is notable for stroke in a sister. She denies recent illness, headache, chest pain, palpitations (in retrospect she had felt 'fluttering' at times), trauma, or recent surgery. Her last normal was clearly 50 minutes ago. She takes no medications that affect coagulation and has had no recent bleeding. Functional baseline is fully independent.
cases.exam: Awake, follows axial commands but has expressive aphasia with effortful, nonfluent speech. Right facial droop sparing the forehead. Right arm 1/5, right leg 3/5 weakness; left side normal. Right-sided sensory inattention. Eyes with leftward gaze preference. Pulse is irregularly irregular. No carotid bruit. Heart sounds irregular without murmur. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema. NIHSS approximately 14. Plantar response upgoing on the right. No nuchal rigidity, no signs of trauma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate diagnostic step?
options: Emergent non-contrast head CT
Transthoracic echocardiogram
Carotid duplex ultrasound
12-lead ECG before any imaging
stages.explanation: In an acute stroke syndrome within the treatment window, the immediate priority is an emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage and enable a thrombolysis decision. An ECG is important and will be done promptly, but it must not delay imaging or the time-critical lytic pathway. Carotid duplex and echocardiography are part of the etiologic workup but are not the immediate step and do not change acute reperfusion decisions. CT first is the standard initial action in suspected acute ischemic stroke.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: No hemorrhage; hyperdense left MCA sign; no large established hypodensity (ASPECTS 9).
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate ~115.
Glucose: 124 mg/dL
INR: 1.0
Platelets: 240 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

stages.label: ACUTE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is 75 minutes from onset, NIHSS 14, BP 158/92, glucose 124, INR 1.0, no hemorrhage on CT. What is the best acute treatment?
options: Rate control of atrial fibrillation first, then reassess for stroke therapy as the immediate next step
IV thrombolysis now, with emergent evaluation for endovascular thrombectomy given the hyperdense MCA sign
Aspirin 325 mg and admit for observation as the immediate next step
Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: She is within the time window with no contraindication, so IV thrombolysis should proceed immediately, and the hyperdense MCA sign with a high NIHSS suggests a large-vessel occlusion warranting emergent CTA and thrombectomy evaluation. Treating the AF rate first wastes the reperfusion window and does not address the brain. Acute therapeutic anticoagulation does not benefit acute ischemic stroke and markedly increases hemorrhagic transformation—especially with a large infarct. Aspirin alone is inadequate when the patient qualifies for thrombolysis and possible thrombectomy.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most likely stroke mechanism in this patient?
options: Cardioembolism from newly identified atrial fibrillation
Hypoperfusion/watershed injury before discharge planning
Lacunar small-vessel disease as the immediate next step
Large-artery atherosclerosis of the carotid
stages.explanation: A sudden, maximal-at-onset cortical syndrome (aphasia, gaze preference, dense arm weakness, neglect) with a hyperdense MCA and newly documented atrial fibrillation points squarely to cardioembolism. Lacunar strokes are subcortical and lack cortical signs like aphasia and neglect. Large-artery atherosclerosis is possible but less consistent with a clean, abrupt embolic occlusion and no bruit; AF is the obvious source. Hypoperfusion causes borderzone, often bilateral, patterns rather than a single large MCA-territory cortical embolus.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: After her infarct stabilizes, what is the cornerstone of long-term secondary prevention?
options: Carotid endarterectomy as the immediate next step
Lifelong aspirin monotherapy as the immediate next step
Dual antiplatelet therapy indefinitely as the immediate next step
Oral anticoagulation , typically started after an appropriate delay based on infarct size
stages.explanation: Atrial fibrillation-associated cardioembolic stroke is prevented long term with oral anticoagulation; a DOAC is preferred over warfarin for most nonvalvular AF, and timing of initiation is staggered after the acute infarct to balance recurrence against hemorrhagic transformation (larger infarcts wait longer). Aspirin is markedly inferior to anticoagulation for AF and is not the cornerstone. Carotid endarterectomy treats symptomatic carotid stenosis, which is not her mechanism. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy does not match anticoagulation's efficacy in AF and adds bleeding risk.

cases.diagnosis: Cardioembolic stroke from newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation
pearls: Sudden maximal cortical deficit (aphasia, gaze deviation, neglect) plus an irregularly irregular pulse screams cardioembolic stroke from AF.
Get the non-contrast head CT first; do the ECG promptly but never let it delay thrombolysis.
A hyperdense MCA sign with high NIHSS flags a large-vessel occlusion—evaluate for thrombectomy.
AF stroke is prevented with anticoagulation (DOAC preferred), started after a delay scaled to infarct size.

cases.title: A Stroke With No Obvious Cause
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up after a recent stroke whose cause was never identified.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman presents to the stroke clinic three weeks after an admission for sudden left-hand clumsiness and a left facial droop that largely resolved over days. Inpatient MRI confirmed a small acute cortical infarct in the right MCA territory. During that admission, non-contrast CT, CTA head/neck, transthoracic echocardiogram, lipid panel, hemoglobin A1c, and 48 hours of inpatient telemetry were all unrevealing; she was discharged on aspirin and a statin. PMH includes well-controlled hypertension (losartan 50 mg daily) and migraine without aura. She takes aspirin 81 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. NKDA. She is a never-smoker, drinks one glass of wine weekly, and exercises regularly. Family history is unremarkable for early stroke or clotting disorders. She has no palpitations, no prior DVT/PE, and no miscarriages. She is perimenopausal and not on hormonal therapy. Her residual deficit is minimal. She asks why no cause was found and what should be done next.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, fully oriented. Cranial nerves intact; subtle residual flattening of the left nasolabial fold only with effort. Motor strength 5/5 throughout except 4+/5 fine finger movements of the left hand. Sensation intact; no neglect or visual field deficit. Coordination intact. Gait normal. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur; no carotid bruit. No peripheral edema, no livedo, no rash. Remainder of the general exam is normal.

stages.label: DEFINITION
stages.question: Her workup meets criteria for embolic stroke of undetermined source (ESUS). Which feature is required for that designation?
options: Confirmed atrial fibrillation on monitoring as the immediate next step
A documented lacunar infarct under 1.5 cm as the immediate next step
A nonlacunar infarct with no major cardioembolic source
An ipsilateral 70% carotid stenosis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: ESUS is defined as a nonlacunar (presumed embolic) brain infarct without a clearly identified high-risk cardioembolic source and without significant (≥50%) atherosclerotic stenosis in the relevant artery, after a standard workup—exactly her situation. A lacunar infarct is by definition small-vessel disease, which excludes ESUS. Confirmed atrial fibrillation would make the stroke cardioembolic, not undetermined. A 70% ipsilateral carotid stenosis would attribute the stroke to large-artery atherosclerosis, again removing it from the ESUS category.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Inpatient telemetry was unrevealing. What is the highest-yield next investigation for an occult cause in ESUS?
options: Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram as the immediate next step
Prolonged ambulatory cardiac monitoring for occult paroxysmal AF
Empiric long-term anticoagulation without further testing as the immediate next step
CT coronary angiography as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Brief inpatient telemetry misses many cases of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation; prolonged monitoring—especially an implantable loop recorder—substantially increases AF detection in cryptogenic/ESUS stroke (CRYSTAL-AF, EMBRACE), and finding AF changes therapy to anticoagulation. A repeat TTE rarely adds yield after a normal study unless a specific question arises (a TEE or bubble study might, but is not the single highest-yield AF investigation). CT coronary angiography assesses coronary disease, not a stroke source. Empiric anticoagulation without a target is not supported—trials (NAVIGATE-ESUS, RE-SPECT ESUS) failed to show benefit of routine anticoagulation over aspirin in unselected ESUS.

stages.label: MONITORING RESULTS
stages.content:
Implantable loop recorder, day 62: Two episodes of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, longest 47 minutes, rate 140s.
Repeat basic labs: TSH 2.1 µIU/mL, normal CBC and CMP.
CHA2DS2-VASc reviewed: age 54, female, hypertension, prior stroke.
No bleeding history; estimated CrCl >60 mL/min.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The loop recorder captured paroxysmal AF. How should her antithrombotic therapy change?
options: Start warfarin only if she develops symptoms as initial therapy
Continue aspirin alone since the episodes were brief as initial therapy
Transition from aspirin to oral anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant
Add clopidogrel for dual antiplatelet therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Documented atrial fibrillation in a patient with a prior stroke (high CHA2DS2-VASc, including the prior event itself) is an indication for oral anticoagulation; a DOAC is preferred over warfarin for nonvalvular AF and is superior to aspirin for cardioembolic prevention. Continuing aspirin alone underprotects her—even brief AF episodes after a stroke carry meaningful recurrence risk. Dual antiplatelet therapy does not match anticoagulation in AF and adds bleeding risk. Waiting for symptoms is unsafe because AF-related strokes often occur without warning palpitations.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks why she was not simply anticoagulated empirically right after her stroke. What is the best evidence-based explanation?
options: Empiric anticoagulation is always preferred in ESUS as the immediate next step
Anticoagulation is unsafe in anyone over 50 as the immediate next step
Aspirin and anticoagulation are equally effective once AF is confirmed as the immediate next step
Randomized trials in unselected ESUS did not show that routine anticoagulation beats aspirin, so therapy is targeted once a specific source like AF is confirmed
stages.explanation: Large randomized trials (NAVIGATE-ESUS with rivaroxaban, RE-SPECT ESUS with dabigatran) failed to show that empiric anticoagulation is superior to aspirin in unselected ESUS, while increasing bleeding—so the strategy is to keep antiplatelet therapy and pursue prolonged monitoring, then anticoagulate when a true cardioembolic source such as AF is documented. Age over 50 is not a contraindication to anticoagulation. Once AF is confirmed, anticoagulation is clearly superior to aspirin, so they are not equivalent. Empiric anticoagulation is not the default in ESUS precisely because the trials were negative.

cases.diagnosis: Embolic stroke of undetermined source (ESUS) with later-detected paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
pearls: ESUS = nonlacunar infarct, no high-risk cardioembolic source, and no ≥50% relevant stenosis after standard workup.
Prolonged monitoring (loop recorder) markedly raises occult AF detection after cryptogenic stroke (CRYSTAL-AF, EMBRACE).
Routine empiric anticoagulation does NOT beat aspirin in unselected ESUS (NAVIGATE-ESUS, RE-SPECT ESUS).
Once AF is documented, switch to oral anticoagulation—a DOAC is preferred for nonvalvular AF.

cases.title: Young Man With a Stroke and a Hole in the Heart
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up after an unexplained stroke in a young, otherwise healthy man.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents to clinic after hospitalization for sudden right-arm numbness and difficulty speaking that resolved within two days; MRI showed a small acute left parietal cortical infarct. The event occurred shortly after a 9-hour flight home, during which he sat with minimal movement. He had a viral illness with calf soreness the prior week. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications; he started aspirin 81 mg at discharge. NKDA. He is a never-smoker, drinks socially, and runs recreationally. Family history is negative for early stroke or known clotting disorders. He has no hypertension, diabetes, or dyslipidemia. Inpatient workup included normal CTA head/neck, normal fasting lipids and A1c, normal transthoracic echocardiogram, and unremarkable 24-hour telemetry. He has had no prior DVT or PE that he knows of, though he recalls one episode of right calf swelling years ago that resolved on its own. He wants to know why a young, fit person would have a stroke and whether the 'hole in the heart' mentioned during admission needs fixing.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, fully oriented. Cranial nerves intact. Motor 5/5 throughout; no drift. Sensation now intact to all modalities; he describes only faint residual right-hand 'tingling' subjectively. No aphasia or neglect on testing. Coordination and gait normal. Cardiac exam regular without murmur; no fixed splitting appreciated. Lungs clear. Calves nontender, no asymmetry or cords today. No skin findings. General exam otherwise normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best confirms a patent foramen ovale and demonstrates right-to-left shunting as the suspected mechanism?
options: Coronary angiography as the primary diagnosis
Agitated-saline contrast echocardiography, ideally transesophageal, with Valsalva
Repeat non-contrast transthoracic echocardiogram as the primary diagnosis
Cardiac stress test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An agitated-saline bubble study with Valsalva demonstrates right-to-left shunting; transesophageal echocardiography best characterizes the PFO and any atrial septal aneurysm and is the diagnostic gold standard. Coronary angiography evaluates coronary arteries, not an interatrial shunt. A stress test assesses ischemia, not a PFO. A repeat plain (non-contrast) TTE without agitated saline frequently misses a PFO because the shunt is only revealed when contrast crosses during a provocative maneuver.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
Transesophageal echo with agitated saline: Patent foramen ovale with large right-to-left shunt on Valsalva; small atrial septal aneurysm present.
Lower-extremity venous duplex: Chronic-appearing changes in the right calf veins, no acute clot.
Hypercoagulable panel: Pending.
RoPE score calculation reviewed with patient.
Age 38, no hypertension, no diabetes, no smoking, cortical infarct on imaging.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: The RoPE score is used in this setting to do what?
options: Quantify carotid stenosis severity as the immediate next step
Determine the size of the PFO requiring surgery as the immediate next step
Estimate the probability that the PFO is causally related to the stroke
Predict the risk of major bleeding on anticoagulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The RoPE score estimates the likelihood that a discovered PFO is causally related (pathogenic) rather than incidental; younger age, absence of vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes, smoking, prior stroke), and a cortical infarct raise the score and the probability of a causal PFO—features this patient has. It does not estimate bleeding risk, which is assessed by other tools. It does not dictate a surgical size threshold; closure decisions weigh shunt features and clinical context. It has nothing to do with carotid stenosis grading.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is 38, has a high RoPE score, a large-shunt PFO, and a cortical (embolic-appearing) infarct without another cause. What is the guideline-supported management?
options: Percutaneous PFO closure combined with antiplatelet therapy
Immediate open surgical PFO repair as initial therapy
Lifelong aspirin alone with no procedure as initial therapy
No therapy because PFOs are universally benign
stages.explanation: For a patient under 60 with a cryptogenic, embolic-appearing stroke and a high-risk PFO (large shunt and/or atrial septal aneurysm) and no other cause, randomized trials (RESPECT extended, CLOSE, REDUCE, DEFENSE-PFO) support percutaneous PFO closure plus antiplatelet therapy to reduce recurrence. Aspirin alone is inferior to closure in these well-selected patients. Open surgical repair is unnecessarily invasive when transcatheter closure is effective. Doing nothing ignores a demonstrated, modifiable mechanism in an appropriately selected young patient with a causal PFO.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He asks whether every person with a PFO and stroke should get it closed. What is the most accurate response?
options: PFO closure replaces the need for any antithrombotic therapy as the immediate next step
Yes, all PFOs should be closed once found as the immediate next step
Closure is only for patients over 60 as the immediate next step
No—closure benefits carefully selected patients ; incidental PFOs in older patients with other stroke causes generally should not be closed
stages.explanation: PFOs are common in the general population, so most are incidental; closure is reserved for carefully selected patients—generally younger than 60 with high-risk PFO anatomy and a truly cryptogenic, embolic-appearing stroke after excluding other causes. Closing every discovered PFO would treat many incidental findings and expose patients to procedural risk without benefit. Closure is not restricted to those over 60; the evidence base is strongest in younger patients. Closure does not eliminate the need for antithrombotic therapy—patients continue antiplatelet treatment afterward.

cases.diagnosis: Patent foramen ovale-associated (paradoxical embolic) cryptogenic stroke
pearls: Suspect paradoxical embolism via PFO in young cryptogenic stroke, especially after prolonged immobility or a Valsalva trigger.
Confirm with agitated-saline bubble study (TEE preferred) showing right-to-left shunt on Valsalva.
RoPE score gauges how likely a PFO is causal vs incidental—higher with youth, few risk factors, and a cortical infarct.
Closure plus antiplatelet beats antiplatelet alone in selected patients <60 with high-risk PFO (RESPECT, CLOSE, REDUCE, DEFENSE-PFO).

cases.title: Fleeting Blindness and a Whoosh in the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: A brief episode of vision loss in one eye and transient right-hand weakness last week.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man comes to clinic after two transient events in the past 8 days. First, he had painless loss of vision in his left eye 'like a curtain coming down' lasting about 10 minutes with full recovery. Three days later he had 5 minutes of right-hand clumsiness and slurred speech that resolved completely. He delayed care thinking it was 'nothing.' PMH includes hypertension (amlodipine 10 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily), type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily), and coronary artery disease with prior PCI. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily. NKDA. He is a 45 pack-year smoker, still smoking, and drinks occasionally. He is a retired machinist. Family history includes stroke and MI. He has had no headache, no fever, no recent trauma, and no palpitations. He has not had any prior carotid imaging. His symptoms have fully resolved and he feels well today, which is why he questions whether evaluation is needed.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, fully oriented. Visual acuity now normal bilaterally; no field cut, no retinal embolus seen on fundoscopy today. Cranial nerves intact. Motor 5/5 throughout with no drift; sensation intact. No aphasia, neglect, or dysarthria currently. Coordination and gait normal. A left carotid bruit is audible. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur. Peripheral pulses intact. No focal residual neurologic deficit. ABCD2 risk features include age, hypertension, diabetes, and prior speech/weakness symptoms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His left-eye amaurosis and right-body TIA point to disease where, and what is the best initial imaging?
options: Right internal carotid artery; obtain right carotid stenting
Posterior circulation; obtain MRI of the brainstem as the first step today
Left internal carotid artery
Cardiac source; obtain coronary angiography as the first step today
stages.explanation: Transient monocular vision loss (amaurosis fugax) in the left eye plus right-sided motor/speech symptoms localizes to the left internal carotid artery, which supplies both the left ophthalmic artery and the left hemisphere; carotid imaging (duplex first, confirmed with CTA or MRA) is the appropriate initial study, and a left carotid bruit supports this. Posterior-circulation imaging does not fit a monocular visual loss plus contralateral hemispheric TIA. The right carotid is the wrong side, and proceeding directly to stenting before imaging is premature. Coronary angiography evaluates the heart, not this carotid-territory ischemia.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Carotid duplex: Left internal carotid artery 80% stenosis with elevated peak systolic velocities; right ICA <50%.
CTA neck: Confirms ~80% left ICA stenosis with ulcerated plaque; intracranial vessels patent.
MRI brain (DWI): Tiny punctate left hemispheric cortical diffusion lesion; no large infarct.
LDL 96 mg/dL; HbA1c 7.4%.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has symptomatic 80% left ICA stenosis with recent TIAs and only a tiny infarct. What is the best management?
options: Carotid revascularization plus intensified medical therapy, performed early
Anticoagulation with warfarin instead of any procedure as initial therapy
Medical therapy alone indefinitely, deferring any procedure as initial therapy
Carotid revascularization delayed by 3 months to let symptoms settle as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For symptomatic high-grade (70-99%) carotid stenosis, carotid endarterectomy plus best medical therapy substantially reduces recurrent stroke, and the benefit is greatest when performed early—ideally within 2 weeks of the index event—since recurrence risk is highest in that window. Medical therapy alone is inferior to CEA for symptomatic high-grade stenosis. Warfarin is not indicated for atherosclerotic carotid disease; antiplatelet therapy plus revascularization is standard. Delaying revascularization 3 months forfeits the early high-risk period when intervention yields the most benefit.

stages.label: PERIPROCEDURAL
stages.question: Which factor would most favor carotid artery stenting over endarterectomy in a symptomatic patient?
options: Surgically inaccessible high cervical lesion or hostile neck
Diabetes mellitus as the immediate next step
Age over 70 with no other complicating features in this setting
Presence of an ulcerated plaque as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Anatomic and surgical-access issues—such as a high cervical lesion above the angle of the mandible, prior neck radiation, prior radical neck surgery, or a tracheostomy—favor stenting because they make open endarterectomy technically difficult. Older age (notably >70) actually favors endarterectomy, as stenting carries higher periprocedural stroke risk in the elderly (CREST). An ulcerated plaque is an indication for revascularization but does not by itself select stenting over surgery. Diabetes alone does not determine the choice between CEA and stenting.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond revascularization, which combination best reduces his future vascular events?
options: High-intensity statin alone with no other measures as the immediate next step
Stop aspirin and rely on the procedure alone as the immediate next step
Antiplatelet therapy, high-intensity statin to a low LDL goal, BP control, diabetes optimization,
Add chronic anticoagulation to aspirin as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Optimal secondary prevention is multifactorial: continue antiplatelet therapy, drive LDL low with a high-intensity statin, control blood pressure, optimize diabetes, and—critically in a current smoker—achieve smoking cessation, the single most impactful modifiable risk factor here. Stopping aspirin after revascularization removes essential antiplatelet protection. Routine chronic anticoagulation added to aspirin increases bleeding without benefit for atherosclerotic carotid disease absent another indication. A statin alone neglects the other major modifiable contributors, especially ongoing tobacco use.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic high-grade (80%) internal carotid artery stenosis presenting with amaurosis fugax and TIA
pearls: Amaurosis fugax plus a contralateral hemispheric TIA localizes to the ipsilateral internal carotid artery.
Symptomatic 70-99% carotid stenosis: revascularize early—ideally within 2 weeks—because recurrence risk peaks then.
Stenting is favored for surgically inaccessible/high lesions or hostile necks; CEA is generally preferred in older patients.
Pair revascularization with antiplatelet, high-intensity statin, BP/glucose control, and smoking cessation.

cases.title: A Neck Bruit Found on a Routine Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymptomatic; a neck bruit was heard during a routine physical.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man presents for routine follow-up. He feels well and denies any vision changes, weakness, numbness, speech difficulty, or dizziness, now or in the past. During the visit his physician auscultated a right-sided neck bruit and ordered carotid imaging. PMH includes hypertension (lisinopril 20 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg daily), and well-controlled type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily). He takes aspirin 81 mg daily for primary prevention. NKDA. He is a former smoker (25 pack-years, quit 8 years ago) and drinks rarely. He walks daily and is functionally independent. Family history includes hypertension and a brother with coronary stents. He has never had a TIA or stroke and has no symptoms referable to the carotid territory. His most recent LDL was at goal. He is anxious about the bruit and asks whether he needs surgery to prevent a stroke.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, fully oriented. Visual fields full; fundoscopy without retinal emboli. Cranial nerves intact. Motor 5/5 throughout; sensation intact; no drift, aphasia, or neglect. Coordination and gait normal. A right carotid bruit is audible; no left bruit. Cardiac rhythm regular without murmur. Peripheral pulses intact. There are no focal neurologic deficits and no history of any transient neurologic symptoms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study to evaluate the bruit?
options: Coronary CT angiography in this clinical context
Catheter (digital subtraction) angiography
Brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging
Carotid duplex ultrasonography
stages.explanation: Carotid duplex ultrasound is the appropriate, noninvasive first-line study to quantify stenosis in a patient with a bruit; it is safe, accurate for screening, and guides further imaging. Catheter angiography is invasive with a small stroke risk and is reserved for cases where noninvasive imaging is discordant or intervention is planned. Brain MRI does not assess the carotid lumen and is not needed in an asymptomatic patient without deficits. Coronary CT angiography evaluates the coronary arteries and is unrelated to characterizing carotid stenosis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Carotid duplex: Right internal carotid artery 70% stenosis (elevated peak systolic and end-diastolic velocities); left ICA <50%.
CTA neck (confirmatory): Right ICA ~70% smooth stenosis, no ulceration; intracranial vessels patent.
Brain MRI: No infarcts.
LDL 62 mg/dL on current statin; HbA1c 6.6%; BP averaging 132/80.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has asymptomatic right ICA stenosis of 70%. What is the most appropriate management for most such patients today?
options: Immediate carotid endarterectomy for all stenosis ≥70% as initial therapy
Anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant as initial therapy
No treatment and routine reassurance without follow-up as initial therapy
Intensive medical therapy with consideration of revascularization only in selected higher-risk patients
stages.explanation: For asymptomatic carotid stenosis, modern intensive medical therapy has markedly lowered stroke risk, so most patients are managed medically (antiplatelet, high-intensity statin to a low LDL, BP and glucose control, smoking avoidance), with revascularization reserved for carefully selected patients (e.g., low surgical risk, longer life expectancy, possibly progressive or higher-risk plaque features). Routine immediate CEA for all ≥70% asymptomatic lesions overtreats many patients given today's medical therapy. Anticoagulation is not indicated for atherosclerotic carotid stenosis. Doing nothing without follow-up is inappropriate; surveillance and risk-factor optimization are essential.

stages.label: PATIENT SELECTION
stages.question: Which factor would most strongly support offering revascularization in an asymptomatic patient like him?
options: Low perioperative risk plus a life expectancy long enough to realize the delayed benefit, ideally at a center with low complication rates
Presence of a loud bruit as the immediate next step
A single normal brain MRI as the immediate next step
His current age of 67 alone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Because the benefit of revascularizing asymptomatic stenosis accrues only over years, the patients most likely to benefit are those with low perioperative risk and sufficient life expectancy, treated where periprocedural stroke/death rates are low—this favorable risk-benefit balance is what justifies intervention. Age alone does not justify it. Bruit loudness correlates poorly with stenosis severity or stroke risk and does not drive the decision. A normal brain MRI does not argue for surgery; if anything, the absence of any infarct underscores the asymptomatic, lower-acuity nature of the disease.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He worries that finding the bruit means he will surely have a stroke. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Surgery is the only way to lower his risk as the immediate next step
A bruit guarantees an imminent stroke as the immediate next step
He should stop his statin to avoid side effects as the immediate next step
With intensive medical therapy, the annual stroke risk from asymptomatic stenosis is low
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic carotid stenosis carries a relatively low annual stroke risk under contemporary intensive medical therapy, and the most important actions are adherence to antiplatelet and statin therapy, blood pressure and glucose control, lifestyle measures, and immediate reporting of any transient neurologic symptoms (which would change management). A bruit does not guarantee a stroke and correlates imperfectly with risk. Surgery is not the only risk-reducing option and is not first-line for most asymptomatic patients. Stopping the statin would raise his vascular risk and is the opposite of appropriate advice.

cases.diagnosis: Asymptomatic internal carotid artery stenosis (70%)
pearls: Evaluate a carotid bruit with duplex ultrasound first; reserve catheter angiography for discordant cases or planned intervention.
Most asymptomatic carotid stenosis is now managed with intensive medical therapy; revascularization is for selected lower-risk, longer-lived patients.
Bruit loudness correlates poorly with stenosis severity and stroke risk.
Counsel patients to report any transient neurologic symptom immediately—conversion to symptomatic disease changes management.

cases.title: Pounding Headache and Seizure After Neck Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe one-sided headache, then a seizure, on the first day after carotid surgery.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man underwent right carotid endarterectomy yesterday for symptomatic 90% right internal carotid artery stenosis after a recent TIA. The operation was uncomplicated and he awoke at his neurologic baseline. Preoperatively his blood pressure was poorly controlled despite multiple agents. Overnight he developed a severe, throbbing right-sided headache that he rated as the worst of his life, followed this morning by a witnessed focal seizure with left arm twitching that generalized briefly. PMH includes long-standing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and the recent TIA. Home meds include amlodipine, chlorthalidone, atorvastatin, metformin, and aspirin; he was restarted on aspirin postoperatively. NKDA. He is a former smoker. Family history is noncontributory. Postoperatively his systolic blood pressures have repeatedly exceeded 190 mmHg. He has no fever and the neck incision looks clean without expanding hematoma. The team is called urgently to evaluate the headache and seizure.
cases.exam: Postictal and drowsy but arousable, oriented after a few minutes. Right-sided throbbing headache. Pupils equal and reactive. Mild left pronator drift and subtle left facial flattening (new from baseline). No expanding neck hematoma; trachea midline; incision intact without active bleeding. No carotid bruit. Speech intact. Reflexes symmetric; left plantar equivocal. No nuchal rigidity. The marked hypertension is persistent despite the postoperative setting.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Severe ipsilateral headache, seizure, and new contralateral signs days after CEA for a high-grade stenosis, with severe hypertension, most likely represent what?
options: Acute postoperative ischemic stroke from carotid re-occlusion
Migraine with aura as the primary diagnosis
Expanding cervical wound hematoma
Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome
stages.explanation: Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome occurs days after revascularization of a high-grade stenosis: chronically hypoperfused vessels with impaired autoregulation are suddenly exposed to normal/high pressure, producing the classic triad of ipsilateral headache, seizures, and focal deficits, often with severe hypertension—exactly this picture. Acute carotid re-occlusion would cause ischemic deficits but not this hyperperfusion triad with a throbbing headache and seizures driven by hypertension. An expanding neck hematoma threatens the airway and is excluded by the clean, non-expanding incision. Migraine does not explain a postoperative seizure with new focal signs and malignant hypertension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management step while imaging is arranged?
options: Emergent reopening of the neck wound as the first step today
Aggressive but controlled blood pressure lowering with an IV titratable agent
Permissive hypertension to maintain perfusion as the first step today
Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed re-occlusion
stages.explanation: Hyperperfusion syndrome is driven by uncontrolled blood pressure on top of impaired autoregulation, so the immediate priority is aggressive, controlled BP reduction with a titratable IV agent (e.g., labetalol or nicardipine) plus treating the seizure—this can prevent progression to edema and hemorrhage. Permissive hypertension is exactly wrong and risks intracerebral hemorrhage. Empiric anticoagulation is inappropriate and dangerous if hemorrhage is developing. Reopening the neck wound is reserved for an expanding hematoma compromising the airway, which is not present here.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: Right hemispheric vasogenic edema with a small acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage in the right parietal lobe; no large established infarct.
CT/Doppler of neck: Patent right carotid endarterectomy site; no re-occlusion.
INR 1.0; platelets 210 ×10³/µL.
BP after initial therapy: 178/96 mmHg, still elevated.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging now shows hyperperfusion-related edema with a small intracerebral hemorrhage and a patent endarterectomy site. What is the most appropriate next step regarding antithrombotics and blood pressure?
options: Continue aspirin and allow permissive hypertension as initial therapy
Give IV thrombolysis for the focal deficit as initial therapy
Hold antiplatelet therapy
Start a heparin infusion to protect the endarterectomy site as initial therapy
stages.explanation: With a hemorrhage now demonstrated, antiplatelet therapy should be held to limit bleeding, and strict blood pressure control to a low target must continue (the core treatment for hyperperfusion), along with ongoing seizure management. Continuing aspirin and permitting hypertension would worsen the bleed. A heparin infusion is contraindicated in the face of active intracerebral hemorrhage and a patent vessel. Thrombolysis is absolutely contraindicated with intracranial hemorrhage and would be catastrophic; the deficit here is from hyperperfusion injury, not an acute thrombotic occlusion.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which preoperative/postoperative measure most reduces the risk of this syndrome after CEA?
options: Meticulous perioperative blood pressure control with close postoperative monitoring, especially after revascularizing very high-grade stenosis
Routine postoperative anticoagulation as the immediate next step
Avoiding all antiplatelet agents before surgery as the immediate next step
Routine postoperative hypertension to maintain flow as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome is most strongly tied to high-grade stenosis and uncontrolled hypertension, so meticulous perioperative blood pressure control and vigilant postoperative monitoring (recognizing headache, seizures, and focal signs early) are the key preventive measures. Routine anticoagulation does not prevent it and adds hemorrhage risk. Deliberately maintaining postoperative hypertension would provoke the syndrome. Withholding preoperative antiplatelet therapy increases perioperative ischemic stroke risk and is not a strategy to prevent hyperperfusion; antiplatelet therapy is generally continued perioperatively for CEA.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome after carotid endarterectomy (with seizure and intracerebral hemorrhage)
pearls: Post-CEA triad of ipsilateral headache, seizures, and focal deficits with severe hypertension = cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome.
It follows revascularization of very high-grade stenosis when impaired autoregulation meets restored/normal pressure.
Treatment is aggressive, controlled BP lowering plus seizure management; permissive hypertension is dangerous.
Once hemorrhage appears, hold antiplatelets/anticoagulants and never give thrombolysis; prevention hinges on perioperative BP control and monitoring.

cases.title: Neck Pain and a Droopy Eyelid After the Chiropractor
cases.chiefComplaint: Left-sided neck pain and a drooping left eyelid that began after a neck adjustment, now with right arm weakness.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents to the ED with 2 days of left anterolateral neck pain and headache that started hours after a high-velocity cervical chiropractic manipulation for chronic neck stiffness. He noticed a mild left eyelid droop and a smaller left pupil the next morning. This morning, while making breakfast, he developed sudden weakness and clumsiness of the right hand and arm and mild slurring of speech that has partially persisted. He denies trauma otherwise, fever, vision loss, vertigo, or prior similar episodes. PMH is notable only for chronic mechanical neck pain; no hypertension, diabetes, or known vascular disease. Medications: occasional ibuprofen 400 mg PRN. No anticoagulants. Allergies: none. Social: works as a graphic designer, never-smoker, drinks socially, no illicit drug use. Family history: a maternal aunt with a 'connective tissue problem'; no early stroke or aneurysm. He is otherwise healthy and physically active, with no recent infection. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, palpitations, recent flights, or limb swelling.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Speech mildly dysarthric, language intact. Left ptosis with miosis (anisocoria greater in dim light), and the left side of the face appears less sweaty; no proptosis. Visual fields full, extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. Right pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the right upper extremity and 5/5 in the legs. Right-sided fine-motor clumsiness. Sensation intact. Reflexes symmetric; right plantar equivocal. Gait cautious but non-ataxic. No neck rigidity. Carotid auscultation unremarkable. Cardiac and respiratory exams normal; regular rhythm, no murmur. NIHSS 3.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical feature most specifically points toward a cervical carotid artery dissection as the cause of this presentation?
options: Mild dysarthria with right arm weakness as the first step today
Painful partial Horner syndrome ipsilateral to neck pain after manipulation
Blood pressure of 146/88 mmHg as the first step today
A family history of a connective tissue problem as the first step today
stages.explanation: A painful partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis) ipsilateral to neck pain following cervical manipulation is the classic triad of internal carotid dissection: the expanding mural hematoma stretches the sympathetic plexus on the carotid wall, producing a postganglionic (third-order) Horner that spares facial sweating over most of the face because sudomotor fibers travel with the external carotid. Right arm weakness and dysarthria (option A) indicate ischemia but are nonspecific. A BP of 146/88 (option C) is mild and nonspecific. A connective-tissue family history (option D) raises suspicion but is not a clinical sign of dissection. The temporal link to manipulation plus the painful Horner is the most specific clue.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next imaging study to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound
Non-contrast CT of the head only
CT angiography of the head
Lumbar puncture in this clinical context
stages.explanation: CT angiography of the head and neck is the preferred first-line test to confirm cervical carotid dissection; it rapidly demonstrates the long tapered ('flame'/'string sign') luminal narrowing, intimal flap, or pseudoaneurysm and simultaneously screens for intracranial occlusion and infarct burden. Non-contrast CT alone (option A) excludes hemorrhage but cannot visualize the dissection. Carotid duplex (option B) often misses high cervical/distal ICA dissections and is operator-dependent. Lumbar puncture (option D) is for subarachnoid hemorrhage, not relevant here. MRI/MRA with fat-saturated sequences is an excellent alternative that shows the crescentic intramural hematoma, but CTA is fastest and most available in the acute ED setting.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage; no established large infarct
CTA head/neck: long, tapered narrowing of the left cervical internal carotid artery beginning ~2 cm above the bifurcation with an intimal flap; distal 'string sign'; small left ICA pseudoaneurysm
MRI brain (DWI): small scattered embolic infarcts in the left MCA borderzone
CBC: WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL, Hgb 14.6 g/dL, Plt 245 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 30 s
Glucose 104 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He presents 30 hours after symptom onset with a confirmed extracranial dissection and small embolic infarcts but no hemorrhage. What is the most appropriate antithrombotic strategy?
options: No antithrombotic therapy; observation only as initial therapy
IV alteplase (tPA) thrombolysis now as initial therapy
Emergent carotid endarterectomy as initial therapy
Antithrombotic therapy with either an antiplatelet agent or anticoagulation
stages.explanation: For extracranial carotid dissection with ischemic symptoms, the goal is preventing thromboembolism from the disrupted intima. The CADISS trial and subsequent data show antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation are comparable in stroke prevention, so either is acceptable; many start aspirin. IV alteplase (option B) is excluded here because he is well outside the 4.5-hour window (30 hours). Emergent endarterectomy (option C) is not standard for spontaneous dissection and is technically difficult at the high cervical/distal ICA; stenting is reserved for refractory cases. No therapy (option D) is inappropriate given active embolic infarction and ongoing stroke risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important to give this patient regarding recovery and recurrence?
options: Most dissections heal over 3-6 months
Antithrombotic therapy can be stopped now that he is stable with appropriate follow-up
He requires immediate surgical repair of the pseudoaneurysm before discharge with appropriate follow-up
He should remain on lifelong warfarin regardless of healing with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Most cervical artery dissections heal spontaneously over 3-6 months. Antithrombotic therapy is typically continued for 3-6 months and then reassessed with repeat vascular imaging (CTA/MRA); if the artery has recanalized or stabilized, therapy can often be transitioned to aspirin or stopped. He should avoid cervical chiropractic manipulation, which precipitated this event. Lifelong warfarin (option A) is not required for a healing dissection. Pseudoaneurysms (option C) are usually managed conservatively and rarely need acute repair. Stopping therapy now (option D) is premature given recent embolic infarcts and an unhealed dissection. Screening for an underlying connective-tissue disorder may be considered given family history.

cases.diagnosis: Cervical (extracranial) internal carotid artery dissection with embolic infarction, precipitated by chiropractic manipulation
pearls: The triad of unilateral neck/face pain, partial Horner syndrome (ptosis + miosis, sweating spared), and cerebral/retinal ischemia strongly suggests internal carotid dissection.
CTA or fat-saturated MRI/MRA of the neck confirms the diagnosis; carotid duplex frequently misses distal/high cervical dissections.
CADISS showed antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy are equivalent for stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection.
Most dissections heal in 3-6 months; reimage before stopping antithrombotics, and counsel against cervical manipulation.

cases.title: Sudden Occipital Headache and the Spinning Room After Yoga
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe back-of-the-head pain, vertigo, and trouble swallowing that began during a yoga session involving deep neck extension.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman presents with 6 hours of severe right occipital and posterior neck pain that began abruptly during a hot-yoga class while holding a deep backbend (wheel pose) with sustained neck hyperextension. Within an hour she developed spinning vertigo, nausea with vomiting, unsteadiness, and a sense that liquids were going down the wrong way. She reports numbness on the right side of her face and a 'patchy' loss of pain sensation on the left side of her body. No limb weakness, no chest pain, no headache like this previously. PMH: migraine without aura since adolescence; no hypertension or diabetes. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill daily; sumatriptan PRN (none taken today). Allergies: none. Social: vegetarian, never-smoker, occasional wine, no drugs; practices yoga several times weekly. Family history: noncontributory, no aneurysms or early stroke. She is a marathon runner with no prior neurologic events. ROS: denies diplopia at rest, denies fever, denies recent trauma other than the yoga posture.
cases.exam: Alert, uncomfortable from vertigo, dysarthric and dysphonic with a hoarse voice. Right Horner (ptosis, miosis). Decreased pinprick on the RIGHT face and the LEFT trunk/limbs (crossed sensory pattern). Diminished gag, right palatal droop with uvular deviation to the left. Right-sided limb and gait ataxia with past-pointing on finger-to-nose; truncal instability. Strength 5/5 in all limbs. Nystagmus on lateral gaze. No facial weakness of the lower face, fields full. Cardiac exam regular without murmur. NIHSS 4, largely from ataxia and dysarthria.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The crossed sensory findings (right face, left body), Horner syndrome, dysphagia/hoarseness, and ipsilateral ataxia localize the lesion to which structure?
options: Right lateral medulla
Left internal capsule in this setting
Cerebellar vermis only in this setting
Right midbrain (Weber syndrome)
stages.explanation: This is the lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome: ipsilateral facial pain/temperature loss (spinal trigeminal nucleus) with contralateral body loss (spinothalamic tract) = the crossed pattern; ipsilateral Horner (descending sympathetics); dysphagia/hoarse voice/decreased gag (nucleus ambiguus); vertigo/nystagmus (vestibular nuclei); and ipsilateral limb/gait ataxia (inferior cerebellar peduncle). Midbrain Weber (option A) causes a CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis, not this. An internal capsule lesion (option B) causes pure contralateral hemiparesis/hemisensory loss without crossed findings or Horner. Isolated vermian cerebellar injury (option D) causes truncal ataxia but not the crossed sensory loss, Horner, or bulbar findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a young woman with occipital pain after neck hyperextension and a lateral medullary syndrome, which is the most likely underlying vascular mechanism, and what is the best confirmatory test?
options: Hypertensive small-vessel lacune; obtain non-contrast CT only as the primary diagnosis
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; obtain CT venography as the primary diagnosis
Vertebral artery dissection causing PICA-territory ischemia
Cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation; obtain a transthoracic echocardiogram first in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Abrupt occipital/posterior neck pain triggered by neck hyperextension (yoga, salon sink, chiropractic) in a young patient with a lateral medullary syndrome is the classic story for vertebral artery dissection, which compromises the intracranial vertebral/PICA territory. CTA head and neck (or fat-saturated MRA showing the crescentic intramural hematoma) confirms the dissection and defines extent. Atrial fibrillation (option A) is unlikely at 35 with sinus rhythm and this trigger; echo is not the first step. A hypertensive lacune (option C) does not fit a young normotensive patient or this trigger, and non-contrast CT poorly images the posterior fossa. CVST (option D) presents with headache, seizures, or venous infarcts, not a crossed brainstem syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage; subtle right lateral medullary hypodensity, posterior fossa partly obscured by beam-hardening
MRI brain DWI: acute infarct in the right lateral medulla and right inferior cerebellum (PICA territory)
CTA head/neck: irregular narrowing and tapering of the right V3-V4 vertebral artery with intimal flap; intramural hematoma on fat-sat MRA
β-hCG: negative
INR 1.0, Plt 260 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.2 g/dL
Glucose 92 mg/dL, Cr 0.8 mg/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next 24 hours, which complication of a PICA-territory cerebellar infarct most threatens her life and warrants close neuro-monitoring?
options: Aspiration pneumonia from dysphagia as the leading concern
Hemorrhagic transformation into the basal ganglia as the leading concern
Posterior fossa swelling causing fourth-ventricle compression, obstructive hydrocephalus,
Hyponatremia from SIADH as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Cerebellar (PICA) infarcts can swell over 2-4 days, and because the posterior fossa is a tight compartment, edema can compress the fourth ventricle (causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus) and the brainstem, leading to rapid deterioration, coma, and death. This is why these patients are admitted for serial neuro checks and may need EVD or suboccipital decompressive craniectomy. Hemorrhagic transformation into the basal ganglia (option A) is not the territory at risk here. SIADH/hyponatremia (option C) and aspiration pneumonia (option D) are real concerns from dysphagia but are not the immediately life-threatening, time-critical complication that mandates posterior fossa surveillance.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her infarct is non-hemorrhagic and she is now 8 hours out with stable, moderate deficits. What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Discharge home on a triptan with neurology follow-up as initial therapy
Immediate stenting of the V4 segment as initial therapy
IV alteplase immediately to reverse the deficit as initial therapy
Start antithrombotic therapy , admit for neuro-monitoring
stages.explanation: For vertebral artery dissection with established non-hemorrhagic infarct, antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation—shown equivalent in CADISS) prevents further thromboembolism. She must be admitted for posterior fossa monitoring given cerebellar swelling risk, kept NPO until a swallow evaluation due to dysphagia/aspiration risk, and reimaged in months to assess healing. IV alteplase (option A) at 8 hours is outside the window and risky with an evolving cerebellar infarct. Acute V4 stenting (option C) is not first-line and carries high periprocedural risk; it is reserved for refractory ischemia or enlarging pseudoaneurysm. Discharge on a triptan (option D) is dangerous—this is a stroke, not a migraine.

cases.diagnosis: Right vertebral artery dissection (V3-V4) causing lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome and PICA-territory cerebellar infarction after neck hyperextension
pearls: Wallenberg syndrome = crossed sensory loss (ipsilateral face, contralateral body), ipsilateral Horner, dysphagia/hoarseness, vertigo, and ipsilateral ataxia—classically from vertebral/PICA territory ischemia.
Vertebral artery dissection should be suspected with occipital/neck pain after neck rotation or hyperextension (yoga, chiropractic, salon sink) in young patients.
Cerebellar infarcts can swell over days; watch for fourth-ventricle compression and obstructive hydrocephalus requiring EVD or decompression.
Antiplatelet and anticoagulation are equivalent for stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection (CADISS); most arteries heal in 3-6 months.

cases.title: Collapsed at Home with a Dense Weak Side and Sky-High Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden inability to move the left arm and leg with slurred speech, found by his wife.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is brought in by EMS after his wife found him slumped at the kitchen table, unable to move his left side, about 45 minutes earlier. He had complained of a headache and was vomiting before he became less responsive. He has a long history of poorly controlled hypertension and admits he 'ran out of his pills' weeks ago.
