PMH: hypertension for 15 years, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, prior tobacco use. Medications (when taking them): amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 40 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He is not on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet agent. Allergies: none. Social: 30-pack-year smoker, quit 2 years ago; drinks beer on weekends; no illicit drugs. Family history: father had a stroke at 70; mother hypertensive. Last known well 45 minutes ago. ROS limited by his decreased responsiveness; per wife no recent trauma, fever, or seizure activity, though he was briefly stiff.
cases.exam: Drowsy, opens eyes to voice, follows simple commands inconsistently. Dysarthric. Right gaze preference (eyes deviated toward the lesion). Left facial droop, left homonymous-type inattention. Dense flaccid left hemiplegia (0/5 arm and leg), left-sided sensory neglect, and an upgoing left plantar response. Right side moves spontaneously and antigravity. Pupils equal and reactive. No neck rigidity. Cardiac exam regular, no murmur; lungs clear. NIHSS approximately 18. No external signs of trauma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with sudden focal deficit, headache, vomiting, and BP 222/126, what is the single most important immediate diagnostic step?
options: Start an insulin drip and recheck a fingerstick
Lumbar puncture to rule out meningitis
Administer IV alteplase based on the clinical picture
Emergent non-contrast CT of the head
stages.explanation: An emergent non-contrast head CT is the critical first step in any acute focal neurologic deficit because it immediately distinguishes ischemic stroke from intracerebral hemorrhage—a distinction that completely changes management. Headache, vomiting, decreased consciousness, and markedly elevated BP raise concern for hemorrhage, and giving alteplase (option B) before excluding bleeding would be catastrophic. An insulin drip (option C) is irrelevant before checking glucose and is not the priority. Lumbar puncture (option D) is contraindicated before imaging in a patient with a focal deficit and possible mass lesion/elevated ICP, and there is nothing to suggest meningitis. Imaging first is the rule.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: hyperdense (acute) hematoma centered in the RIGHT putamen/external capsule, ~3.5 cm, with mild surrounding edema and early effacement of the right lateral ventricle; no intraventricular extension
Fingerstick glucose: 168 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 9.0 ×10³/µL, Hgb 15.1 g/dL, Plt 230 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 29 s
Na 139, K 4.0, Cr 1.2 mg/dL
Troponin: negative
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, LVH by voltage criteria

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT confirms a deep putaminal hemorrhage. What is the most likely underlying cause given his history?
options: Hemorrhagic conversion of an embolic infarct as the primary diagnosis
Chronic hypertensive vasculopathy
Ruptured saccular (berry) aneurysm as the primary diagnosis
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A deep hemorrhage in the putamen/basal ganglia in a patient with long-standing, poorly controlled hypertension is the textbook presentation of hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage from chronic small-vessel damage and rupture of lenticulostriate perforators (Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms). The other deep predilection sites are thalamus, pons, and cerebellum. A ruptured berry aneurysm (option A) typically causes subarachnoid hemorrhage, not a deep intraparenchymal clot. Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (option C) causes lobar (cortical) hemorrhages in older normotensive patients, not deep putaminal bleeds. Hemorrhagic conversion of an embolic infarct (option D) follows a vascular-territory infarct and looks different, and there is no preceding infarct here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His BP is 222/126 with a confirmed acute ICH and INR 1.0. What is the most appropriate acute blood pressure management?
options: Do not lower BP; permissive hypertension to maintain perfusion as initial therapy
Lower systolic BP carefully to a target around 140 mmHg using a titratable IV agent , avoiding precipitous drops
Administer IV alteplase to dissolve the clot as initial therapy
Give a single large IV bolus of labetalol to drop BP below 110 systolic immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In acute spontaneous ICH with markedly elevated BP, guideline-based care is early, smooth lowering of systolic BP to roughly 140 mmHg using a titratable IV infusion such as nicardipine, which is associated with reduced hematoma expansion and is safe when done gradually. Permissive hypertension (option A) risks hematoma growth. An aggressive bolus driving BP below 110 (option C) risks cerebral hypoperfusion and is not the target; the INTERACT2/ATACH-2 data support careful reduction, not overshoot. IV alteplase (option D) is absolutely contraindicated—it would worsen the bleed. He also needs neurosurgical consultation, ICU admission, and reversal of any coagulopathy (none here, INR 1.0).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Besides BP control, which additional intervention is most appropriate at this point?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent stroke with appropriate follow-up
ICU admission with neurosurgical consultation, frequent neuro checks, normoglycemia,
Immediate craniotomy for clot evacuation of this deep putaminal hemorrhage with appropriate follow-up
Prophylactic antiepileptic drugs for all ICH patients with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: He needs ICU-level care with neurosurgical consultation, serial neurologic exams (to detect expansion or herniation), glucose control, and mechanical DVT prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression started early; pharmacologic prophylaxis only after the hematoma is stable). Immediate craniotomy for a deep putaminal bleed (option A) is generally not beneficial (STICH trials); surgery is more often considered for cerebellar hemorrhages or deteriorating supratentorial bleeds, and minimally invasive evacuation is investigational. Therapeutic anticoagulation (option C) is contraindicated in acute hemorrhage. Routine prophylactic antiepileptics (option D) are not recommended—treat seizures if they occur, but do not give prophylaxis to all comers.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive intracerebral (right putaminal/basal ganglia) hemorrhage
pearls: Deep ICH at the putamen, thalamus, pons, or cerebellum in a hypertensive patient implicates rupture of lenticulostriate perforators (Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms).
Always get a non-contrast head CT first—it separates ischemic from hemorrhagic stroke and prevents catastrophic thrombolysis of a bleed.
In acute ICH, lower systolic BP smoothly toward ~140 mmHg with a titratable infusion (nicardipine); avoid precipitous drops.
Surgery rarely helps deep supratectorial bleeds (STICH); start mechanical DVT prophylaxis early and reverse any coagulopathy.

cases.title: Numb Half-Body and Eyes That Stare at the Nose
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden numbness of the entire left side of the body with a strange downward gaze, noted by family.
cases.history: A 69-year-old woman presents with abrupt onset, about 2 hours ago, of dense numbness involving the left face, arm, and leg, accompanied by mild left-sided clumsiness and a headache. Her daughter noticed her eyes 'looking down toward her nose' and that she seemed confused about the left side of space. PMH: hypertension treated for 10 years, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, losartan 50 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily (for primary prevention). No anticoagulants. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: never-smoker, no alcohol, lives with her daughter. Family history: hypertension; no known aneurysms. She admits inconsistent BP control and missed doses. ROS: denies seizure, no recent head trauma, no fever, no vision loss in one eye, no prior similar spells. She is right-handed and was fully independent before this event.
cases.exam: Awake, mildly drowsy, dysarthric. Striking eye findings: both eyes deviated downward and inward (toward the nose), with impaired upgaze and small poorly reactive pupils. Dense left hemisensory loss (face, arm, leg) to all modalities, with left-sided sensory neglect/inattention. Left mild hemiparesis (4/5) that is less prominent than the sensory loss. No aphasia (right-hemisphere lesion). Reflexes symmetric, left plantar upgoing. No nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam regular, no murmur; lungs clear. NIHSS approximately 9.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: A predominant hemisensory loss with downward-and-inward eye deviation (eyes 'pointing at the nose'), impaired upgaze, and small poorly reactive pupils localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Pons (basis pontis) in this setting
Cerebellar hemisphere in this setting
Thalamus
Lobar parietal cortex in this setting
stages.explanation: A thalamic hemorrhage classically produces a dense contralateral hemisensory loss (the thalamus is the major sensory relay) that often exceeds the motor deficit. When a thalamic bleed compresses or extends into the dorsal midbrain/tectal region, it produces the characteristic 'wrong-way' or downward-and-inward gaze—eyes deviated down and medially toward the nose—with impaired upgaze and small, poorly reactive pupils. A parietal cortical lesion (option A) gives cortical sensory loss and neglect but not this eye sign. A cerebellar lesion (option C) causes ataxia, not hemisensory loss. A basis pontis lesion (option D) causes motor deficits, dysarthria, and pinpoint pupils, not a sensory-predominant syndrome with this gaze.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic study?
options: Non-contrast CT of the head
MRI brain with contrast
EEG as the first step today
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the fastest, most appropriate first study; it immediately identifies acute blood and is the gold standard for detecting intracerebral hemorrhage, which is high on the differential given her abrupt sensory-predominant deficit, headache, BP of 198/110, and the dorsal-midbrain gaze sign. MRI with contrast (option A) is more time-consuming, less available emergently, and contrast adds nothing for acute blood (and raises concern with her CKD). Carotid duplex (option C) evaluates extracranial carotid disease, irrelevant to a deep thalamic process. EEG (option D) is for seizure evaluation, not the acute structural question here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: acute hyperdense hematoma in the RIGHT thalamus, ~2.0 cm, with a small amount of intraventricular extension into the third ventricle; mild early dilation of the temporal horns
Fingerstick glucose: 150 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.0 g/dL, Plt 210 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1, aPTT 30 s
Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 1.6 mg/dL (baseline ~1.5), eGFR 38
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The CT shows intraventricular extension with early temporal horn dilation. Which complication should be anticipated and monitored most closely?
options: Vasospasm typical of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage in this presentation
Acute obstructive hydrocephalus from intraventricular blood blocking CSF flow
Hemorrhagic transformation of the contralateral hemisphere
Demyelination of the corpus callosum as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Thalamic hemorrhages often rupture into the adjacent third/lateral ventricles; intraventricular blood can obstruct CSF outflow (especially at the third ventricle/aqueduct or foramina), producing acute obstructive hydrocephalus—suggested here by early temporal horn dilation. This can cause rapid decline in consciousness and may require an external ventricular drain. Vasospasm (option B) is a feature of aneurysmal SAH days later, not deep ICH. Callosal demyelination (option C) is unrelated. Hemorrhagic transformation of the opposite hemisphere (option D) is not a recognized consequence of a thalamic bleed. Serial neuro checks and repeat imaging are essential to catch worsening hydrocephalus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her INR is 1.1 and BP is 198/110. Which management bundle is most appropriate?
options: Start IV heparin to prevent venous thromboembolism as initial therapy
Give IV alteplase since she is within 4.5 hours as initial therapy
Reverse anticoagulation with PCC and give vitamin K as initial therapy
Carefully lower systolic BP toward ~140 mmHg with IV nicardipine, neurosurgery consult for possible EVD, ICU admission,
stages.explanation: The correct bundle is smooth BP reduction toward a systolic of ~140 mmHg with titratable IV nicardipine, urgent neurosurgical consultation given intraventricular extension and developing hydrocephalus (possible EVD), ICU admission, and frequent neurologic monitoring. Reversal with PCC/vitamin K (option A) is unnecessary because her INR is normal (1.1)—she is not anticoagulated. IV heparin (option C) is contraindicated in acute hemorrhage. IV alteplase (option D) is absolutely contraindicated in ICH regardless of time window. Hold her aspirin and address glucose; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis is deferred until the hematoma is stable, using mechanical prophylaxis in the interim.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive thalamic intracerebral hemorrhage with intraventricular extension and early obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Thalamic hemorrhage produces contralateral hemisensory loss often greater than motor loss, plus a dorsal-midbrain gaze sign: eyes deviated down and inward toward the nose with impaired upgaze and small poorly reactive pupils.
Thalamic bleeds readily extend into the third/lateral ventricles—watch for obstructive hydrocephalus requiring an external ventricular drain.
Manage like other deep ICH: non-contrast CT first, smooth BP lowering to ~140 mmHg, ICU care, and reverse coagulopathy only if present.
Aphasia is absent in right (nondominant) thalamic/hemispheric lesions; expect neglect instead.

cases.title: Found Comatose with Pinpoint Pupils and a Soaring Fever
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden collapse into unresponsiveness, found by a coworker who noted snoring respirations.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man is brought to the ED, then admitted to the ICU, after collapsing at work. A coworker reports he grabbed his head, said he felt 'awful,' then slumped over unresponsive within minutes; there was no convulsion but he was rigid and had irregular breathing. He has severe, longstanding hypertension and is notoriously non-adherent. PMH: hypertension (15+ years), chronic kidney disease, prior left ventricular hypertrophy on echo, heavy alcohol use. Medications (poorly adherent): amlodipine 10 mg, chlorthalidone 25 mg, lisinopril 40 mg daily; no antiplatelet or anticoagulant. Allergies: none known. Social: drinks a pint of liquor most days, 40-pack-year smoker, divorced, works construction. Family history: father died of stroke, brother hypertensive. Collateral confirms no trauma and no recent illness; he was complaining of a headache earlier in the day. Last known well roughly 30 minutes before collapse. There is no available history of seizures or prior strokes.
cases.exam: Comatose, GCS 4 (E1 V1 M2), not following commands. Pinpoint pupils (~1 mm) that remain reactive to light when viewed with magnification. Absent horizontal eye movements with loss of reflexive horizontal gaze; ocular bobbing noted. Quadriplegia with extensor (decerebrate) posturing to noxious stimulus, bilaterally upgoing toes. Corneal reflexes diminished; gag impaired. Breathing irregular, requiring intubation for airway protection. Hyperthermia (central). No nuchal rigidity appreciated. Cardiac exam bradycardic, regular. The combination is consistent with a destructive pontine process.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Coma with pinpoint-but-reactive pupils, loss of horizontal eye movements with ocular bobbing, quadriplegia, decerebrate posturing, and central hyperthermia localizes the catastrophe to which structure?
options: Unilateral putamen in this setting
Bilateral frontal lobes
Pons
Cervical spinal cord
stages.explanation: This is a devastating pontine lesion. Pinpoint (~1 mm) but reactive pupils are the hallmark of pontine hemorrhage, caused by interruption of descending sympathetic fibers with preserved parasympathetic (Edinger-Westphal) input. The pons houses horizontal gaze centers (PPRF/abducens), so a destructive lesion abolishes reflexive horizontal eye movements and produces ocular bobbing. Disruption of the corticospinal tracts in the basis pontis causes quadriplegia and decerebrate posturing, and damage to central thermoregulatory pathways causes hyperthermia. Bilateral frontal lesions (option A) do not give pinpoint pupils or this oculomotor picture. A unilateral putaminal bleed (option C) causes a hemisyndrome with a gaze preference, not quadriplegia with pinpoint pupils. Cervical cord injury (option D) spares pupils and consciousness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After securing the airway, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture as the first step today
Empiric IV alteplase for presumed basilar occlusion
Immediate decompressive craniectomy
Emergent non-contrast head CT
stages.explanation: After airway control (he was intubated for GCS 4 and irregular respirations), an emergent non-contrast head CT is essential to define the structural cause—here, to distinguish a pontine hemorrhage from a basilar artery occlusion, since both can produce coma, pinpoint pupils, and quadriplegia but require opposite treatments. Empiric alteplase (option B) would be catastrophic if this is a hemorrhage and must never precede imaging. Lumbar puncture (option C) is dangerous and unhelpful in a comatose patient with a likely posterior fossa mass lesion. Decompressive craniectomy (option D) is not a standard, beneficial intervention for primary pontine hemorrhage and is premature without imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: large acute hemorrhage centered in the pons (~3 cm) with extension into the midbrain and the fourth ventricle; effacement of perimesencephalic cisterns
CT angiography: no aneurysm or vascular malformation; basilar artery patent
Fingerstick glucose: 176 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 12.0 ×10³/µL, Hgb 14.0 g/dL, Plt 200 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 28 s
Na 142, K 4.1, Cr 1.8 mg/dL
ABG (intubated): pH 7.28, pCO2 52, pO2 88
ECG: sinus bradycardia

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT confirms a large primary pontine hemorrhage with fourth-ventricle extension. Given the imaging and exam, what is the most accurate statement about prognosis and management?
options: Immediate full-dose anticoagulation prevents extension of the bleed as the primary diagnosis
Large pontine hemorrhages with coma, fourth-ventricle extension, hyperthermia,
Emergent surgical evacuation of the pontine clot is the standard of care and reliably restores function as the primary diagnosis
Prognosis is excellent and full recovery is expected with BP control alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Large primary pontine hemorrhages presenting with coma, quadriplegia, hyperthermia, fourth-ventricular extension, and impaired brainstem reflexes carry a grim prognosis with very high early mortality. Care is largely supportive: airway protection, careful BP reduction (smooth lowering, avoiding extremes), ICU monitoring, treatment of hyperthermia, EVD if obstructive hydrocephalus develops, and—critically—an early, honest goals-of-care conversation with family. Option 0 is false; outcomes are poor. Surgical evacuation of the pons (option C) is not standard and is generally not beneficial given the eloquent, inaccessible location. Anticoagulation (option D) is absolutely contraindicated and would worsen the hemorrhage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which supportive intervention is most appropriate in the ICU at this time?
options: Start IV alteplase to recanalize presumed thrombus as initial therapy
Smooth, controlled reduction of systolic BP with a titratable IV agent, treat hyperthermia, monitor for hydrocephalus with possible EVD,
Begin therapeutic hypothermia to 32°C for neuroprotection as initial therapy
Aggressively drop systolic BP below 100 mmHg as fast as possible as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Appropriate ICU care is smooth, controlled systolic BP reduction with a titratable infusion (e.g., nicardipine), antipyresis and cooling for central hyperthermia, vigilance for obstructive hydrocephalus from fourth-ventricle blood (EVD if it develops), and supportive neurocritical care, alongside goals-of-care discussion. Crashing the systolic below 100 (option A) risks hypoperfusion and is not the target. Therapeutic hypothermia to 32°C (option C) is not an evidence-based therapy for ICH and carries coagulopathy and infection risks. IV alteplase (option D) is contraindicated—this is a hemorrhage, not an occlusion. The basilar artery was confirmed patent on CTA, so there is no thrombus to treat.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hypertensive pontine hemorrhage with fourth-ventricular extension
pearls: Pinpoint but reactive pupils plus coma and quadriplegia are the classic signature of pontine hemorrhage (interrupted descending sympathetics with intact parasympathetics).
Pontine hemorrhage abolishes horizontal gaze (PPRF/abducens) and may cause ocular bobbing; central hyperthermia signals brainstem thermoregulatory damage.
Always image before treating—pontine hemorrhage and basilar occlusion mimic each other but demand opposite therapies.
Large pontine bleeds with coma and fourth-ventricle extension have very high mortality; care is supportive with early goals-of-care discussion.

cases.title: A Splitting Headache and a Weak Leg in a Healthy-Looking Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe occipital headache with vomiting and inability to walk.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents with the abrupt onset, 90 minutes ago, of a severe occipital headache accompanied by repeated vomiting and dizziness. When he tried to stand he fell to the right and could not walk; he denies any one-sided weakness but feels profoundly off-balance. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily; he takes aspirin 81 mg daily after reading about heart health. No anticoagulants. Allergies: penicillin (hives). Social: former smoker (20 pack-years), retired electrician, drinks 1-2 beers nightly. Family history: brother with hypertension; no aneurysms. He notes his home BP readings have been creeping up. ROS: denies chest pain, no head trauma, no fever, no neck stiffness, no diplopia, no slurred speech initially though he now feels his speech is 'thick.' He was independent and active before today.
cases.exam: Alert but uncomfortable, vomiting. Dysarthric speech. No facial droop, no limb weakness (5/5 throughout), and sensation intact. Marked right-sided limb ataxia with dysmetria on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin; severe truncal ataxia—cannot sit unsupported and cannot stand or walk. Gaze-evoked nystagmus, with an inability to gaze toward the right (ipsilesional gaze palsy). Pupils equal and reactive. No nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam regular without murmur; lungs clear. The picture is dominated by acute cerebellar dysfunction. NIHSS underestimates severity because ataxia/truncal signs are under-weighted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A hypertensive patient with sudden occipital headache, vomiting, dysarthria, and severe truncal/appendicular ataxia but no hemiparesis should immediately raise concern for what, and what is the first test?
options: Vestibular neuritis; start meclizine and discharge
Benign peripheral vertigo; obtain orthostatic vitals
A cerebellar hemorrhage
Migraine with brainstem aura; give a triptan as the first step today
stages.explanation: Sudden occipital headache, vomiting, dysarthria, and prominent truncal and appendicular ataxia (cannot sit/stand) with a gaze palsy in a hypertensive patient is a classic cerebellar lesion until proven otherwise—most urgently a cerebellar hemorrhage—and demands an emergent non-contrast head CT. The danger is posterior fossa mass effect and brainstem compression. Benign peripheral vertigo (option A) does not cause truncal ataxia preventing sitting, dysarthria, or a gaze palsy. A triptan for 'migraine' (option C) would dangerously delay diagnosis. Vestibular neuritis (option D) is a peripheral diagnosis that should never be made when central signs (dysarthria, gaze palsy, truncal ataxia, headache) are present—missing a cerebellar bleed can be fatal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: acute hyperdense hematoma in the RIGHT cerebellar hemisphere measuring 3.4 cm in maximal diameter; effacement of the fourth ventricle with early upward displacement; quadrigeminal cistern partly effaced; early temporal horn dilation suggesting developing hydrocephalus
Fingerstick glucose: 142 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 9.6 ×10³/µL, Hgb 14.8 g/dL, Plt 220 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 29 s
Na 140, K 4.0, Cr 1.1 mg/dL
ECG: sinus rhythm with LVH

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a 3.4 cm right cerebellar hematoma with fourth-ventricle effacement and early hydrocephalus. Based on accepted surgical thresholds, what is the most appropriate management decision?
options: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for suboccipital decompression/clot evacuation because the hematoma is ≥3 cm with brainstem compression
Observation alone; cerebellar hemorrhages never require surgery as the primary diagnosis
IV alteplase to dissolve the clot as the primary diagnosis
Discharge with outpatient MRI in one week as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Cerebellar hemorrhage is the ICH for which surgery is most clearly beneficial. Guidelines recommend urgent surgical evacuation (suboccipital craniectomy) when the hematoma is ≥3 cm in diameter, or when there is brainstem compression and/or obstructive hydrocephalus, or with neurologic deterioration—all present here (3.4 cm, fourth-ventricle effacement, developing hydrocephalus). An external ventricular drain may also be needed for hydrocephalus, but EVD alone without decompression can risk upward herniation. Observation alone (option A) is wrong and dangerous for this size/mass effect. IV alteplase (option C) is contraindicated in hemorrhage. Outpatient management (option D) would be lethal—this is a neurosurgical emergency.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting the OR his consciousness suddenly declines and his right pupil becomes sluggish. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Hypoglycemia as the leading concern
Hemorrhagic transformation of a contralateral infarct as the leading concern
New subarachnoid hemorrhage from an aneurysm as the leading concern
Brainstem compression and/or worsening obstructive hydrocephalus from the expanding posterior fossa hematoma
stages.explanation: Acute deterioration in a cerebellar hemorrhage is most often due to direct brainstem compression and/or rapidly worsening obstructive hydrocephalus as the posterior fossa hematoma (or its surrounding edema) expands in a tight compartment—this can progress to coma and death within minutes to hours and is exactly why urgent decompression is indicated. Hemorrhagic transformation of a contralateral infarct (option A) is unrelated and not suggested. A new aneurysmal SAH (option C) is not the expected course of an established hypertensive cerebellar bleed. Hypoglycemia (option D) is excluded by his glucose of 142. This event mandates immediate neurosurgical intervention and airway protection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging emergent surgery, which medical measure is most appropriate now?
options: Withhold all BP treatment to preserve cerebral perfusion as initial therapy
Smoothly lower systolic BP with a titratable IV agent toward ~140 mmHg, protect the airway,
Start therapeutic heparin to prevent DVT as initial therapy
Give nimodipine to prevent vasospasm as initial therapy
stages.explanation: While mobilizing the neurosurgical team, provide smooth systolic BP reduction toward ~140 mmHg with a titratable IV infusion (nicardipine), protect the airway given declining consciousness (intubate as needed), and elevate the head of bed to aid venous drainage and reduce ICP. Therapeutic heparin (option B) is contraindicated in acute hemorrhage. Nimodipine (option C) is for preventing delayed cerebral ischemia in aneurysmal SAH, not for cerebellar ICH. Withholding BP treatment entirely (option D) permits continued hematoma expansion. These temporizing measures bridge to the definitive treatment, which is surgical decompression.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive cerebellar (right hemispheric) hemorrhage with fourth-ventricle effacement and obstructive hydrocephalus requiring surgical decompression
pearls: Cerebellar hemorrhage = sudden occipital headache, vomiting, vertigo, dysarthria, and truncal/appendicular ataxia without hemiparesis—never dismiss central signs as peripheral vertigo.
Surgical evacuation is indicated for cerebellar hematoma ≥3 cm, or with brainstem compression, hydrocephalus, or deterioration—this is the ICH most amenable to surgery.
Beware EVD alone for hydrocephalus from a cerebellar bleed; it can precipitate upward herniation—decompression is usually needed.
Posterior fossa bleeds can deteriorate abruptly from brainstem compression; protect the airway and lower BP smoothly to ~140 mmHg while arranging surgery.

cases.title: Another Bleed in an Elderly Woman with a History of Spells
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden right-hand weakness and word-finding trouble in a woman with a prior brain bleed.
cases.history: A 79-year-old woman presents with the abrupt onset, 1 hour ago, of right-hand weakness and difficulty getting words out, with a mild headache. Two years ago she had a 'small brain bleed' in a different lobe that resolved with rehab, and she has had several transient episodes of spreading numbness and tingling over the years. PMH: normotensive (BP usually 120s/70s), mild cognitive impairment, osteoporosis, prior left occipital lobar hemorrhage. Medications: donepezil 10 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D, alendronate weekly; she takes NO anticoagulant and only occasional acetaminophen. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives with her daughter, never-smoker, no alcohol. Family history: mother with late-life dementia. She is NOT hypertensive and has never been on blood thinners, which makes the recurrent bleeding notable. ROS: denies trauma, fever, seizure, or visual loss in one eye. She remains alert and conversant.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly anxious. Nonfluent aphasia with effortful speech and intact comprehension. Right facial droop (lower face), right pronator drift with 4/5 right arm strength; right leg full strength. Mild right-hand cortical sensory loss. No neglect. Visual fields appear full. No nuchal rigidity. Gait not tested due to deficit. Cardiac exam regular without murmur; carotids without bruit. The relatively preserved consciousness and lobar localization (left frontal/perisylvian) are notable. NIHSS approximately 6.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic test for this acute focal deficit?
options: CT perfusion only
MRI brain without gradient-echo sequences
Non-contrast head CT
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the immediate first study for any acute focal neurologic deficit because it rapidly separates ischemia from hemorrhage and is the standard for detecting acute blood. Given her recurrent lobar bleeds and normotension, hemorrhage is high on the differential. A standard MRI without gradient-echo/susceptibility sequences (option B) is slower and would miss the key chronic microbleeds that gradient-echo (GRE) or SWI detects; if MRI is done, those sequences are essential. CT perfusion alone (option C) is for ischemic core/penumbra assessment, not the first-line question here. Carotid duplex (option D) evaluates extracranial atherosclerosis and is irrelevant to a lobar hemorrhagic process.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: acute lobar hematoma in the LEFT frontal cortex/subcortical white matter, ~2.2 cm, reaching the cortical surface; encephalomalacia in the left occipital lobe from prior hemorrhage
MRI brain with GRE/SWI: numerous lobar (cortical-subcortical) microhemorrhages and superficial cortical siderosis; relative sparing of deep gray structures
Fingerstick glucose: 110 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.6 g/dL, Plt 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 30 s
Na 139, K 4.1, Cr 0.9 mg/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An elderly normotensive woman with recurrent lobar hemorrhages, numerous cortical microbleeds, and superficial siderosis on GRE/SWI most likely has which underlying disease?
options: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy
Septic embolism from endocarditis
Arteriovenous malformation
Chronic hypertensive vasculopathy
stages.explanation: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) is the classic cause of recurrent lobar (cortical/subcortical) hemorrhages in elderly, often normotensive patients, with characteristic lobar microbleeds and cortical superficial siderosis on gradient-echo/SWI MRI; the modified Boston criteria use exactly these features for diagnosis. Her transient spreading sensory 'spells' likely represent amyloid spells from siderosis. Hypertensive vasculopathy (option A) causes deep (basal ganglia/thalamus/pons/cerebellum) bleeds, not lobar ones, and she is normotensive. Septic embolism (option C) implies endocarditis with fever and a cardiac source—absent here. An AVM (option D) is possible in a single bleed but does not explain diffuse lobar microbleeds and siderosis across both hemispheres.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her INR is normal and she takes no anticoagulant. Which acute and long-term management plan is most appropriate?
options: Smoothly control BP toward ~140 systolic, admit for monitoring, and
Start lifelong warfarin to prevent future strokes as initial therapy
Give IV alteplase since she is within the time window as initial therapy
Begin dual antiplatelet therapy immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acutely, control BP smoothly toward a systolic of ~140 mmHg, admit for neuro monitoring, and counsel about CAA. Long term, the central management issue is that CAA carries a high risk of recurrent lobar hemorrhage, so antithrombotic and especially anticoagulant therapy are generally avoided unless there is a compelling indication; the decision is individualized but leans against blood thinners. Warfarin (option B) and dual antiplatelets (option C) markedly increase rebleeding risk and are contraindicated without a strong indication. IV alteplase (option D) is absolutely contraindicated in hemorrhage. There is no acute reversal agent needed since she is not anticoagulated.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most accurate for her and her daughter?
options: CAA causes recurrent lobar bleeds and is a risk factor for cognitive decline
CAA is curable with surgical resection of the affected vessels with appropriate follow-up
Future bleeding is essentially impossible now that this one is treated with appropriate follow-up
She should start a statin and antiplatelet for stroke prevention like a typical ischemic stroke patient based on current risk
stages.explanation: Appropriate counseling: CAA is a chronic small-vessel disease causing recurrent lobar hemorrhages and is associated with cognitive decline/dementia; good blood pressure control reduces recurrence, and antithrombotics/anticoagulants should generally be avoided when possible because they raise rebleeding risk. Option 0 is false—CAA is a diffuse vasculopathy, not surgically curable. Treating her like an atherosclerotic ischemic-stroke patient with antiplatelet therapy (option C) is exactly wrong and increases hemorrhage risk. Option 3 is false—recurrence risk is substantial in CAA. Genetic forms exist but most CAA is sporadic and age-related.

cases.diagnosis: Lobar intracerebral hemorrhage due to cerebral amyloid angiopathy (recurrent, with cortical microhemorrhages and superficial siderosis)
pearls: CAA causes recurrent LOBAR (cortical/subcortical) hemorrhages in elderly, often normotensive patients—contrast with hypertensive DEEP bleeds.
Gradient-echo/SWI MRI showing numerous lobar microbleeds and cortical superficial siderosis supports CAA (modified Boston criteria); 'amyloid spells' can mimic TIA.
Avoid anticoagulants and, when possible, antiplatelets in CAA because of high recurrent-hemorrhage risk; control blood pressure.
CAA is associated with cognitive decline and is not surgically curable—management is risk-factor control and avoidance of bleeding-promoting drugs.

cases.title: Sudden Hemiparesis in a Man on a Blood Thinner
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden left-sided weakness and confusion in a man taking warfarin for atrial fibrillation.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation is brought in after sudden left arm and leg weakness with confusion that began about 1 hour ago; he also had a headache and one episode of vomiting. His daughter reports his INR checks have been erratic and he recently started an antibiotic (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) for a UTI, which can potentiate warfarin. PMH: atrial fibrillation, hypertension, heart failure with preserved EF, prior TIA. Medications: warfarin 5 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, and the new TMP-SMX. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, retired teacher, rare alcohol, lives with his daughter. Family history: hypertension, CAD. He has no recent trauma. ROS: denies fever now, denies chest pain, no seizure observed. The combination of anticoagulation, a potentiating drug interaction, and acute focal deficit is the crux of the case. Last known well 1 hour ago.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable, oriented to self only, dysarthric. Right gaze preference. Left facial droop, left hemiparesis (2/5 arm, 3/5 leg), left hemisensory loss, and left neglect. Pupils equal and reactive. No nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam irregularly irregular, no murmur; lungs with mild bibasilar crackles. The acute deficit with depressed consciousness and headache in an anticoagulated patient is concerning for hemorrhage. NIHSS approximately 14.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What are the two most important immediate steps in this anticoagulated patient with an acute focal deficit?
options: Start a heparin drip and obtain an echocardiogram
Give vitamin K alone and observe without imaging
Give IV alteplase and check an INR afterward in this clinical context
Obtain an emergent non-contrast head CT
stages.explanation: In an anticoagulated patient with acute focal deficits, the two essential immediate steps are an emergent non-contrast head CT (to detect hemorrhage) and a STAT INR/coagulation panel (to quantify the degree of anticoagulation and guide reversal). These run in parallel. Giving alteplase first (option A) would be catastrophic if there is a bleed and is contraindicated in patients with elevated INR. Starting heparin (option C) is dangerous before excluding hemorrhage. Giving vitamin K without imaging (option D) delays diagnosis—imaging is required to know what you are treating, and vitamin K alone is far too slow for an acute bleed.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: acute hyperdense hematoma in the RIGHT basal ganglia/deep white matter, ~3.8 cm, with a possible fluid-fluid level (suggesting active/ongoing bleeding); mild midline shift 4 mm; surrounding edema
INR 4.5
aPTT 44 s
CBC: WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 195 ×10³/µL
Fingerstick glucose: 138 mg/dL
Na 137, K 4.3, Cr 1.3 mg/dL
Fibrinogen 280 mg/dL
ECG: atrial fibrillation, controlled rate

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has a warfarin-associated ICH with INR 4.5. What is the most appropriate reversal strategy?
options: Fresh frozen plasma as the sole and preferred agent as monotherapy
Vitamin K alone (10 mg IV) and recheck INR in 12 hours
Andexanet alfa as initial therapy
Four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate PLUS IV vitamin K 10 mg
stages.explanation: For warfarin-associated ICH with an elevated INR, the standard is rapid reversal with four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) PLUS IV vitamin K. PCC works within minutes to replace vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) and corrects the INR faster and with less volume than plasma; IV vitamin K is given concurrently because PCC's effect is transient and vitamin K provides durable correction by restoring hepatic factor synthesis. Vitamin K alone (option A) is far too slow (hours) for an expanding bleed. FFP (option C) is inferior to PCC—slower, large volume, risk of overload—and is only used when PCC is unavailable. Andexanet alfa (option D) reverses factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban), not warfarin.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The CT shows a possible fluid-fluid level and 4 mm of midline shift. Which finding most predicts ongoing hematoma expansion and warrants close monitoring with repeat imaging?
options: His controlled heart rate as the leading concern
A normal fibrinogen level as the leading concern
The presence of a fluid-fluid level / active bleeding signs in a coagulopathic patient
The patient's atrial fibrillation as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A fluid-fluid level within the hematoma is a recognized sign of active, ongoing bleeding and, especially in a coagulopathic patient (INR 4.5), predicts hematoma expansion—hence the urgency of reversal and the need for a repeat CT (typically within ~6 hours) and serial neuro checks. (Other expansion markers include the CT-angiographic 'spot sign' and irregular/large baseline hematomas.) Atrial fibrillation (option B) is the reason he was anticoagulated but does not itself predict expansion. A controlled heart rate (option C) is reassuring cardiovascularly but irrelevant to expansion. A normal fibrinogen (option D) is favorable, not a predictor of expansion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After reversal and ICU admission, how should his anticoagulation be handled going forward?
options: Resume warfarin immediately at the same dose once the INR normalizes with appropriate follow-up
Permanently avoid all stroke prevention regardless of risk with appropriate follow-up
Hold anticoagulation acutely
Switch immediately to high-dose dual antiplatelet therapy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After reversal, anticoagulation must be held acutely. Resumption is a nuanced, individualized decision balancing his cardioembolic risk (AF, prior TIA) against the risk of rebleeding; if restarted, it is typically delayed by weeks after the hemorrhage has stabilized, often with input from neurology/cardiology, and left atrial appendage closure is an alternative for those who cannot tolerate anticoagulation. Resuming warfarin immediately (option A) risks re-bleeding into an unstable hematoma. High-dose dual antiplatelet therapy (option C) is not a substitute and carries its own bleeding risk. Permanently abandoning all stroke prevention (option D) ignores his substantial cardioembolic risk—the goal is a measured, later decision.

cases.diagnosis: Warfarin-associated (anticoagulant-associated) intracerebral hemorrhage with INR 4.5, potentiated by a TMP-SMX drug interaction
pearls: In any anticoagulated patient with acute focal deficits, get a non-contrast CT and a STAT INR/coags simultaneously—reversal cannot wait.
Warfarin-associated ICH is reversed with 4-factor PCC PLUS IV vitamin K; PCC is fast and durable correction comes from vitamin K. FFP is second-line.
Drugs like TMP-SMX, metronidazole, fluconazole, and amiodarone potentiate warfarin and can drive supratherapeutic INRs.
A fluid-fluid level (or CTA spot sign) signals active bleeding and hematoma expansion risk; resume anticoagulation only later, weighing rebleed vs. cardioembolic risk.

cases.title: Acute Weakness in a Woman on a Newer Blood Thinner
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden right-sided weakness and speech difficulty in a woman taking apixaban for atrial fibrillation.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman on apixaban for atrial fibrillation presents with sudden right arm and leg weakness and trouble speaking that began roughly 90 minutes ago, with a headache. Her last apixaban dose was about 3 hours ago. PMH: atrial fibrillation, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 3 (which is relevant to apixaban clearance). Medications: apixaban 5 mg twice daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, retired nurse, no alcohol, lives with her husband. Family history: hypertension, stroke in a sibling. No recent trauma. ROS: denies fever, no chest pain, no observed seizure. The key issue is an acute neurologic deficit in a patient anticoagulated with a direct oral factor Xa inhibitor. Last known well 90 minutes ago.
cases.exam: Drowsy, arousable, expressive aphasia with intact comprehension; dysarthric. Left gaze preference. Right facial droop, right hemiparesis (3/5 arm, 4/5 leg), right hemisensory loss. Pupils equal and reactive. No nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam irregularly irregular, no murmur; lungs clear. The depressed consciousness, headache, and aphasia with hemiparesis in an anticoagulated patient point toward a left hemispheric hemorrhage. NIHSS approximately 12.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step, and which lab is most useful to gauge apixaban effect?
options: MRI brain with contrast first; a normal INR rules out a drug effect as the first step today
Non-contrast head CT now
CT angiography only; coagulation testing is unnecessary for DOACs as the first step today
Lumbar puncture; the aPTT reliably quantifies apixaban as the first step today
stages.explanation: Get an emergent non-contrast head CT immediately to detect hemorrhage. For apixaban (a factor Xa inhibitor), standard INR/PT and aPTT are unreliable and may be normal despite a clinically significant drug effect; a calibrated anti-factor Xa assay (apixaban-specific) best quantifies the level, though reversal decisions are usually driven by timing of last dose and the clinical/imaging picture rather than waiting for the assay. MRI with contrast first (option B) is slow and a 'normal INR' does NOT exclude DOAC effect. LP (option C) is unhelpful/dangerous and aPTT does not reliably reflect apixaban. Skipping coagulation assessment entirely (option D) is wrong—knowing the last dose and drug level guides reversal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: acute hyperdense hematoma in the LEFT basal ganglia extending into deep white matter, ~3.0 cm; mild mass effect; no intraventricular extension
INR 1.3, PT 14 s, aPTT 33 s (near-normal despite therapeutic apixaban)
Calibrated anti-Xa (apixaban) level: elevated, consistent with a therapeutic/recent dose
CBC: WBC 9.1 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.9 g/dL, Plt 215 ×10³/µL
Fingerstick glucose: 162 mg/dL
Na 138, K 4.4, Cr 1.5 mg/dL, eGFR 40
ECG: atrial fibrillation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has an apixaban-associated ICH with her last dose ~3 hours ago and an elevated anti-Xa level. What is the most appropriate specific reversal agent?
options: Protamine sulfate as initial therapy
Andexanet alfa as the targeted reversal agent for apixaban/rivaroxaban
Vitamin K plus fresh frozen plasma as initial therapy
Idarucizumab as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Andexanet alfa is the targeted reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban): it is a recombinant, catalytically inactive factor Xa decoy that sequesters the drug, and it is indicated for life-threatening bleeding such as ICH, particularly when the last dose was recent (within ~18 hours) and the anti-Xa level is elevated. Where andexanet is unavailable, 4-factor PCC is a reasonable alternative. Idarucizumab (option A) specifically reverses dabigatran (a direct thrombin inhibitor), not apixaban. Protamine (option C) reverses heparin. Vitamin K plus FFP (option D) is the warfarin strategy and is ineffective for direct Xa inhibitors. Activated charcoal can be considered if the last dose was very recent (within ~2-4 hours).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Alongside reversal, what is the appropriate blood pressure target and supportive care?
options: Administer IV alteplase because she is within 4.5 hours as initial therapy
Drive systolic BP below 90 mmHg rapidly as initial therapy
Smoothly lower systolic BP toward ~140 mmHg with a titratable IV agent , admit to ICU/neuro unit, serial neuro checks,
Leave BP untreated to maintain perfusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Beyond reversal, lower systolic BP smoothly toward ~140 mmHg using a titratable IV infusion (nicardipine), admit to an ICU or neuro step-down for serial neurologic monitoring, and repeat the CT to assess for hematoma expansion. Leaving BP untreated (option A) permits expansion. Driving systolic below 90 (option C) causes hypoperfusion and is far below target. IV alteplase (option D) is absolutely contraindicated in hemorrhage and is also contraindicated within 48 hours of a DOAC dose even for ischemic stroke. Mechanical DVT prophylaxis is started early; pharmacologic prophylaxis is deferred until the hematoma is stable.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement about her future anticoagulation is most accurate?
options: Restart apixaban at full dose within 24 hours regardless of stability with appropriate follow-up
She should never be anticoagulated again under any circumstances with appropriate follow-up
Hold anticoagulation acutely
Switch to warfarin immediately as it is safer than DOACs after ICH with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Anticoagulation is held acutely after an anticoagulant-associated ICH. Whether and when to resume is individualized, weighing her cardioembolic risk (AF) against rebleeding risk; if restarted, it is generally delayed (often weeks) after the hematoma stabilizes, and left atrial appendage occlusion is a recognized alternative for patients who cannot safely take anticoagulants. Restarting full-dose apixaban within 24 hours (option A) risks catastrophic rebleeding. Categorically never anticoagulating her again (option C) ignores her real stroke risk and the option of later, careful resumption or LAA closure. Switching to warfarin (option D) is not safer—warfarin-associated ICH risk is at least as high as with DOACs.

cases.diagnosis: Apixaban (DOAC, factor Xa inhibitor)-associated intracerebral hemorrhage
pearls: Standard INR/PT/aPTT are unreliable for factor Xa inhibitors; a calibrated anti-Xa assay best reflects apixaban/rivaroxaban effect, but reversal is driven by timing and clinical picture.
Andexanet alfa is the targeted reversal agent for apixaban/rivaroxaban; idarucizumab reverses dabigatran; 4F-PCC is the alternative when andexanet is unavailable.
Alteplase is contraindicated within 48 hours of a DOAC dose (for ischemic stroke) and always in hemorrhage; lower systolic BP smoothly to ~140 mmHg.
After anticoagulant-associated ICH, hold anticoagulation and decide on delayed, individualized resumption versus left atrial appendage occlusion.

cases.title: The Worst Headache of Her Life
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, explosive headache described as the worst of her life that peaked instantly.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents 3 hours after the abrupt onset of an explosive headache that reached maximal intensity within seconds while she was gardening. She describes it as 'the worst headache of my life,' unlike any prior headache, with associated neck stiffness, photophobia, nausea, and one episode of vomiting. She had a transient loss of consciousness at onset but recovered. PMH: hypertension, migraine (but states this is completely different), former smoker. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; sumatriptan PRN for migraines (none today). Allergies: codeine (nausea). Social: smoked 15 pack-years, quit 3 years ago; social alcohol; works as an accountant. Family history: a sister had a 'brain aneurysm' and her mother had a subarachnoid hemorrhage—a significant family history. ROS: denies focal weakness, denies fever, no recent trauma. She is anxious and holding her head. The thunderclap quality and family history are key.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented but in distress from headache, photophobic. Neck stiffness with discomfort on flexion (meningismus); no focal motor or sensory deficit; speech normal. Cranial nerves intact; fundoscopy shows no papilledema but a small subhyaloid (preretinal) hemorrhage is noted in one eye. No pronator drift, reflexes symmetric, plantars downgoing. Gait deferred. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. The absence of focal deficits with meningismus and a thunderclap onset is characteristic. Hunt-Hess grade is low (alert, only headache/meningismus).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this thunderclap (instantly maximal) headache, what is the most appropriate first diagnostic test?
options: Reassurance and treat as migraine
Non-contrast head CT, performed as early as possible
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
MRI brain with contrast as the first step today
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT performed as early as possible is the first-line test for suspected aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage; its sensitivity is highest in the first 6 hours (approaching ~98-100% with modern scanners) and declines as blood is cleared/degraded over the following days. MRI with contrast (option A) is not the first-line acute test and is slower/less available. Lumbar puncture before imaging (option C) is not the first step—CT comes first; LP is reserved for when CT is negative but suspicion remains. Treating as migraine (option D) is dangerous: a thunderclap headache unlike prior headaches, with meningismus and a strong family history, mandates an SAH workup.

stages.label: INITIAL CT
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT (3 hours after onset): NO definite subarachnoid blood identified; no hydrocephalus, no mass, no midline shift
Fingerstick glucose: 108 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 9.2 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.5 g/dL, Plt 250 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, aPTT 30 s
Na 139, K 4.0, Cr 0.9 mg/dL
ECG: sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST-T changes (neurogenic)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The early CT is negative but clinical suspicion for SAH remains high. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start empiric IV antibiotics for meningitis and admit as the immediate next step
Discharge home with migraine medication as the immediate next step
Repeat the identical non-contrast CT in 24 hours and do nothing else
Lumbar puncture and/or CT angiography to evaluate for aneurysm/SAH
stages.explanation: When the non-contrast CT is negative but suspicion for SAH persists, the next step is further evaluation with lumbar puncture (looking for red blood cells that do not clear across tubes and, importantly, xanthochromia from bilirubin, which takes several hours to develop) and/or CT angiography to detect an aneurysm. Many centers use a CT-then-CTA or CT-then-LP pathway. Discharging on migraine meds (option A) risks missing a sentinel SAH. A repeat identical CT alone (option C) is inadequate and lower-yield than LP/CTA. Empiric antibiotics for meningitis (option D) is not indicated—she is afebrile with a thunderclap onset, and SAH, not infection, is the concern.

stages.label: LP & ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 24 cm H2O; CSF with RBC 7,500/µL in tube 1 and 7,200/µL in tube 4 (no clearance); supernatant XANTHOCHROMIC
CSF: WBC 6/µL, protein 60 mg/dL, glucose 62 mg/dL
CT angiography: 7 mm saccular aneurysm at the anterior communicating artery
Na 138 mEq/L
ECG: ongoing neurogenic ST-T changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Aneurysmal SAH from a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm is confirmed. Which management plan is most appropriate?
options: Start full-dose anticoagulation to prevent stroke as initial therapy
Admit to a neuro-ICU
Give IV alteplase to lyse the aneurysmal clot as initial therapy
Outpatient neurology follow-up with oral analgesics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Confirmed aneurysmal SAH requires admission to a neuro-ICU and early securing of the aneurysm—by endovascular coiling or surgical clipping—to prevent rebleeding, which carries high mortality. Oral nimodipine is given to all aneurysmal SAH patients to reduce delayed cerebral ischemia (it improves neurologic outcomes), and BP and pain are controlled to limit rebleeding risk before the aneurysm is secured. Outpatient management (option A) would be fatal. Anticoagulation (option C) is contraindicated and would worsen bleeding. IV alteplase (option D) is absolutely contraindicated in hemorrhage. Monitor for hydrocephalus, vasospasm (days 4-14), seizures, and hyponatremia (cerebral salt wasting/SIADH).

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm (CT-negative, LP- and CTA-confirmed)
pearls: A thunderclap headache—maximal within seconds and 'the worst of my life'—is SAH until proven otherwise; non-contrast CT is most sensitive within the first 6 hours.
If early CT is negative but suspicion persists, pursue LP (xanthochromia, non-clearing RBCs across tubes) and/or CTA to find the aneurysm.
Secure the ruptured aneurysm early by coiling or clipping to prevent rebleeding; give nimodipine to reduce delayed cerebral ischemia.
Watch for SAH complications: rebleeding, hydrocephalus, vasospasm/delayed cerebral ischemia (days 4-14), seizures, and hyponatremia.

cases.title: Day 7 Confusion and Weakness After a Ruptured Aneurysm
cases.chiefComplaint: New confusion and right-sided weakness on post-bleed day 7 in a man recovering from a ruptured aneurysm.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man was admitted 7 days ago after a sudden 'worst headache of my life' followed by brief loss of consciousness. CT showed diffuse subarachnoid blood (modified Fisher grade 4), and CT angiography revealed a 6 mm anterior communicating artery aneurysm, which was secured with endovascular coiling on day 1. His Hunt-Hess grade was 3 on arrival. He was started on oral nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours and maintained euvolemic with normal blood pressure. He had been neurologically stable, following commands and moving all extremities, until this morning. PMH: hypertension and a 30-pack-year smoking history. Medications: nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h, acetaminophen, levetiracetam, pantoprazole, and subcutaneous heparin for VTE prophylaxis. Allergies: none. Social: active smoker, drinks 6 beers weekly, works in construction. Family history: father died of a 'brain bleed' at 52. Overnight nursing notes describe increasing somnolence; on rounds he is now disoriented to place and date with a new right pronator drift and slurred speech.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable, oriented to self only. Pupils equal and reactive. Mild right facial droop and dysarthria. Right arm and leg with 4/5 strength and a new pronator drift; left side full strength. No neck rigidity beyond baseline. No fever, no new external ventricular drain output change. No papilledema. Sensation grossly intact. Reflexes symmetric, plantar responses flexor. No seizure activity observed. The timing (post-bleed day 7), diffuse high-grade SAH, and new fluctuating focal deficit are the salient features.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing and presentation, what is the most likely cause of his new neurologic deterioration?
options: Acute hydrocephalus from intraventricular blood
Nonconvulsive status epilepticus
Aneurysm rebleeding from coil compaction
Delayed cerebral ischemia from cerebral vasospasm
stages.explanation: Delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI) from vasospasm peaks between days 4 and 14 after aneurysmal SAH, with the highest risk around days 7-10, and presents as a new focal deficit or decline in consciousness, often fluctuating. High-grade Fisher (thick/diffuse blood) is the strongest predictor. Rebleeding is most common in the first 24-72 hours and the aneurysm is already secured, making it less likely now. Acute hydrocephalus typically produces global decline with worsening drowsiness rather than a discrete pronator drift and aphasia, though it remains on the differential. Nonconvulsive status is possible but would not by itself be the leading explanation for a stepwise focal deficit precisely on day 7 in high-grade SAH; vasospasm is the classic answer.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: CT angiography with CT perfusion
Continuous EEG monitoring as the primary diagnosis
MRI brain with contrast as the primary diagnosis
Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: When DCI is suspected, vascular imaging confirms vasospasm. CT angiography demonstrates arterial narrowing and CT perfusion shows regions of reduced flow/prolonged mean transit time; digital subtraction angiography is the gold standard and allows simultaneous endovascular therapy, while transcranial Doppler is used for serial bedside monitoring (elevated mean velocities). Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia confirms prior SAH, which is already diagnosed, and would not address vasospasm. Continuous EEG is useful if seizures are suspected but does not diagnose vasospasm. MRI with contrast is slower, less practical in an unstable ICU patient, and not the first-line test to demonstrate large-vessel spasm acutely.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
Transcranial Doppler: mean MCA velocity 220 cm/s (baseline 90), Lindegaard ratio 4.5
CT angiography: severe narrowing of the left M1 and A1 segments
CT perfusion: prolonged mean transit time in left MCA territory, preserved cerebral blood volume
Noncontrast CT head: no new hemorrhage, stable ventricles, no infarct yet
Serum sodium 137 mEq/L, glucose 142 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His aneurysm is already secured. What is the most appropriate first-line hemodynamic management of symptomatic vasospasm causing DCI?
options: Induced hypertension to augment cerebral perfusion
Prophylactic hypervolemia with large-volume crystalloid boluses
Discontinue nimodipine to avoid hypotension as monotherapy
Aggressive blood pressure lowering with labetalol
stages.explanation: Once the aneurysm is secured, the cornerstone of symptomatic DCI management is induced hypertension to augment cerebral perfusion across spastic vessels; vasopressors are titrated to a higher MAP/SBP target while watching for neurologic improvement. Blood pressure lowering would worsen ischemia and is exactly wrong here. Prophylactic hypervolemia is no longer recommended (triple-H therapy has been abandoned); the goal is euvolemia, not volume overload, which risks pulmonary edema. Nimodipine should be continued throughout the at-risk period because it improves outcomes after aneurysmal SAH; if it causes hypotension, the dose can be split (30 mg q2h) rather than stopped.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite a higher blood pressure target with norepinephrine, his deficits persist. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Start IV heparin infusion for presumed thrombosis as the leading concern
Emergent decompressive hemicraniectomy as the leading concern
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to remove subarachnoid blood as the leading concern
Endovascular therapy with intra-arterial vasodilators and/or balloon angioplasty
stages.explanation: When symptomatic vasospasm is refractory to induced hypertension and medical therapy, endovascular rescue is indicated: intra-arterial vasodilators (e.g., verapamil, nicardipine) and balloon angioplasty for accessible proximal spasm can restore flow. Therapeutic lumbar puncture is not standard treatment for established vasospasm and risks complications. IV heparin is not indicated; the problem is arterial spasm with hypoperfusion, not thrombosis, and anticoagulation would add hemorrhage risk after SAH. Decompressive hemicraniectomy is a salvage procedure for malignant infarction with herniation, not the next step before perfusion has been restored by treating the spasm itself.

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage complicated by delayed cerebral ischemia from cerebral vasospasm
pearls: Vasospasm/DCI after aneurysmal SAH peaks days 4-14 (highest risk ~7-10); thick diffuse blood (high Fisher grade) is the key predictor.
Oral nimodipine improves neurologic outcomes after aneurysmal SAH and should be continued through the at-risk window even though it does not reduce angiographic spasm.
Once the aneurysm is secured, treat symptomatic DCI with induced hypertension and euvolemia; the old 'triple-H' (hypervolemia/hemodilution) approach has been abandoned.
Refractory vasospasm is treated with endovascular therapy: intra-arterial vasodilators and balloon angioplasty.

cases.title: Thunderclap Headache With Rapidly Declining Consciousness
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman with a sudden severe headache becomes progressively more drowsy over several hours.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presented to the ED 4 hours ago after the abrupt onset of an excruciating headache while gardening, described as 'like being hit in the back of the head.' She vomited twice and had transient neck stiffness. She was alert on arrival but has become increasingly drowsy over the last 2 hours. PMH: hypertension, poorly controlled, and migraine without aura. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, occasional sumatriptan. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: former smoker (quit 5 years ago), no alcohol or drug use, works as a teacher. Family history: a sister had a 'brain aneurysm.' On initial assessment she was oriented with a normal motor exam and no focal deficits, but had photophobia and nuchal rigidity. A noncontrast head CT was obtained. Now in the ICU she opens her eyes to voice, is slow to respond, and answers questions with one-word replies. Repeat neuro checks show worsening somnolence and the nurse reports she is harder to arouse with each check.
cases.exam: Drowsy, opens eyes to voice, follows simple commands intermittently (GCS approximately 12, declining). Pupils 3 mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally, no anisocoria yet. Marked nuchal rigidity and photophobia. No focal motor weakness, moves all four extremities to command. Upgaze appears limited. No papilledema seen on limited fundoscopy. Reflexes brisk and symmetric. The combination of thunderclap onset, meningismus, and a deteriorating level of consciousness with bradycardia and hypertension is the key feature.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic test for her thunderclap headache?
options: Noncontrast head CT
Carotid duplex ultrasound
MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
stages.explanation: A noncontrast head CT is the first-line test for thunderclap headache and is highly sensitive for subarachnoid hemorrhage within the first 6 hours of onset. It is fast, widely available, and detects acute blood and hydrocephalus. MRI is more sensitive for subacute blood but is slower and not the initial test in a deteriorating patient. Lumbar puncture is reserved for when CT is negative but suspicion for SAH remains; performing it first risks herniation if there is mass effect or hydrocephalus and delays detection of blood. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial atherosclerosis and has no role in acute thunderclap headache.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast head CT: diffuse subarachnoid hemorrhage in the basal cisterns with blood filling the third and fourth ventricles
Ventricles: dilated temporal horns and ballooned lateral ventricles consistent with acute hydrocephalus
Na 139 mEq/L, glucose 138 mg/dL, platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0, PTT 30 s
GCS now 11 (E3 V3 M5)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which mechanism best explains her worsening level of consciousness?
options: Status epilepticus as the primary diagnosis
Acute obstructive hydrocephalus from intraventricular blood blocking CSF outflow
Communicating hydrocephalus from chronic CSF malabsorption in this clinical context
Diffuse cerebral edema from anoxic injury as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Intraventricular blood, particularly in the third and fourth ventricles and at the aqueduct, obstructs CSF flow and causes acute obstructive hydrocephalus, the most common early cause of declining consciousness after SAH. The CT shows ventricular blood with ballooned ventricles, fitting this mechanism. Communicating hydrocephalus from chronic arachnoid scarring evolves over weeks and would not explain rapid deterioration over hours. Anoxic edema requires a hypoxic insult, which she has not had. Status epilepticus is possible but there is no seizure activity, and the imaging directly demonstrates obstructive hydrocephalus as the explanation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate emergent intervention for her acute obstructive hydrocephalus?
options: High-dose IV mannitol alone as monotherapy
Immediate ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Therapeutic lumbar puncture as monotherapy
Emergent external ventricular drain placement
stages.explanation: Acute obstructive hydrocephalus from SAH with declining consciousness is a neurosurgical emergency treated with an external ventricular drain (EVD), which diverts CSF, relieves intracranial pressure, and allows ICP monitoring and drainage of bloody CSF. Mannitol may transiently lower ICP but does not address the mechanical CSF obstruction and is not definitive. A ventriculoperitoneal shunt is a permanent device inappropriate in the acute, bloody setting because the blood would occlude it; it is considered later if shunt-dependent hydrocephalus develops. Therapeutic lumbar puncture is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a non-communicating system because it risks downward herniation.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After the EVD is placed and ICP is controlled, what is the next priority in her management?
options: Begin induced hypertension immediately
Vascular imaging to identify
Discharge planning with outpatient follow-up
Start therapeutic anticoagulation in this setting
stages.explanation: After ICP is stabilized, the next priority is CT angiography (and often digital subtraction angiography) to identify the ruptured aneurysm and secure it early by clipping or coiling, because rebleeding risk is highest in the first 24-72 hours and carries high mortality. Therapeutic anticoagulation is contraindicated in acute SAH and would precipitate catastrophic rebleeding. Induced hypertension is the treatment for delayed vasospasm later in the course and would dangerously raise rebleeding risk before the aneurysm is secured. Discharge is clearly inappropriate in a critically ill patient with an unsecured ruptured aneurysm and a ventricular drain.

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage complicated by acute obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Noncontrast head CT is most sensitive for SAH within the first 6 hours; if negative with high suspicion, follow with lumbar puncture for xanthochromia.
Intraventricular blood obstructing CSF outflow causes acute hydrocephalus, the most common early cause of decreased consciousness after SAH.
Emergent external ventricular drain placement is the treatment for acute obstructive hydrocephalus; avoid LP when the system is non-communicating.
Secure the ruptured aneurysm early (clip or coil) because rebleeding risk peaks in the first 24-72 hours.

cases.title: An Unexpected Finding on a Headache Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman is referred to discuss a vascular abnormality found incidentally on brain imaging.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman is referred to the cerebrovascular clinic after an MRI/MRA performed for chronic tension-type headaches incidentally revealed an aneurysm. She has no history of subarachnoid hemorrhage, no thunderclap headache, and no focal neurologic symptoms. PMH: hypertension, well controlled, and migraine. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: smokes half a pack per day for 25 years and is trying to quit; drinks socially. Family history is striking: her mother and a maternal aunt both had ruptured brain aneurysms, the mother fatally at age 58. She is anxious and asks whether she needs surgery. She has read about both 'clipping' and 'coiling' online and wants to understand her options. Functionally she is independent, works full time as an accountant, and exercises regularly. She specifically wants to know her risk of rupture and whether she can simply be watched. Review of systems is otherwise negative; no visual changes, no diplopia, no cranial nerve symptoms.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and in no distress. Cranial nerves II-XII intact, including full extraocular movements and normal pupillary responses; no third nerve palsy and no visual field deficit. Motor strength 5/5 throughout, normal tone. Sensation intact. Reflexes normal and symmetric. Gait normal, no ataxia. No bruits on cranial or carotid auscultation. The exam is notable for being entirely normal, consistent with an unruptured, asymptomatic aneurysm.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRA brain: 7 mm saccular aneurysm at the basilar artery apex (posterior circulation)
Aneurysm has a relatively wide neck; no surrounding edema or mass effect
No other aneurysms identified
No evidence of prior hemorrhage or infarct
Remainder of vasculature unremarkable

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which feature of this patient most increases her risk of aneurysm rupture and argues against pure surveillance?
options: The 7 mm size, posterior circulation location, family history,
Her age of 46 as the immediate next step
Her well-controlled hypertension as the immediate next step
Her history of tension headaches as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Rupture risk rises with larger size (≥7 mm), posterior circulation location (basilar apex, posterior communicating), prior SAH, and patient factors like a positive family history and active smoking, as captured by tools such as the PHASES score. This patient has several of these high-risk features, which favors treatment over observation. Controlled hypertension is a modifiable contributor but is not the dominant driver here. Tension headaches are unrelated to rupture risk and are likely incidental. Her age of 46 is not itself a major risk factor; younger age can even argue for treatment because of the cumulative lifetime risk, but it is not the feature elevating rupture risk in this question.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a 7 mm wide-necked basilar apex aneurysm, which treatment consideration is most accurate?
options: Surgical clipping is generally preferred for deep posterior circulation aneurysms as initial therapy
Endovascular coiling is generally favored for posterior circulation aneurysms because surgical access is difficult
She must undergo immediate treatment today to prevent imminent rupture as initial therapy
No treatment is ever indicated for aneurysms under 10 mm as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Posterior circulation aneurysms, especially at the basilar apex, are surgically difficult to reach, so endovascular coiling (often with adjuncts like stent or balloon assistance for wide necks) is generally favored; the wide neck may require these adjunctive techniques. Surgical clipping is more often chosen for accessible anterior circulation aneurysms and certain anatomies. There is no need for emergency same-day treatment of an unruptured, asymptomatic aneurysm; this is a deliberate, shared decision. The claim that aneurysms under 10 mm never warrant treatment is false: a 7 mm posterior circulation aneurysm with high-risk features can reasonably be treated.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: Regardless of whether she chooses treatment or surveillance, which intervention should be recommended to every patient like her?
options: Begin anticoagulation with warfarin in this setting
Strict permanent avoidance of all physical exertion
Smoking cessation and optimization of blood pressure control
Start lifelong aspirin to prevent rupture in this setting
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation and good blood pressure control are universally recommended because smoking and hypertension are the major modifiable risk factors for aneurysm formation, growth, and rupture; counseling on cessation is essential for this active smoker. Aspirin does not prevent rupture and adds bleeding risk if rupture occurs; some data suggest a possible benefit on inflammation but it is not a standard preventive prescription for this purpose. Anticoagulation with warfarin would increase the severity of any hemorrhage and is contraindicated as a preventive measure. Permanent avoidance of all exertion is neither evidence-based nor practical; patients are generally counseled to control blood pressure rather than become sedentary.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: If she ultimately elects imaging surveillance instead of treatment, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Catheter angiography every 3 months indefinitely in this setting
No further imaging is needed once an aneurysm is documented
Repeat noninvasive vascular imaging at intervals to monitor for growth
Annual lumbar puncture to detect early bleeding before discharge planning
stages.explanation: Surveillance of an unruptured aneurysm uses serial noninvasive imaging (MRA or CTA), typically at 6-12 months initially and then periodically, because aneurysm growth on follow-up markedly increases rupture risk and would prompt reconsideration of treatment. Lumbar puncture detects hemorrhage that has already occurred and is not a screening tool for stable aneurysms. Routine catheter angiography every 3 months is invasive, carries procedural stroke risk, and is not used for routine surveillance. Doing no further imaging is unsafe because undetected growth would be missed, defeating the purpose of choosing surveillance.

cases.diagnosis: Incidental unruptured intracranial aneurysm (7 mm basilar apex)
pearls: Rupture risk increases with size (≥7 mm), posterior circulation location, prior SAH, family history, smoking, and hypertension; the PHASES score helps stratify.
Posterior circulation and basilar apex aneurysms are often treated endovascularly because surgical access is difficult; wide necks may need stent/balloon assistance.
Smoking cessation and blood pressure control are recommended for all patients with aneurysms regardless of treatment choice.
If surveillance is chosen, follow with serial MRA/CTA; demonstrated growth substantially raises rupture risk and prompts intervention.

cases.title: Severe Headache and Seizures Two Weeks After Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: A postpartum woman presents with a worsening headache that culminates in a generalized seizure.
