No organisms on special stains; no amyloid on Congo red; no atypical lymphoid population
Findings consistent with primary angiitis of the central nervous system

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms primary CNS vasculitis. What is the most appropriate induction therapy?
options: High-dose corticosteroids combined with cyclophosphamide for induction
Nimodipine plus a short steroid taper as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin as initial therapy
Aspirin monotherapy and risk-factor control as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Biopsy-proven primary CNS vasculitis is treated with aggressive immunosuppression: high-dose corticosteroids combined with a steroid-sparing/induction agent such as cyclophosphamide (rituximab is an alternative), followed by maintenance immunosuppression (e.g., azathioprine or mycophenolate) and a steroid taper. Nimodipine with a short steroid course is appropriate for RCVS, not biopsy-proven vasculitis, and would undertreat. Aspirin and risk-factor control address atherothrombotic stroke, not an inflammatory arteritis. Anticoagulation does not treat vessel-wall inflammation and adds hemorrhage risk. Monitoring includes serial MRI/MRA and clinical response, with infection prophylaxis given the immunosuppression.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which feature in this case best argues against reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome as the explanation for the angiographic beading?
options: The presence of a single thunderclap headache at onset with appropriate follow-up
Progressive encephalopathy with multifocal infarcts of varying ages, abnormal lymphocytic CSF,
Normal blood pressure on presentation with appropriate follow-up
Absence of any vasoactive drug exposure with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: RCVS typically presents with recurrent thunderclap headaches, usually normal or near-normal CSF, reversible vasoconstriction over weeks, and is not associated with transmural vessel-wall inflammation. Here, the subacute progressive encephalopathy, infarcts of varying ages, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with elevated protein, and biopsy showing transmural inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis all argue strongly for true vasculitis rather than vasospasm. A single thunderclap headache could occur in either and is not discriminating. Normal blood pressure and absence of vasoactive drugs do not by themselves exclude RCVS (and many RCVS cases lack triggers). The CSF and biopsy findings are the decisive discriminators.

cases.diagnosis: Primary angiitis of the central nervous system (primary CNS vasculitis)
pearls: Subacute headache with stepwise multifocal strokes and encephalopathy in a young patient without systemic inflammation should raise primary CNS vasculitis.
Angiographic 'beading' is nonspecific (vasculitis, RCVS, atherosclerosis, infection); CSF often shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein in PACNS.
Brain/leptomeningeal biopsy is the gold standard, confirming transmural inflammation and excluding mimics like infection, lymphoma, and amyloid-beta-related angiitis.

Treat biopsy-proven PACNS with corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide (or rituximab) for induction, then maintenance immunosuppression; RCVS, by contrast, is managed with nimodipine and trigger avoidance.

cases.title: Recurrent Worst-Ever Headaches in a New Mother
cases.chiefComplaint: Several abrupt, severe 'worst headache of my life' episodes over the past week, three weeks postpartum.
cases.history: A 39-year-old woman presents to the ED with recurrent thunderclap headaches. She delivered a healthy baby three weeks ago by uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Over the past week she has had at least five episodes of sudden, explosive bilateral headache peaking within seconds to a 10/10, several triggered by coughing, straining during a bowel movement, or breastfeeding. Each lasts 1-3 hours. The most recent was accompanied by transient blurred vision and a brief tingling in her right hand. She took pseudoephedrine for congestion and uses a herbal galactagogue. PMH: migraine without aura. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen, pseudoephedrine, and the herbal supplement. She had been prescribed methylergonovine briefly after delivery for bleeding. No allergies. She does not smoke; rare alcohol; denies cocaine or other stimulants. Family history unremarkable for aneurysm or stroke. Review of systems: no fever, neck stiffness reported only with the headaches, no rash, no jaw claudication, no recent trauma, and no preeclampsia diagnosis during pregnancy though she recalls borderline blood pressures near term.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but alert and fully oriented; no acute headache at the moment of exam. Neck supple with full range of motion; no meningismus. Cranial nerves intact; fundi without papilledema; visual fields full on confrontation. Motor strength full in all limbs without drift. Reflexes symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Sensation intact; the earlier right-hand tingling has resolved. Coordination and gait normal. No carotid bruits, no temporal artery tenderness. Skin without rash or petechiae. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. No focal deficit at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has recurrent thunderclap headaches. What is the single most important immediate diagnostic step in the ED?
options: Empiric verapamil and discharge as the first step today
Reassurance and outpatient migraine follow-up as the first step today
MRI of the cervical spine for suspected dissection as the first step today
Non-contrast head CT,
stages.explanation: Any thunderclap headache mandates urgent exclusion of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage: a non-contrast head CT first, and if negative (especially beyond 6 hours), a lumbar puncture for xanthochromia/RBCs or CT angiography to evaluate for aneurysm and SAH. Only after life-threatening SAH is excluded should other causes (including RCVS) be pursued. Reassurance/discharge is unsafe with thunderclap headache. Empiric verapamil presumes RCVS before excluding hemorrhage. Cervical-spine MRI for dissection is not the priority; the emergent concern is intracranial hemorrhage. Her recurrent, trigger-provoked thunderclap pattern raises RCVS, but SAH must be excluded first.

stages.label: IMAGING & CSF
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: no hemorrhage; small focal cortical subarachnoid blood is NOT seen this time (clean study)
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure normal; RBC 2/µL; no xanthochromia; WBC 1/µL; protein 30 mg/dL; glucose normal
MR angiography head: multifocal segmental narrowing ('string of beads') of multiple intracranial arteries
MRI brain: no acute infarct; small area of vasogenic edema in the parieto-occipital region
Urine drug screen: negative for cocaine/amphetamines

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: SAH is excluded and MRA shows multifocal segmental arterial narrowing. Given the postpartum timing, vasoactive exposures, and thunderclap pattern, what is the most likely diagnosis and the key feature that confirms it?
options: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, confirmed by reversibility of vasoconstriction on repeat imaging at 12 weeks
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, confirmed by absent venous flow as the primary diagnosis
Primary CNS vasculitis, confirmed by abnormal inflammatory CSF as the primary diagnosis
Aneurysmal SAH, confirmed by CT angiography as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The postpartum state, vasoactive exposures (pseudoephedrine, methylergonovine, herbal galactagogue), recurrent trigger-provoked thunderclap headaches, normal/near-normal CSF, and segmental 'string of beads' arterial narrowing are classic for RCVS. The defining feature is reversibility, vasoconstriction resolves within about 12 weeks, distinguishing it from true vasculitis. PACNS typically has abnormal (lymphocytic) CSF and is not reversible; her CSF is normal. SAH is excluded by clean CT and LP. CVST would show venous (not arterial) abnormalities and a different headache profile. Documenting normalization of the arteries on follow-up imaging confirms RCVS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for her reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome?
options: High-dose corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide as initial therapy
Discontinue all vasoactive agents, control blood pressure, give a calcium-channel blocker ,
Emergent aneurysm coiling as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: RCVS management centers on removing precipitants and supporting the vessels: stop all vasoconstrictive/serotonergic drugs (pseudoephedrine, ergot/methylergonovine, the herbal supplement), control blood pressure, and use a calcium-channel blocker such as nimodipine or verapamil to relieve headaches and vasospasm, while counseling avoidance of triggers (Valsalva, exertion, sexual activity, stimulants). Corticosteroids are not only unhelpful but may worsen RCVS outcomes and are reserved for true vasculitis. Anticoagulation is not indicated and risks hemorrhagic complications (RCVS can cause cortical SAH and reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy). There is no aneurysm to coil. Most patients recover fully with trigger removal and supportive care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is most characteristically associated with RCVS and should be anticipated during her course?
options: Convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage
Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured berry aneurysm
Cavernous sinus thrombosis as the leading concern
Spinal epidural hematoma as the leading concern
stages.explanation: RCVS is classically associated with non-aneurysmal convexity (cortical) subarachnoid hemorrhage, as well as PRES-like vasogenic edema (often parieto-occipital), watershed ischemic strokes, and occasionally intracerebral hemorrhage. Her MRI already shows parieto-occipital vasogenic edema, fitting PRES overlap. Aneurysmal SAH arises from a berry aneurysm and has been excluded here; the SAH of RCVS is cortical/convexity, not basal-cistern aneurysmal bleeding. Cavernous sinus thrombosis and spinal epidural hematoma are unrelated to RCVS pathophysiology. Recognizing the cortical SAH/PRES association guides imaging surveillance and reinforces avoiding anticoagulation and steroids.

cases.diagnosis: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
pearls: RCVS presents with recurrent trigger-provoked thunderclap headaches, classically postpartum or after vasoactive/serotonergic drugs (pseudoephedrine, ergots, SSRIs, cannabis).
Always exclude aneurysmal SAH first (CT then LP/CTA); RCVS shows 'string of beads' segmental arterial narrowing with normal/near-normal CSF.
Diagnosis is confirmed by reversibility of vasoconstriction within ~12 weeks, distinguishing it from primary CNS vasculitis (abnormal CSF, non-reversible).
Treat by removing precipitants, controlling BP, and using a calcium-channel blocker (nimodipine/verapamil); avoid steroids and anticoagulation. Watch for convexity SAH and PRES.

cases.title: An Older Woman With Dizziness, Double Vision, and Jaw Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden vertigo, double vision, and unsteadiness after weeks of headaches and jaw pain while chewing.
cases.history: A 76-year-old woman is brought to the ED with acute onset of severe vertigo, binocular diplopia, and gait unsteadiness for two hours. For the preceding six weeks she has had a new persistent bitemporal headache, scalp tenderness when combing her hair, and pain in her jaw that worsens midway through meals and forces her to stop chewing. She has had fatigue, low-grade malaise, low appetite, and bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness worst in the mornings. She briefly lost vision in her right eye yesterday for several minutes. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg. No allergies. Former smoker, quit 20 years ago; no alcohol or drugs. Lives independently. Family history noncontributory. Review of systems: no chest pain, palpitations, fever, weight loss quantified but clothes feel looser, no rash. She has not had recent trauma. She denies tongue pain but reports the jaw pain is reproducible and fatiguing, classic for claudication, and the headache is unlike any prior headache.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, ill-appearing. Right temporal artery is thickened, tender, and pulseless to palpation; left temporal artery tender. Visual acuity: right 20/60, left 20/25; right relative afferent pupillary defect is borderline. Cranial nerves: partial right sixth-nerve palsy producing diplopia; nystagmus on lateral gaze. Motor strength full. Dysmetria on right finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin; truncal and gait ataxia with a wide base, unable to tandem walk. Reflexes symmetric; plantars flexor. Proximal shoulder and hip girdle tenderness with mildly reduced active range from pain. No carotid bruits. Cardiac and lung exams unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her acute deficits (vertigo, diplopia from a sixth-nerve palsy, limb and gait ataxia) localize to which vascular territory?
options: Anterior cerebral artery territory as the first step today
Vertebrobasilar circulation supplying the brainstem
Left middle cerebral artery (anterior circulation)
Lenticulostriate small-vessel (pure lacunar) territory
stages.explanation: Vertigo, brainstem cranial-nerve findings (sixth-nerve palsy, nystagmus), and cerebellar signs (limb dysmetria, truncal/gait ataxia) localize to the posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulation supplying the brainstem and cerebellum. An MCA infarct would cause contralateral face/arm weakness, aphasia, or neglect, not this brainstem-cerebellar syndrome. ACA territory causes contralateral leg-predominant weakness. Pure lacunar syndromes (e.g., pure motor or pure sensory stroke) lack the multimodal brainstem-cerebellar combination seen here. Recognizing posterior-circulation localization, combined with her systemic symptoms, points toward a large-vessel arteritis affecting the vertebral arteries.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 92 mm/hr; CRP 8.4 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (normocytic); platelets 480 ×10³/µL
MRI brain (DWI): acute infarcts in the right cerebellar hemisphere and lateral pons
MRA/CTA neck: bilateral smooth tapering and stenosis of the distal vertebral arteries
Temporal artery ultrasound: hypoechoic 'halo sign' around the right temporal artery

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Markedly elevated ESR/CRP, jaw claudication, temporal artery abnormality with a halo sign, polymyalgia symptoms, and vertebral-artery stenosis with posterior strokes. What is the most appropriate immediate action even before biopsy?
options: Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately to prevent permanent vision loss
Withhold all therapy until temporal artery biopsy results return
Start therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin as the primary diagnosis
Begin tPA thrombolysis for the cerebellar infarct as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency because of impending irreversible vision loss (and, here, ongoing posterior-circulation stroke). When clinical suspicion is high, high-dose corticosteroids (often IV methylprednisolone with ocular involvement) must be started immediately and should NOT be delayed for biopsy, the biopsy remains positive for 1-2 weeks after steroids begin. Withholding therapy risks blindness. tPA is inappropriate: she is outside any window with established infarcts and an inflammatory, non-thrombolyzable mechanism. Anticoagulation does not treat the underlying arteritis and adds bleeding risk. Steroids address the inflammatory arteritis driving both the cranial ischemia and stroke.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: After starting steroids, what is the most appropriate confirmatory test, and what is the key caveat?
options: Serum ANCA testing; a positive result confirms GCA as the immediate next step
Catheter cerebral angiography; it is the gold standard and is unaffected by steroids in this setting
Temporal artery biopsy, ideally within ~1-2 weeks of starting steroids
Lumbar puncture; CSF pleocytosis confirms the diagnosis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard for GCA and should be obtained promptly, it usually remains positive for one to two weeks after starting steroids, so treatment should not wait. A key caveat is that GCA causes patchy 'skip lesions,' so a negative unilateral biopsy does not exclude the disease; a contralateral or longer-segment biopsy, or imaging (ultrasound halo sign, MRA/PET) may be needed. Catheter angiography is not the confirmatory test for cranial GCA and adds risk. ANCA is associated with small-vessel vasculitides (e.g., GPA), not GCA. CSF analysis is not used to diagnose GCA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond corticosteroids, which adjunctive treatment has the best evidence to reduce relapses and cumulative steroid exposure in GCA?
options: Methotrexate as definitive monotherapy replacing steroids
Daily aspirin as the primary disease-modifying therapy
Tocilizumab as a steroid-sparing agent
Cyclophosphamide induction as first line for all patients
stages.explanation: Tocilizumab, an IL-6 receptor antagonist, has randomized-trial evidence (GiACTA) for inducing and maintaining remission while reducing cumulative glucocorticoid exposure and relapses in GCA, making it the best-supported steroid-sparing agent. Aspirin is often added for ischemic-event reduction but is adjunctive, not disease-modifying. Methotrexate has modest steroid-sparing benefit but is not a standalone replacement for steroids and is less effective than tocilizumab. Cyclophosphamide is reserved for refractory or severe cases, not routine first-line GCA. Comprehensive care also includes steroid taper, bone protection, and glucose/BP monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which ophthalmologic complication of GCA is the most feared and the reason for emergent steroid initiation?
options: Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy causing sudden permanent vision loss
Central serous chorioretinopathy as the leading concern
Posterior vitreous detachment as the leading concern
Open-angle glaucoma as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AAION), from inflammatory occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries, causes sudden, often irreversible monocular vision loss and can rapidly affect the second eye; this is why GCA is treated emergently and steroids are not delayed for biopsy. Her transient monocular vision loss (amaurosis) is an ominous warning of impending AAION. Central serous chorioretinopathy is associated with steroid use/stress, not GCA ischemia. Open-angle glaucoma and posterior vitreous detachment are unrelated to arteritis. Preventing AAION (and contralateral involvement) is the central rationale for immediate high-dose corticosteroids.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell arteritis with vertebrobasilar stroke
pearls: New headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, polymyalgia symptoms, and markedly elevated ESR/CRP in a patient over 50 should prompt urgent GCA evaluation.
GCA can cause vertebrobasilar stroke via vertebral-artery arteritis; the temporal artery halo sign on ultrasound supports the diagnosis.
Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately when GCA is suspected, do NOT wait for biopsy; biopsy stays positive for 1-2 weeks and skip lesions can cause false negatives.
Arteritic AION causes sudden permanent vision loss; tocilizumab is an evidence-based steroid-sparing agent, and aspirin is a useful adjunct.

cases.title: Fever, Strokes, and a New Heart Murmur in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Fevers and confusion with a new focal neurologic deficit in a patient who injects drugs.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man with a history of intravenous heroin use is admitted with one week of fevers, chills, malaise, and night sweats, now with confusion and sudden left-sided weakness. He reports using non-sterile injection technique. Two days ago he had transient slurred speech that resolved. PMH: prior skin abscesses from injection; hepatitis C; no known cardiac disease. Medications: none. No allergies. Social: active IV drug use, smokes tobacco, intermittently homeless, last incarcerated two years ago. Family history noncontributory. Review of systems: pleuritic chest discomfort, mild dyspnea, decreased appetite with weight loss, and a 'sudden severe headache' yesterday that has since faded to a dull ache. He denies recent dental work. He reports painful nodules on his fingers and some new dark spots on his palms and soles. No prior endocarditis. He has not been on antibiotics recently and has had no recent hospitalizations besides abscess drainage.
cases.exam: Febrile, tachycardic, ill-appearing, mildly confused (oriented to person and place only). New holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla and a separate early diastolic murmur. Skin: tender violaceous nodules on finger pads (Osler nodes), non-tender erythematous macules on palms/soles (Janeway lesions), and a splinter hemorrhage under one nail. Fundi: a flame hemorrhage with pale center (Roth spot). Neuro: left facial droop, left arm and leg weakness 4/5, left-sided hyperreflexia with an upgoing left toe; mild left neglect. Lungs with scattered crackles. Multiple track marks on both forearms. No nuchal rigidity at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Fever, new regurgitant murmurs, embolic skin/eye stigmata, and a focal stroke in an IV drug user. What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic priority?
options: Obtain at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites
Start therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed cardioembolic stroke
Give IV tPA for the acute deficit as the first step today
Order outpatient Holter monitoring for atrial fibrillation in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The picture is highly suggestive of infective endocarditis with septic embolization. The immediate priority is to obtain multiple sets of blood cultures (ideally three from separate venipunctures) before antibiotics and to arrange echocardiography (transthoracic, then transesophageal for sensitivity) to identify vegetations, per modified Duke criteria. Anticoagulation is contraindicated in this setting because of high hemorrhagic transformation and mycotic-aneurysm rupture risk. Holter monitoring for AF misattributes the embolic source, the source here is cardiac vegetation, not arrhythmia. tPA is contraindicated in suspected infective endocarditis due to markedly increased intracranial hemorrhage risk from septic emboli/mycotic aneurysms.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL with left shift; CRP 14 mg/dL; ESR 70 mm/hr
Blood cultures (3/3 sets): Staphylococcus aureus, methicillin-sensitive
Transesophageal echo: 1.4 cm mobile vegetation on the mitral valve with moderate regurgitation; small aortic-valve vegetation
CT head: multiple bilateral cortical infarcts of varying ages; small focus of subarachnoid blood at a distal MCA branch
CTA head: 4 mm distal MCA branch aneurysm (mycotic) suspected

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Cultures grow MSSA with mitral and aortic vegetations and embolic strokes. He also has a small distal MCA aneurysm. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Start IV antibiotics plus full-dose anticoagulation to prevent further emboli
Begin antiplatelet therapy and add warfarin for stroke prevention as initial therapy
Start prolonged IV antibiotics targeting MSSA
Defer antibiotics until valve surgery is performed as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Treatment of infective endocarditis is prolonged culture-directed IV antibiotics (for MSSA, an antistaphylococcal beta-lactam such as nafcillin/cefazolin). Anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy are generally withheld in IE because they do not prevent embolization from vegetations and substantially increase the risk of hemorrhagic transformation and rupture of mycotic aneurysms, of which he already has one. Adding warfarin or antiplatelets would be dangerous here. Antibiotics must not be deferred for surgery; they are started immediately after cultures. Surgical indications (e.g., heart failure, large mobile vegetation, recurrent emboli, abscess) are evaluated in parallel, with neurosurgery weighing in on timing given the intracranial findings.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why does the presence of a mycotic (infectious) intracranial aneurysm specifically delay or contraindicate anticoagulation in this patient?
options: Mycotic aneurysms are unaffected by anticoagulation as the leading concern
Anticoagulation causes the aneurysm to thrombose harmlessly as the leading concern
Mycotic aneurysms have friable, inflamed walls prone to rupture,
Anticoagulation accelerates clearance of the infection from the valve as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Mycotic (infectious) aneurysms form when septic emboli seed and inflame the arterial wall, producing thin, friable, rupture-prone vessels. Anticoagulation in this setting dramatically increases the risk of fatal intracranial hemorrhage from these fragile aneurysms and from hemorrhagic transformation of septic infarcts, which is why it is avoided or delayed in endocarditis-associated stroke. Anticoagulation does not clear valvular infection (antibiotics do) nor safely thrombose mycotic aneurysms. Saying they are unaffected is wrong, the bleeding risk is precisely the concern. Management includes serial vascular imaging; enlarging or ruptured aneurysms may require endovascular or surgical treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He needs mitral valve surgery for a large mobile vegetation and worsening regurgitation, but he just had embolic strokes and has a small unruptured mycotic aneurysm. How should valve-surgery timing be approached?
options: Coordinate a multidisciplinary decision
Proceed only after the mycotic aneurysm has fully resolved on its own with appropriate follow-up
Operate emergently regardless of recent stroke or hemorrhage status with appropriate follow-up
Permanently defer surgery because any stroke is an absolute contraindication with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Timing of valve surgery after neurologic events requires a multidisciplinary (cardiology, cardiac surgery, neurology/neurosurgery, ID) approach. Evidence supports that after a small, non-hemorrhagic ischemic stroke, valve surgery for a strong indication (large mobile vegetation, severe regurgitation, heart failure) can often proceed without prolonged delay because cardiopulmonary bypass anticoagulation risk is lower than the embolic/cardiac risk. However, recent significant intracranial hemorrhage usually mandates delaying surgery (often weeks) to reduce catastrophic bleeding. Emergent surgery regardless of bleeding status is unsafe. Stroke is not an absolute permanent contraindication. Waiting for spontaneous aneurysm resolution is impractical and risky; aneurysm management proceeds on its own track.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond medical and surgical management of this episode, what is a critical component of his long-term care plan?
options: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis for all future minor procedures only as the immediate next step
Lifelong warfarin to prevent recurrent emboli as the immediate next step
Indefinite bed rest to prevent embolization as the immediate next step
Substance-use disorder treatment
stages.explanation: Because his endocarditis stems from injection drug use, the single most impactful long-term intervention is treating the underlying substance-use disorder, offering medication for opioid use disorder (e.g., buprenorphine or methadone) and harm-reduction services, which directly reduces recurrent endocarditis and reinfection of any new valve. Lifelong warfarin is not indicated and is hazardous given mycotic-aneurysm/hemorrhage risk. Antibiotic prophylaxis guidelines apply selectively (e.g., high-risk cardiac conditions before certain dental procedures) and do not address the root cause. Bed rest does not prevent vegetation embolization. Addiction treatment, alongside hepatitis C management and social support, is central to preventing recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis with septic cerebral emboli and mycotic aneurysm
pearls: Fever, new regurgitant murmur, embolic stigmata (Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, Roth spots, splinter hemorrhages), and stroke in an IV drug user mean infective endocarditis until proven otherwise.
Get at least three blood culture sets and echocardiography (TTE then TEE); apply modified Duke criteria; S. aureus is the leading organism in IV drug users.
Avoid anticoagulation, antiplatelets, and tPA in IE-related stroke, mycotic aneurysms and septic infarcts make intracranial hemorrhage risk prohibitive.
Valve surgery timing is multidisciplinary: small ischemic strokes rarely delay needed surgery, but recent intracranial hemorrhage does; treat the underlying opioid use disorder to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Recurrent Strokes, Fevers, and Weight Loss in a Middle-Aged Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Two strokes in six months with ongoing low-grade fevers, joint aches, and unintended weight loss.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman is referred after a second embolic-appearing stroke in six months. The first caused transient right-arm weakness; the second a brief episode of aphasia. Between events she has had months of low-grade fevers, fatigue, migratory joint aches, and an unintentional 12-pound weight loss. She reports positional symptoms, sometimes a brief lightheaded, near-syncopal feeling when she stands or rolls over in bed, occasionally with a fleeting 'thud' sensation in her chest. PMH: none significant; no atrial fibrillation on prior monitoring. Medications: none. No allergies. She does not smoke, rarely drinks, no drugs. She works as a librarian. Family history noncontributory; no early stroke, no clotting disorders, no autoimmune disease. Review of systems: occasional exertional dyspnea, no chest pain at rest, no rash, no oral ulcers, no Raynaud, no miscarriages, no photosensitivity. She had an extensive prior workup including a normal lipid panel and HbA1c, and a transthoracic echo earlier in the year that was reported as 'limited' and 'technically suboptimal.'
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, fluent with fully resolved prior deficits. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields full. Motor strength full without drift. Reflexes symmetric; plantars flexor. Sensation intact; coordination and gait normal. Cardiac exam: a low-pitched diastolic sound ('tumor plop') after S2 and a soft mid-diastolic murmur at the apex that changes with position. No fixed murmur of classic mitral stenosis quality. No peripheral stigmata of endocarditis (no Osler/Janeway lesions, no splinters). Lungs clear. No rash, livedo, or synovitis on exam today. No carotid bruit. Mild pallor of conjunctivae.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Recurrent embolic strokes plus constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, arthralgias), a position-dependent diastolic 'plop,' and near-syncope when changing position most strongly suggest which source?
options: Antiphospholipid syndrome as the first step today
A mobile intracardiac mass, such as a left atrial myxoma, causing embolism, obstruction,
Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as the first step today
Carotid atherosclerotic plaque with artery-to-artery embolism as the first step today
stages.explanation: The triad of embolic phenomena, constitutional/inflammatory symptoms (fever, weight loss, arthralgias, anemia), and intracardiac obstruction (position-dependent diastolic 'tumor plop,' near-syncope when the mass intermittently obstructs the mitral valve) is classic for a left atrial myxoma. Constitutional symptoms arise from cytokine (IL-6) release by the tumor. Paroxysmal AF can cause emboli but not a tumor plop, positional obstructive symptoms, or constitutional inflammation. Carotid atheroembolism doesn't explain cardiac obstructive findings or systemic symptoms and is unlikely with a normal vascular risk profile. Antiphospholipid syndrome causes thrombosis/miscarriage but not a diastolic plop or positional near-syncope; she has no obstetric or thrombotic history.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 58 mm/hr; CRP 4.2 mg/dL; IL-6 elevated
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (normocytic anemia); platelets normal
Blood cultures x3: no growth
MRI brain: multiple cortical infarcts of differing ages in multiple territories
Transthoracic echo (repeat, adequate): a mobile pedunculated mass in the left atrium attached to the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis, prolapsing toward the mitral valve in diastole

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Echo shows a mobile pedunculated left atrial mass attached to the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis. Which feature most favors atrial myxoma over a thrombus or vegetation?
options: Attachment to the mitral valve leaflet tips with positive blood cultures as the primary diagnosis
Pedunculated attachment to the interatrial septum at the fossa ovalis with negative blood cultures
Multiple small mobile masses on all four valves as the primary diagnosis
A laminated mass filling the left atrial appendage in a patient with atrial fibrillation in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Atrial myxomas, the most common primary cardiac tumor, are typically pedunculated and attach to the interatrial septum at the fossa ovalis, prolapsing through the mitral valve in diastole, and they cause constitutional/inflammatory symptoms (fever, weight loss, anemia, elevated ESR/IL-6) with negative blood cultures. Valve-tip masses with positive cultures indicate infective endocarditis vegetations. A laminated appendage mass in an AF patient indicates left atrial appendage thrombus. Diffuse small masses on multiple valves suggest nonbacterial thrombotic (marantic) endocarditis. The septal/fossa-ovalis pedunculated morphology plus sterile cultures and systemic inflammation is the discriminating profile for myxoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for her left atrial myxoma?
options: Serial echocardiographic surveillance without intervention
Long-term anticoagulation with warfarin
Chemotherapy with anthracyclines as initial therapy
Prompt surgical resection of the tumor
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for atrial myxoma is prompt surgical resection, which both eliminates the embolic source and relieves intracardiac obstruction; it is generally curative, with low recurrence except in familial (Carney complex) cases. Anticoagulation does not address a friable tumor that embolizes fragments and does not relieve obstruction; it is not definitive. Chemotherapy is for malignant tumors, whereas myxomas are benign and surgically curable. Surveillance alone is inappropriate given recurrent strokes and obstructive symptoms, the risk of further embolization and sudden hemodynamic compromise mandates timely surgery. Postoperatively, surveillance echocardiography monitors for the rare recurrence.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful resection, what is the most appropriate counseling regarding recurrence and follow-up?
options: Most sporadic myxomas do not recur, but familial cases recur more often, so ask about family history of myxomas, pigmented skin lesions,
Recurrence is impossible after resection, so no follow-up is needed with appropriate follow-up
She will require lifelong warfarin to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
Recurrence always indicates malignant transformation requiring chemotherapy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Most sporadic atrial myxomas are cured by complete resection with low recurrence; however, familial myxoma syndromes, particularly Carney complex, recur more frequently and can be multifocal. Counseling should screen for a family history of myxomas, spotty skin pigmentation (lentigines), and endocrine overactivity (e.g., Cushing syndrome), and arrange periodic surveillance echocardiography, especially in younger patients or those with suggestive features. Claiming recurrence is impossible is false. Lifelong warfarin is not indicated for prevention after resection. Recurrence of a myxoma does not equal malignant transformation, myxomas are benign, and recurrence usually reflects incomplete resection or a familial predisposition rather than cancer.

cases.diagnosis: Left atrial myxoma with embolic stroke
pearls: Suspect atrial myxoma when recurrent embolic strokes accompany constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, anemia, elevated ESR/IL-6) and obstructive/positional cardiac findings ('tumor plop').
Myxomas are typically pedunculated and attach to the interatrial septum at the fossa ovalis, prolapsing through the mitral valve; blood cultures are negative.
A 'technically limited' TTE can miss it, repeat with adequate imaging or TEE when suspicion is high.
Definitive treatment is prompt surgical resection (usually curative); screen for Carney complex (familial myxoma, lentigines, endocrine tumors) given higher recurrence.

cases.title: Sudden Leg Paralysis With Loss of Pain but Preserved Position Sense
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt severe back pain followed within minutes by paralysis of both legs.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with sudden onset of sharp interscapular back pain followed within minutes by inability to move both legs and loss of bladder control. He was lifting boxes when symptoms began. PMH: hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg and lisinopril 20 mg, type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. He had an endovascular abdominal aortic aneurysm repair 18 months ago. No allergies. Social: retired mechanic, current smoker, moderate alcohol. Family history: father had an MI at 60. Review of systems: no fever, no recent infection, no trauma to the back beyond lifting, no preceding leg numbness or weakness. He reports the legs feel 'dead' and he cannot feel hot or cold or pinprick below the chest, yet he can still tell where his toes are when moved. No neck pain, no arm symptoms, no facial droop, no diplopia. He denies recent procedures aside from a routine surveillance CT for his aortic graft last month.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, in distress from pain. Cranial nerves intact; arms with full strength and normal sensation. Lower extremities: flaccid paraplegia (0-1/5 strength bilaterally). Sensory: absent pinprick and temperature sensation below a T4-T5 level bilaterally; however, vibration and proprioception are PRESERVED in both feet and toes. Reflexes initially absent in the legs (spinal shock) with bilateral upgoing toes. Anal sphincter tone reduced; urinary retention with a palpable bladder. No spinal tenderness to percussion, no step-off. No saddle anesthesia sparing of light touch noted; temperature/pain clearly lost. Abdominal aortic graft site without bruit change.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has abrupt flaccid paraplegia with loss of pain/temperature below T4-T5 but PRESERVED vibration and proprioception, plus autonomic (bladder/sphincter) involvement. What does this dissociated sensory pattern localize to?
options: A peripheral polyneuropathy as the first step today
The anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord
Posterior columns (dorsal cord) as the first step today
The cerebral cortex (parasagittal region)
stages.explanation: Loss of pain and temperature (spinothalamic) and motor function (corticospinal) with sparing of vibration and proprioception (dorsal columns) is the hallmark anterior cord syndrome, localizing to the anterior two-thirds of the cord supplied by the single anterior spinal artery. Autonomic and bladder involvement fit cord, not peripheral, disease. Posterior column (dorsal cord) lesions would impair vibration/proprioception, the opposite of his findings. A peripheral polyneuropathy is length-dependent, gradual, lacks a sensory level and upgoing toes, and spares sphincter abruptly. A parasagittal cortical lesion can cause bilateral leg weakness but would not produce a dissociated spinal sensory level with preserved proprioception.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the abrupt onset, vascular risk factors, and prior aortic surgery, what is the most appropriate immediate imaging to confirm the suspected diagnosis and exclude compressive causes?
options: Lumbar puncture for cell count and protein
Plain radiographs of the thoracic spine
Emergent MRI of the spine with diffusion-weighted imaging
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies
stages.explanation: Emergent spinal MRI (ideally with diffusion-weighted sequences) is the test of choice: it can demonstrate cord infarction (T2/DWI hyperintensity, classically an 'owl-eye' or anterior pencil-like pattern in the central/anterior cord) and, critically, exclude compressive or hemorrhagic causes (epidural hematoma, abscess, disc, tumor) that require urgent surgery. Plain films cannot assess the cord or its blood supply. Lumbar puncture is not first-line and could be hazardous before excluding a mass; it does not establish infarction. EMG/NCS evaluate peripheral nerves over weeks and have no role in this acute central process. Rapid MRI both confirms ischemia and rules out treatable compression.

stages.label: MRI SPINE
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine: T2 and DWI hyperintensity in the anterior/central cord from T4 to T7
Sagittal images: 'pencil-like' longitudinal hyperintensity in the anterior cord
Axial images: 'owl-eye' (anterior horn) hyperintensity pattern
No epidural or intramedullary hematoma; no abscess; no compressive mass or disc herniation
Vertebral bodies without infarct signal; aortic graft in place

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI confirms anterior spinal cord infarction without a compressive lesion. What is the most appropriate management focus?
options: Supportive care: maintain adequate spinal cord perfusion , treat secondary stroke risk factors/antiplatelet therapy,
Immediate surgical decompression as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin as standard of care as initial therapy
High-dose IV steroids as definitive treatment to reverse the infarct as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Spinal cord infarction has no proven targeted reperfusion therapy; management is supportive and aimed at preserving residual cord perfusion. Key measures: avoid hypotension (sometimes permissive/augmented mean arterial pressure or CSF drainage in perioperative aortic cases), address vascular risk factors, typically start antiplatelet therapy for secondary prevention, and provide aggressive rehabilitation with bladder, bowel, and skin care to prevent complications. Surgery is for compressive lesions, which MRI has excluded. Routine anticoagulation is not standard unless a specific cardioembolic/hypercoagulable source is identified. High-dose steroids are not proven to reverse cord infarction (unlike their disproven/limited role here) and carry risks; they are not definitive therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which underlying mechanism is most consistent with his presentation and should guide secondary prevention?
options: Transverse myelitis from an autoimmune process as the immediate next step
Multiple sclerosis flare as the immediate next step
Aortic pathology/atherosclerotic compromise of radicular supply to the anterior spinal artery
Guillain-Barre syndrome as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Anterior spinal artery territory infarction in a patient with diffuse atherosclerosis and prior aortic surgery most often results from compromise of the segmental/radicular arteries (notably the artery of Adamkiewicz) that feed the anterior spinal artery, frequently in the setting of aortic disease, aortic procedures, hypotension, or atheroembolism. This guides aggressive risk-factor modification and antiplatelet therapy. MS and transverse myelitis are inflammatory, typically subacute, often with enhancement and CSF abnormalities, and do not produce this hyperacute dissociated vascular pattern. Guillain-Barre is an ascending areflexic peripheral neuropathy with albuminocytologic dissociation, not a sensory-level cord syndrome with preserved proprioception and upgoing toes.

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous anterior spinal cord infarction (anterior spinal artery syndrome)
pearls: Anterior cord (anterior spinal artery) syndrome: abrupt paraplegia with loss of pain/temperature but PRESERVED vibration and proprioception, plus early autonomic/bladder dysfunction.
Emergent spinal MRI with DWI confirms cord infarction ('owl-eye' axial, 'pencil-like' sagittal) and excludes compressive causes (epidural hematoma, abscess, tumor) needing surgery.
Aortic disease and aortic surgery threaten the artery of Adamkiewicz and radicular feeders; hypotension and atheroembolism are common precipitants.
No reperfusion therapy exists, manage by preserving cord perfusion (avoid hypotension), antiplatelet/risk-factor control, and intensive rehabilitation with bladder/bowel/skin care.

cases.title: Severe Hypertension With Headache and One-Sided Weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache and right-sided weakness with markedly elevated blood pressure.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after presenting with a severe headache, right arm and leg weakness, and slurred speech that began three hours ago. He has poorly controlled hypertension and admits to running out of his medications two weeks ago. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease (stage 3), and prior tobacco use. Home medications (recently lapsed): amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 40 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg, metformin, and atorvastatin. No allergies. Social: former smoker, occasional alcohol, works as a long-haul driver. Family history: hypertension and stroke in both parents. Review of systems: headache is severe but not 'thunderclap,' no chest pain, no visual loss, no seizure, no recent trauma. He has had some blurred vision and nausea today. He denies cocaine or stimulant use. He reports no prior strokes. On arrival he is hypertensive to a markedly elevated level and is being evaluated for acute stroke with imaging pending the management decision about blood pressure.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, mildly drowsy but follows commands. Speech mildly dysarthric. Cranial nerves: mild right facial droop (lower face), otherwise intact; fundi show grade III hypertensive changes (flame hemorrhages, exudates) without frank papilledema. Motor: right arm and leg weakness 3/5 with a right pronator drift. Right-sided hyperreflexia with an upgoing right toe. Sensation grossly intact. NIH Stroke Scale approximately 6. No nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam regular with an S4; lungs clear. Peripheral pulses intact. No carotid bruit. He is on a cardiac monitor with frequent automated BP cuff readings; an arterial line is being placed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before deciding on blood-pressure targets, what is the single most important immediate test to guide management of his acute focal deficit?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound as the first step today
Non-contrast head CT to distinguish ischemic stroke from intracerebral hemorrhage
Lumbar puncture as the first step today
Electroencephalogram as the first step today
stages.explanation: The blood-pressure strategy hinges entirely on whether this is ischemic stroke or intracerebral hemorrhage, so an emergent non-contrast head CT is the critical first step. Ischemia and hemorrhage have nearly opposite BP goals: in ischemic stroke you tolerate higher pressures to preserve the penumbra (and follow thrombolysis/thrombectomy thresholds), whereas in ICH you lower BP more aggressively to limit hematoma expansion. Lumbar puncture is not indicated acutely and is unsafe before excluding a mass/bleed. Carotid duplex evaluates chronic stenosis, not the acute decision. EEG addresses seizures, not the hemorrhage-versus-ischemia question. CT rapidly resolves this and dictates the BP plan.

stages.label: CT & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: acute ischemic changes in the left MCA territory; no hemorrhage
CT angiography: left M2 branch occlusion
Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline CKD); glucose 184 mg/dL
Troponin negative; ECG: LVH with strain, no acute ischemia
NIH Stroke Scale 6; last known well 3 hours ago

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT shows acute ischemic stroke (no hemorrhage) and he is within the thrombolysis window. What is the appropriate blood-pressure management to permit IV thrombolysis?
options: Leave the BP untreated at 224/126 and give thrombolysis immediately as initial therapy
Rapidly lower BP to below 120/80 mmHg before thrombolysis as initial therapy
Lower BP to below 185/110 mmHg with a titratable agent before giving thrombolysis, then keep it under 180/105 mmHg afterward
Withhold thrombolysis permanently because his BP is too high to ever treat as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For IV thrombolysis in acute ischemic stroke, blood pressure must be reduced to below 185/110 mmHg before administration and maintained below 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours afterward to limit hemorrhagic transformation, using a titratable IV agent (e.g., labetalol or nicardipine). Giving thrombolysis at 224/126 violates safety thresholds and risks hemorrhage. Driving BP below 120/80 is dangerous, it can collapse penumbral perfusion and extend the infarct. Withholding thrombolysis permanently is wrong: the elevated BP is a modifiable contraindication, so you lower it into range and then treat if otherwise eligible. Mechanical thrombectomy is also considered given the M2 occlusion.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Suppose instead he was NOT a candidate for thrombolysis or thrombectomy. How should his blood pressure be managed in the first 24-48 hours of an acute ischemic stroke?
options: Stop all antihypertensives indefinitely regardless of the BP level as the immediate next step
Practice 'permissive hypertension,' generally not lowering BP unless it exceeds about 220/120 mmHg , then reducing it only modestly
Start an aggressive nitroprusside drip to reach normal BP immediately as the immediate next step
Aggressively normalize BP to below 120/80 within the first hour as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In acute ischemic stroke without thrombolysis/thrombectomy, guidelines support 'permissive hypertension': avoid lowering BP unless it exceeds roughly 220/120 mmHg (or there is a comorbid indication such as aortic dissection, acute coronary syndrome, heart failure, or hemorrhagic transformation), and even then reduce it only modestly (about 15% in the first 24 hours). This preserves perfusion to the ischemic penumbra. Aggressive normalization to below 120/80 can extend the infarct by reducing collateral flow. An immediate nitroprusside drip to normal BP is excessive and risky. Stopping all antihypertensives indefinitely ignores the need for controlled, gradual reintroduction once the patient stabilizes.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The core dilemma in his care is balancing two opposing risks. Which statement best captures the rationale behind cautious BP lowering in acute ischemic stroke?
options: Blood pressure has no relationship to penumbral survival as the leading concern
Lower BP always improves outcomes by reducing edema as the leading concern
Hemorrhagic transformation is unrelated to blood pressure as the leading concern
Excessive BP lowering reduces perfusion to the ischemic penumbra
stages.explanation: The central tension in acute ischemic stroke BP management is that the penumbra, salvageable tissue around the infarct core, depends on collateral perfusion that is pressure-passive, so dropping BP too far worsens ischemia and extends the infarct. Conversely, too-high BP, particularly after thrombolysis or thrombectomy, increases the risk of hemorrhagic transformation. This is why targets differ by treatment status (below 185/110 before lysis, below 180/105 after; permissive up to ~220/120 if untreated). Saying lower BP always helps is false and dangerous. Both penumbral survival and hemorrhagic transformation are tightly linked to blood pressure, so the other options are incorrect.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency with acute ischemic stroke
pearls: Always get a non-contrast head CT first, BP targets are nearly opposite for ischemic stroke versus intracerebral hemorrhage.
Before IV thrombolysis, lower BP to below 185/110 mmHg, then keep it below 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours to limit hemorrhagic transformation.
Without reperfusion therapy, practice permissive hypertension: treat only if BP exceeds ~220/120 mmHg (or another indication) and lower it modestly (~15%).
The penumbra is pressure-dependent; over-aggressive BP lowering extends the infarct, while under-control raises bleeding risk, especially after reperfusion.

cases.title: Sudden Right Arm Weakness and Slurred Speech After a Recent Hospital Stay
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset right arm weakness and slurred speech that began 90 minutes ago.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is brought to the ED after his daughter noticed him struggling to lift his coffee cup with the right hand and slurring his words at 09:00. He was last seen normal at 08:30. There was no headache, seizure, syncope, or trauma. Notably, he was discharged 9 days ago after an admission for an upper GI bleed from a bleeding gastric ulcer; he required 2 units of packed red cells and underwent endoscopic clipping, with a Helicobacter pylori-positive biopsy. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications are pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and clarithromycin-amoxicillin triple therapy started at discharge. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. He has had no recent surgery other than the endoscopy, no recent head trauma, and no prior intracranial hemorrhage.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented with mild dysarthria but fluent, comprehensible speech. Mild right central facial droop. Right pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the right arm; right leg 5/5. Sensation intact. No neglect or visual field cut. No aphasia. Reflexes symmetric, right plantar equivocal. No neck stiffness. Abdomen soft, nontender, no melena on rectal exam, stool guaiac negative. NIHSS calculated at 4. Fingerstick glucose 138 mg/dL.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step after stabilization?
options: Lumbar puncture to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage
Non-contrast head CT immediately
MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging first
CT angiography of the head and neck before any non-contrast imaging
Repeat upper endoscopy to assess the prior ulcer
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the first step in any suspected acute stroke because it rapidly excludes intracranial hemorrhage, the critical branch point before thrombolysis. MRI is more sensitive for early ischemia but is slower and should not delay the hemorrhage screen in a thrombolysis candidate. Repeat endoscopy is not indicated acutely and would dangerously delay stroke evaluation; he has no signs of active rebleeding. CT angiography is useful to identify large-vessel occlusion but follows (or accompanies) the non-contrast CT, not replaces it. Lumbar puncture is for thunderclap headache with negative CT suspicious for SAH, which this is not; it is also contraindicated before excluding a mass effect and would delay care.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: no hemorrhage, no established infarct, ASPECTS 10
CT angiography: patent intracranial vessels, no large-vessel occlusion
Glucose: 138 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (was 9.8 at discharge)
Platelets: 232 ×10³/µL
INR: 1.1
aPTT: 31 s
Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR 44)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He is now 100 minutes from onset, NIHSS 4, with no hemorrhage on CT. Regarding IV thrombolysis (alteplase/tenecteplase), which feature of his history is the key contraindication to assess?
options: His age of 70 years in this clinical context
His chronic kidney disease stage 3
His recent course of antibiotics
His blood pressure of 176/94 mmHg
His gastrointestinal hemorrhage 9 days ago
stages.explanation: A gastrointestinal or urinary hemorrhage within the prior 21 days is a contraindication to IV thrombolysis because of the high risk of re-bleeding; his GI bleed 9 days ago falls squarely within this window and is the pivotal exclusion to weigh. CKD itself is not a thrombolysis contraindication (though it affects contrast and outcomes). Age 70 is not a contraindication; alteplase is given regardless of advanced age when otherwise eligible. A BP of 176/94 is below the 185/110 threshold and is manageable, not an absolute exclusion. Recent antibiotics are irrelevant to bleeding risk. Recognizing the recent GI bleed as a relative/absolute contraindication is the central teaching point here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his GI bleed 9 days ago, stable hemoglobin, NIHSS 4, and no large-vessel occlusion, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Withhold IV thrombolysis
Administer full-dose IV alteplase since the bleed was endoscopically treated as initial therapy
Give therapeutic heparin infusion for the acute stroke as initial therapy
Proceed directly to mechanical thrombectomy as initial therapy
Administer half-dose alteplase as a compromise as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Recent GI hemorrhage within 21 days is a contraindication to IV thrombolysis; with a minor, non-disabling deficit (NIHSS 4) and no large-vessel occlusion, the safest evidence-based path is to withhold lytics and start antiplatelet therapy, with dual antiplatelet (aspirin plus clopidogrel) considered for minor stroke per CHANCE/POINT data. Full-dose alteplase ignores the bleeding contraindication. Thrombectomy is only for large-vessel occlusion, which CTA excluded. Therapeutic heparin is not used in acute ischemic stroke and increases hemorrhage risk. Half-dose alteplase is not a validated strategy and still carries bleeding risk in a patient with a recent GI bleed. Antiplatelet therapy reduces recurrence with acceptable bleeding risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After starting antiplatelet therapy, what is the most appropriate next step in his secondary-prevention workup?
options: Admit to a stroke unit with telemetry, carotid imaging, echocardiography,
Discharge home with outpatient follow-up only with appropriate follow-up
Schedule elective endoscopy before any further stroke workup in this clinical context
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation with a DOAC immediately based on current risk
Start warfarin empirically for presumed cardioembolism with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: He should be admitted to a stroke unit with continuous telemetry to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, carotid imaging (he had no LVO but extracranial stenosis must be assessed), transthoracic echocardiography for a cardioembolic source, and a high-intensity statin for secondary prevention. Discharge home is unsafe before an etiologic workup. Empiric warfarin or a DOAC is inappropriate without a documented anticoagulation indication such as AF, and anticoagulation is especially hazardous given his very recent GI bleed. Endoscopy is not the priority over completing the stroke evaluation; his stool was guaiac-negative and hemoglobin stable, so there is no evidence of active rebleeding requiring urgent scoping.

cases.diagnosis: Acute minor ischemic stroke (left hemisphere) with IV thrombolysis contraindicated by recent gastrointestinal hemorrhage; managed with antiplatelet therapy and secondary-prevention workup
pearls: GI or GU hemorrhage within 21 days is a contraindication to IV thrombolysis due to high re-bleeding risk.
Non-disabling minor strokes (NIHSS 0-5) without LVO may be managed with antiplatelet therapy, with DAPT considered per CHANCE/POINT.
Non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage is always the first imaging step before thrombolysis decisions.
Advanced age, CKD, and BP <185/110 are not contraindications to thrombolysis.

cases.title: Sudden Confusion and Left-Sided Weakness in a Diabetic Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute confusion, sweating, and left arm weakness for the past 30 minutes.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her husband after she became suddenly confused, diaphoretic, and unable to use her left arm while preparing dinner. He says she was completely normal 30 minutes earlier. There was no headache, seizure, chest pain, or trauma. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes for 15 years, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Her medications include insulin glargine 24 units at night and insulin aspart with meals, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. She recently started a new exercise program and has been eating less, and her husband notes she may have taken her usual mealtime insulin but then skipped lunch today. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history includes a mother with type 2 diabetes and a brother with stroke at age 60. There is no recent surgery, bleeding, or anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: Drowsy and confused, oriented only to person, with cool diaphoretic skin and a fine tremor. Left facial droop and left arm drift with 3/5 strength; left leg 4/5. Speech mildly slurred. Pupils equal and reactive. No obvious visual field cut. Reflexes symmetric. No neck stiffness. No tongue laceration or incontinence. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. NIHSS estimated at 6 during the episode.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before activating the full stroke pathway, what immediate bedside test is mandatory?
options: Serum troponin in this clinical context
Non-contrast head CT
Bedside ultrasound of the carotids
12-lead ECG as the first step today
Fingerstick blood glucose
stages.explanation: A point-of-care glucose is mandatory in every patient with acute focal neurologic deficits because hypoglycemia is a common, instantly reversible stroke mimic and is a required check before thrombolysis. Her diaphoresis, tremor, tachycardia, confusion, and recent insulin-with-skipped-meal history strongly suggest hypoglycemia. A head CT is essential in the stroke pathway but should not precede the rapid glucose check, which can immediately change management. An ECG and troponin assess cardiac causes but do not explain a focal deficit and are not the first step. Carotid ultrasound has no role in the hyperacute bedside assessment. Checking glucose first can spare an unnecessary thrombolysis decision.

stages.label: POINT-OF-CARE TESTING
stages.content:
Fingerstick glucose: 38 mg/dL
Non-contrast head CT (obtained in parallel): no hemorrhage, no acute infarct
ECG: sinus tachycardia, rate 108, no ischemic changes
Serum glucose (venous): 41 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her fingerstick glucose is 38 mg/dL with focal deficits. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Administer oral glucose tablets only as initial therapy
Obtain MRI brain before any treatment as initial therapy
Give 25 g of IV dextrose
Start an insulin drip to stabilize her glucose as monotherapy
Administer IV alteplase for acute stroke as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The treatment is immediate IV dextrose (25 g of D50) followed by rechecking glucose and the neurologic exam; hypoglycemia can produce focal deficits that resolve completely with glucose correction. IV alteplase is contraindicated and unnecessary because hypoglycemia is a reversible mimic, not ischemia. An insulin drip would worsen hypoglycemia and is dangerous. MRI would delay life-saving glucose repletion. Oral glucose is inappropriate when the patient is drowsy and confused with aspiration risk; the IV route is preferred. After dextrose, observe for recurrence given her long-acting insulin, and provide carbohydrate once she can safely eat.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ten minutes after IV dextrose her glucose is 122 mg/dL and her left-sided weakness and confusion have completely resolved with a normal neurologic exam. What is the diagnosis?
options: Hemiplegic migraine as the primary diagnosis
Hypoglycemia-induced stroke mimic
Todd paralysis after an unwitnessed seizure
Transient ischemic attack in this clinical context
Acute lacunar stroke as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Complete resolution of focal deficits immediately after correcting a documented glucose of 38 mg/dL confirms a hypoglycemia-induced stroke mimic from focal neuroglycopenia. A TIA is a diagnosis of exclusion not supported here because the deficit tracked directly with glucose correction, not spontaneous resolution. A lacunar stroke would not reverse instantly with dextrose and would show evolving deficits. Todd paralysis follows a witnessed or suspected seizure with postictal features, absent here. Hemiplegic migraine occurs in younger patients with headache and a known migraine history and does not resolve with glucose. The temporal link to glucose is diagnostic.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After full recovery, what is the most appropriate disposition and prevention strategy?
options: Start aspirin and clopidogrel for stroke prevention with appropriate follow-up
Observe for several hours given long-acting insulin, ensure carbohydrate intake, review insulin dosing
Initiate anticoagulation pending an embolic workup with appropriate follow-up
Discharge immediately since she is now asymptomatic with appropriate follow-up
Admit to the ICU for an insulin drip with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Because she received long-acting insulin glargine and skipped a meal, hypoglycemia can recur for many hours, so she should be observed, given carbohydrates, and have her insulin regimen and diabetes self-management reviewed before discharge. Immediate discharge risks rebound hypoglycemia from the still-active glargine. An insulin drip would be harmful in a hypoglycemic patient. Antiplatelet therapy is unwarranted because she did not have an ischemic event. Anticoagulation and an embolic workup are unnecessary because the cause was metabolic, not vascular. Addressing the precipitant (insulin-meal mismatch with new exercise and reduced intake) prevents recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Hypoglycemia presenting as a stroke mimic (focal neuroglycopenia) with complete resolution after dextrose
pearls: Always check a point-of-care glucose before activating thrombolysis; hypoglycemia is a classic reversible stroke mimic.
Focal deficits from hypoglycemia resolve promptly after IV dextrose, distinguishing them from true ischemia.
Long-acting insulin can cause prolonged or recurrent hypoglycemia, mandating observation and carbohydrate repletion.
Diaphoresis, tremor, and tachycardia accompanying focal deficits should raise immediate suspicion for hypoglycemia.

cases.title: Found Down With Right-Sided Weakness and a Bitten Tongue
cases.chiefComplaint: Right-sided weakness and confusion after being found unresponsive at home.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man is brought in by EMS after his roommate found him on the bedroom floor, briefly unresponsive, then drowsy and confused with right-sided weakness. The roommate last saw him normal two hours earlier and did not witness the event. Bystanders report no clear time of onset for the weakness. He has a history of alcohol use disorder with prior withdrawal seizures and a remote traumatic brain injury, plus hypertension. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and thiamine; he is not on antiepileptics and admits to recently stopping drinking after a heavy binge. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes one pack daily and drinks heavily. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he is postictal-appearing and slowly improving. There is a lateral tongue laceration and he was incontinent of urine. No anticoagulant use and no recent surgery or known intracranial hemorrhage.
cases.exam: Drowsy, slowly arousable, oriented to person only, gradually improving over minutes. Right facial weakness and right arm drift with 4/5 strength, right leg 4/5; deficits appear to be lessening on serial exams. Lateral tongue laceration noted. Speech mildly dysarthric. Pupils equal and reactive, no gaze deviation. No neck stiffness. Urinary incontinence noted. Reflexes symmetric. Fingerstick glucose 96 mg/dL. NIHSS approximately 5 and decreasing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests a seizure with postictal deficit rather than primary acute ischemic stroke?
options: His history of hypertension as the first step today
His blood pressure of 162/95 mmHg as the first step today
The lateral tongue laceration, urinary incontinence, postictal confusion,
His right facial weakness as the first step today
His low-grade temperature of 37.4 °C as the first step today
stages.explanation: Lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, postictal confusion, and a focal deficit that is improving over minutes are classic for a seizure with Todd (postictal) paralysis rather than acute ischemia, which typically produces a fixed or worsening deficit. Hypertension is common in both stroke and post-seizure states and is nonspecific. Right facial weakness localizes a deficit but does not distinguish seizure from stroke. A chronic history of hypertension is a vascular risk factor but does not point to the acute mechanism. A mildly elevated temperature is nonspecific. The constellation of postictal features plus an unwitnessed loss of consciousness is the key clue.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: no hemorrhage, no acute infarct; old encephalomalacia in the left frontal region
CT angiography: no large-vessel occlusion
Glucose: 96 mg/dL
Sodium: 131 mEq/L
Magnesium: 1.4 mg/dL
Ethanol level: undetectable
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL
Platelets: 178 ×10³/µL, INR 1.1

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the CT findings and clinical course, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute left MCA ischemic stroke as the primary diagnosis
Todd paralysis after an unwitnessed seizure, likely alcohol-withdrawal related
Intracerebral hemorrhage as the primary diagnosis
Transient ischemic attack as the primary diagnosis
Hepatic encephalopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Todd paralysis after an unwitnessed seizure best fits: he has postictal confusion, a tongue laceration, incontinence, an improving focal deficit, old left frontal encephalomalacia (an epileptogenic scar), and alcohol-withdrawal risk with low magnesium and recent cessation. The deficit lateralizes opposite the old cortical injury. Acute MCA stroke is less likely because CT shows no infarct or occlusion and the deficit is improving. Hemorrhage is excluded by CT. A TIA does not cause postictal confusion, tongue biting, or incontinence. Hepatic encephalopathy causes diffuse encephalopathy and asterixis, not a focal hemiparesis with seizure markers. Todd paralysis typically resolves within hours.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering the likely seizure etiology, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Administer IV alteplase for possible stroke as initial therapy
Proceed to emergent mechanical thrombectomy as initial therapy
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Start lifelong phenytoin immediately and discharge as initial therapy
Treat the withdrawal/seizure precipitants , monitor the deficit,
stages.explanation: Management targets the seizure precipitants: benzodiazepines for ongoing alcohol-withdrawal seizures, magnesium and thiamine repletion, fluids, and serial neurologic monitoring, with MRI to exclude infarct and EEG if seizures recur. IV alteplase is contraindicated when seizure with postictal deficit is the likely cause and would expose him to hemorrhage risk without benefit, particularly without confirmed ischemia. Immediate lifelong phenytoin with discharge is inappropriate for a provoked withdrawal seizure; provoked seizures are treated by removing the provocation, and benzodiazepines (not phenytoin) are first-line in alcohol withdrawal. Anticoagulation and thrombectomy treat ischemic stroke, which is not supported here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His weakness fully resolves over 4 hours. What is the best disposition plan?
options: Discharge with no follow-up since he recovered with appropriate follow-up
Start dual antiplatelet therapy for secondary stroke prevention with appropriate follow-up
Transfer for emergent carotid endarterectomy with appropriate follow-up
Admit for alcohol-withdrawal monitoring , electrolyte correction,
Discharge on lifelong antiepileptics with no further workup with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: He should be admitted for monitored alcohol-withdrawal management (CIWA-guided benzodiazepines), correction of magnesium and other electrolytes, thiamine, and arrangement of outpatient neurology follow-up with MRI to characterize the cortical scar. Discharge without follow-up risks recurrent withdrawal seizures and misses an opportunity for addiction treatment. Lifelong antiepileptics are generally not indicated for an isolated provoked withdrawal seizure. Carotid endarterectomy treats symptomatic carotid stenosis, not seizure. Dual antiplatelet therapy is for ischemic stroke/TIA prevention, which does not apply because the event was a seizure with Todd paralysis, not ischemia.

cases.diagnosis: Todd (postictal) paralysis following an unwitnessed alcohol-withdrawal seizure, mimicking acute stroke
pearls: Tongue biting (lateral), urinary incontinence, and postictal confusion point to seizure rather than primary stroke.
Todd paralysis is a postictal focal deficit that typically improves over minutes to hours, unlike a fixed ischemic deficit.
An old cortical lesion (encephalomalacia) can be an epileptogenic focus and lateralizes the postictal weakness.
Alcohol-withdrawal seizures are provoked; treat with benzodiazepines and electrolyte repletion, not empiric lifelong antiepileptics.

cases.title: Young Woman With Headache, Visual Sparkles, and Arm Heaviness
cases.chiefComplaint: Throbbing headache with right arm and face weakness that developed over about 30 minutes.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman presents with a right-sided throbbing headache preceded by 20 minutes of shimmering zigzag lines in her vision, followed by gradually spreading numbness and weakness of her right hand, then arm, then the right side of her face over roughly half an hour. She also describes mild word-finding difficulty during the episode. She has had several similar but milder episodes since adolescence and a sister with comparable spells. Past medical history is notable for migraine with aura since age 16. Medications include a combined oral contraceptive pill and as-needed ibuprofen; she has no known drug allergies. She is a graduate student, does not smoke, drinks occasional wine, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is significant for a mother and sister with migraine, one of whom has had stereotyped hemiplegic spells. There is no recent trauma, fever, neck pain, or anticoagulant use, and no prior intracranial hemorrhage.
cases.exam: Alert, uncomfortable from headache, mildly photophobic. Right hand and arm weakness 4/5 with right facial droop; mild right-sided sensory changes and intermittent word-finding pauses. The motor symptoms reportedly marched from hand to face over minutes. No neck stiffness, no fever. Pupils equal and reactive, no papilledema on fundoscopy. Gait cautious but symmetric. Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor. Fingerstick glucose 94 mg/dL. NIHSS approximately 4.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Despite a suggestive migraine history, what must be done first in this young patient with acute focal deficits?
options: Obtain an outpatient MRI in 1-2 weeks as the first step today
Reassure and discharge with migraine abortive therapy as the first step today
Perform a lumbar puncture before any imaging as the first step today
Give IV alteplase empirically as the first step today
Activate the acute stroke pathway with urgent non-contrast head CT
stages.explanation: Even with a strong migraine history, acute focal deficits in a young patient require activating the stroke pathway with urgent non-contrast head CT and vascular imaging because stroke in the young (dissection, cardioembolism, vasculitis) must be excluded before attributing symptoms to migraine. Reassurance and discharge without imaging is unsafe. Outpatient MRI in 1-2 weeks misses time-critical pathology. Empiric alteplase is premature before excluding hemorrhage, mimics, and confirming ischemia; migraine with aura is a recognized stroke mimic and a relative caution for lytics. Lumbar puncture is not the first step and is contraindicated before imaging excludes mass effect; it is reserved for suspected SAH or meningitis.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: normal, no hemorrhage
CT angiography head/neck: no occlusion, no dissection, no aneurysm
MRI brain with DWI: no acute infarct, no diffusion restriction
Glucose: 94 mg/dL
CBC, electrolytes, coagulation studies: within normal limits
Pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging is normal and her deficits resolve over the next two hours alongside the headache. Given the marching aura, prior stereotyped spells, and family history, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Transient ischemic attack
Hemiplegic (complicated) migraine
Acute ischemic stroke with spontaneous recanalization
Functional neurologic disorder
Focal seizure with Todd paralysis
stages.explanation: Hemiplegic migraine best fits: a young woman with prior stereotyped spells, a positive family history, a slowly marching aura (visual then sensory then motor over minutes), accompanying throbbing headache, and completely normal CT/CTA/MRI with no diffusion restriction. Ischemic stroke is excluded by a normal DWI-MRI. TIA symptoms are typically negative phenomena with abrupt onset and lack the spreading aura and headache; deficits also usually do not march over minutes. Seizure with Todd paralysis would show postictal features and often positive motor phenomena, not a visual scintillating aura. Functional disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not explain the classic spreading aura with cortical spreading depression physiology.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute management and counseling for hemiplegic migraine?
options: Start IV alteplase since symptoms could still be ischemic as initial therapy
Administer a triptan or ergotamine immediately as initial therapy
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for stroke prevention as initial therapy
Provide supportive abortive therapy , avoid triptans/ergots during the hemiplegic attack,
Initiate high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
