stages.explanation: Acute hemiplegic migraine is treated with supportive abortive measures (antiemetics, NSAIDs, IV fluids), while triptans and ergots are traditionally avoided because of their vasoconstrictive properties during a hemiplegic attack. Critically, migraine WITH AURA plus a combined oral contraceptive markedly raises ischemic stroke risk, so the estrogen-containing pill should be stopped and a non-estrogen contraceptive chosen. Alteplase is inappropriate once imaging excludes ischemia and the picture is classic migraine. Anticoagulation does not treat migraine and adds bleeding risk. Corticosteroids are not standard therapy. Counseling on aura plus estrogen is the high-yield prevention point.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term recommendation is most appropriate for this patient?
options: No follow-up needed since imaging was normal with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong daily aspirin and clopidogrel with appropriate follow-up
Permanent driving restriction with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue estrogen-containing contraception, optimize vascular risk factors,
Empiric warfarin therapy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The key long-term steps are discontinuing estrogen-containing contraception (migraine with aura plus estrogen multiplies stroke risk), optimizing modifiable vascular risk factors, and referring to neurology for migraine prophylaxis if attacks are frequent or disabling. Dual antiplatelet therapy is not indicated for migraine and carries bleeding risk without proven benefit here. A permanent driving restriction is not warranted by a single resolved migraine. Empiric warfarin is inappropriate without an anticoagulation indication. Skipping follow-up is unwise because hemiplegic migraine warrants counseling, prophylaxis consideration, and reinforcement of contraceptive and smoking-avoidance advice to lower future stroke risk.

cases.diagnosis: Hemiplegic (complicated) migraine with aura, a stroke mimic with normal diffusion-weighted imaging
pearls: Hemiplegic migraine features a slowly marching aura (visual to sensory to motor) over minutes with headache and normal DWI.
Acute focal deficits in the young still require urgent imaging to exclude dissection, cardioembolism, and true stroke.
Migraine with aura plus estrogen-containing contraception substantially increases ischemic stroke risk; stop the estrogen.
Avoid triptans and ergots during a hemiplegic attack because of their vasoconstrictive risk.

cases.title: Sudden Whole-Body Weakness With an Unusual Exam
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden left-sided weakness and inability to walk that began an hour ago.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents with the sudden onset of left arm and leg weakness and difficulty walking that began about an hour ago after an argument at home. She reports the limbs feel heavy and unresponsive. There is no headache, no speech difficulty, no facial droop she can identify, and no visual change. Past medical history includes anxiety, depression, fibromyalgia, and a prior ED visit for non-epileptic spells. She also reports chronic widespread pain and fatigue. Medications are sertraline 100 mg daily and as-needed gabapentin. She has no known drug allergies. She works as a medical assistant, does not smoke, drinks rarely, and uses no recreational drugs. She has a history of childhood trauma. Family history is unremarkable for stroke or clotting disorders. There is no recent surgery, trauma, anticoagulant use, or prior intracranial hemorrhage. She appears anxious and tearful but is otherwise interactive.
cases.exam: Alert, anxious, fully oriented with normal fluent speech and intact comprehension. Reports dense left arm and leg weakness, but tone is normal and there is give-way (collapsing) weakness on confrontation. Hoover sign is positive (involuntary extension of the 'paralyzed' left leg when testing the right leg against resistance). No facial asymmetry, no pronator drift when distracted, normal coordination of the left hand when reaching for objects. Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor bilaterally. Sensory exam splits exactly at the midline and varies with examination. Fingerstick glucose 98 mg/dL. NIHSS variable and inconsistent across exams.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Although the exam suggests a functional disorder, what is the appropriate initial approach in the hyperacute setting?
options: Administer IV alteplase given the dense hemiparesis as the first step today
Discharge with a psychiatric referral without imaging as the first step today
Activate the stroke pathway
Begin a benzodiazepine for presumed conversion symptoms before imaging as the first step today
Order an outpatient MRI for next week as the first step today
stages.explanation: Even when functional neurologic disorder is suspected, acute focal deficits warrant activating the stroke pathway with urgent non-contrast head CT and vascular imaging while carefully documenting examination signs; functional disorder is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive signs, not merely exclusion, and true stroke must not be missed. Discharging without imaging risks missing a genuine stroke. Empiric alteplase is hazardous because the deficits show positive functional signs (Hoover, give-way, distractibility) and lytics carry hemorrhage risk; functional disorder is a stroke mimic. An outpatient MRI delays time-critical evaluation. Starting a benzodiazepine before evaluation is inappropriate and does not treat functional disorder, which requires diagnosis and explanation.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: normal, no hemorrhage
CT angiography head/neck: normal, no occlusion or dissection
MRI brain with DWI: no acute infarct, no diffusion restriction
Glucose: 98 mg/dL
Electrolytes, CBC, coagulation studies: within normal limits
Urine toxicology: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which positive examination finding most strongly supports a functional rather than organic cause of her leg weakness?
options: Normal blood pressure as the primary diagnosis
A positive Hoover sign
A normal non-contrast head CT as the primary diagnosis
Normal fingerstick glucose as the primary diagnosis
Symmetric deep tendon reflexes as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A positive Hoover sign is a specific physical finding for functional leg weakness: hip extension in the 'paralyzed' leg is weak on direct testing but returns involuntarily when the patient flexes the contralateral hip against resistance, demonstrating intact motor pathways and inconsistency with organic paralysis. Symmetric reflexes are nonspecific and can occur in early stroke or functional disorder. Normal glucose and blood pressure do not address the mechanism of weakness. A normal CT excludes hemorrhage but cannot, by itself, distinguish functional from acute ischemic deficits because CT is often normal early in ischemic stroke. The diagnosis of functional disorder rests on positive signs like Hoover, give-way weakness, and distractibility.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After imaging is normal and multiple positive functional signs are documented, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Provide a clear, validating explanation of functional neurologic disorder, demonstrate the reversible signs,
Admit to the ICU for a heparin drip as initial therapy
Tell the patient nothing is wrong and discharge her abruptly as initial therapy
Start long-term anticoagulation as initial therapy
Administer IV alteplase to be safe as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Best practice is to give a clear, validating diagnosis of functional neurologic disorder, explaining that the problem is genuine and potentially reversible, demonstrating the positive signs (such as Hoover) to the patient, and referring for physiotherapy and psychological therapies including CBT. Telling her 'nothing is wrong' is dismissive, harmful, and worsens outcomes. Alteplase is contraindicated and dangerous when imaging and exam exclude ischemia and reveal functional signs. Anticoagulation has no role and adds bleeding risk. ICU heparin is inappropriate for a non-ischemic, non-thrombotic condition. A positive, empathetic explanation that the diagnosis is made by characteristic signs (not exclusion alone) improves recovery.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which statement best characterizes the prognosis and follow-up for functional neurologic disorder?
options: It is untreatable and permanent as the immediate next step
It is a genuine, often improvable condition
It mandates repeat thrombolysis at the next episode as the immediate next step
It always indicates malingering for secondary gain as the immediate next step
It requires lifelong anticoagulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Functional neurologic disorder is a genuine, often improvable condition; outcomes are better with a confident early diagnosis, multidisciplinary treatment (physiotherapy and psychotherapy such as CBT), and continuity of care. It is neither untreatable nor synonymous with malingering: malingering implies conscious feigning for external gain, whereas functional symptoms are not consciously produced. Lifelong anticoagulation has no role. Repeat thrombolysis at future episodes would be harmful; recognizing recurrent functional presentations prevents unnecessary and dangerous lytic exposure. Educating the patient and treatment team about the positive diagnosis reduces repeat ED visits and iatrogenic harm while supporting recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Functional neurologic disorder (functional limb weakness) distinguished from stroke by positive signs including Hoover sign
pearls: Functional neurologic disorder is diagnosed by positive signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility), not by exclusion alone.
Acute focal deficits still require urgent stroke imaging before attributing them to a functional cause.
A positive Hoover sign reveals intact motor pathways inconsistent with organic paralysis.
A validating explanation plus physiotherapy and psychotherapy improves outcomes; dismissing the patient worsens them.

cases.title: Inpatient Who Cannot Speak or Follow Commands After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Unable to speak or understand spoken commands two days after admission for stroke.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man is on the stroke ward two days after presenting with sudden right-sided weakness and an inability to communicate. He arrived outside the thrombolysis window and was managed medically. His family reports he was a retired teacher who was sharp and talkative before this event; now he neither speaks meaningfully nor seems to understand them. Past medical history includes atrial fibrillation (he had stopped his anticoagulant), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications now include apixaban (restarted per protocol), amlodipine 10 mg daily, and atorvastatin 80 mg daily; he has no known drug allergies. He is right-handed. He is a former pipe smoker and drank wine socially. Family history includes a brother with atrial fibrillation. There is no fever, no recent seizure, and no head trauma. The nursing staff notes he becomes frustrated and agitated when unable to communicate but follows no verbal or written commands.
cases.exam: Awake with eyes open, attentive to the examiner but unable to follow midline or limb commands. Speech output limited to a few stereotyped, nonsensical utterances; cannot name objects, repeat phrases, read, or write. Comprehension severely impaired. Dense right central facial droop, right arm plegia, right leg severe weakness. Right homonymous hemianopia and left gaze preference. Reflexes brisker on the right with an extensor right plantar. No neck stiffness. Pulse irregularly irregular. NIHSS approximately 20.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How would you best characterize his language deficit at the bedside?
options: Severely impaired comprehension
Fluent speech with impaired comprehension and intact repetition
Normal language with pure dysarthria as the first step today
Nonfluent, effortful speech with intact comprehension in this clinical context
Isolated impairment of repetition with preserved comprehension and fluency
stages.explanation: His deficit combines severely impaired comprehension and severely impaired production with poor repetition, which defines global aphasia. Fluent speech with impaired comprehension but intact repetition describes transcortical sensory aphasia, not this picture. Nonfluent, effortful speech with intact comprehension is Broca aphasia, but his comprehension is also impaired. Isolated repetition impairment with preserved fluency and comprehension describes conduction aphasia. Pure dysarthria is a motor speech (articulation) problem with normal language content, whereas he has a true language disorder affecting naming, reading, writing, comprehension, and repetition. The combination of all modalities impaired is the hallmark of global aphasia from a large dominant-hemisphere lesion.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: large left middle cerebral artery territory infarct involving frontal (Broca area) and temporoparietal (Wernicke area) cortex with deep extension
MR angiography: left M1 segment occlusion with distal reconstitution
ECG/telemetry: atrial fibrillation
Transthoracic echocardiogram: left atrial enlargement, no thrombus seen
Glucose: 110 mg/dL, INR 1.1 (on apixaban)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the MRI and exam, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Conduction aphasia from an arcuate fasciculus lesion
Aphasia due to a right MCA infarct as the primary diagnosis
Wernicke aphasia from an isolated left superior temporal infarct
Global aphasia from a large left MCA territory infarct
Broca aphasia from a focal left inferior frontal infarct
stages.explanation: A large left MCA infarct involving both Broca's (frontal) and Wernicke's (temporoparietal) areas produces global aphasia, with loss of comprehension, production, naming, repetition, reading, and writing, exactly as seen here, plus dense right hemiplegia and right hemianopia from the extensive territory. Isolated Broca aphasia spares comprehension, and isolated Wernicke aphasia spares fluency, neither of which matches. Conduction aphasia selectively impairs repetition with preserved comprehension and fluency. A right MCA infarct in a right-handed patient causes left-sided deficits and neglect, not aphasia, because language is left-lateralized. The combined anterior and posterior language-cortex involvement defines global aphasia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management priority on the ward for this patient?
options: Emergent decompressive hemicraniectomy regardless of clinical course as initial therapy
Early swallow screening before oral intake, aspiration precautions, speech-language therapy,
Discharge home with outpatient follow-up only as initial therapy
Immediate IV thrombolysis as initial therapy
Withhold all anticoagulation indefinitely as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Priorities are a swallow screen before oral intake (global aphasia with large MCA stroke carries high aspiration risk), aspiration precautions, early speech-language therapy, and secondary prevention with anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation once hemorrhagic transformation risk allows. Thrombolysis is well outside the window and not indicated days later. Discharge home is premature given his severe deficits and rehabilitation needs. Withholding anticoagulation indefinitely ignores his high cardioembolic recurrence risk from AF. Decompressive hemicraniectomy is considered only for malignant cerebral edema with clinical deterioration and raised intracranial pressure, not routinely; it is reserved for selected patients with large hemispheric infarcts who deteriorate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding his rehabilitation and prognosis?
options: Global aphasia from a large dominant MCA stroke carries a guarded language prognosis
He should avoid all communication attempts to prevent frustration with appropriate follow-up
Speech therapy is not useful in aphasia with appropriate follow-up
Cognitive recovery is impossible, so rehabilitation is futile with appropriate follow-up
Full language recovery is guaranteed within a week with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Global aphasia from a large dominant-hemisphere MCA infarct carries a guarded language prognosis, but intensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation, including speech-language therapy, can produce partial functional gains over months, and some patients evolve toward less severe aphasia types. Promising guaranteed full recovery in a week is inaccurate. Speech therapy is beneficial and a cornerstone of aphasia care. Discouraging communication attempts is counterproductive; supportive communication strategies and augmentative methods help. Declaring rehabilitation futile is wrong and harmful; meaningful improvements in function, mood, and safety occur with structured rehab, family education, and time, even if complete recovery is unlikely.

cases.diagnosis: Global aphasia from a large left (dominant) middle cerebral artery territory infarct
pearls: Global aphasia impairs comprehension, production, naming, repetition, reading, and writing from a large dominant MCA lesion.
It typically accompanies dense contralateral hemiplegia and hemianopia due to the extensive infarct territory.
Swallow screening before oral intake is essential in large MCA strokes to prevent aspiration.
Prognosis for language is guarded but multidisciplinary rehabilitation can yield partial gains over months.

cases.title: Frustrated Patient Struggling to Get Words Out After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Effortful, halting speech and frustration when trying to communicate after a stroke.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman is on the stroke ward recovering from a left-hemisphere stroke three days ago. Nurses report she clearly understands everything said to her, follows commands accurately, and points to what she wants, but speaks in short, effortful, telegraphic phrases and becomes visibly frustrated and tearful when she cannot produce the words she intends. She was admitted after sudden right arm weakness and speech difficulty noted by her husband; she presented beyond the lytic window. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Medications are aspirin 81 mg daily (started for secondary prevention), atorvastatin 80 mg daily, and lisinopril 20 mg daily; she has no known drug allergies. She is right-handed and a retired accountant. Family history includes a father with stroke. There is no fever, no seizure, and no head trauma. She is alert and cooperative and writes notes that are similarly nonfluent and agrammatic.
cases.exam: Alert, attentive, fully comprehending verbal and written commands. Speech is nonfluent, effortful, and agrammatic with reduced phrase length and frequent pauses; naming is impaired and repetition is poor. Comprehension is preserved. Mild right central facial droop and right arm weakness 4/5; right leg near normal. No neglect; right visual fields intact. She shows appropriate frustration and insight into her speech errors. Reflexes slightly brisker on the right. No neck stiffness. NIHSS approximately 6.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside profile best matches her language exam?
options: Fluent speech, impaired comprehension, poor repetition as the first step today
Nonfluent, effortful, agrammatic speech with preserved comprehension
Impaired comprehension and production with poor repetition in this clinical context
Normal fluency and comprehension with isolated repetition impairment
Normal language with pure articulation difficulty as the first step today
stages.explanation: Nonfluent, effortful, agrammatic speech with preserved comprehension and impaired repetition is the classic profile of Broca (expressive) aphasia, and her preserved insight and frustration are characteristic. Fluent speech with impaired comprehension and poor repetition describes Wernicke aphasia. Impaired comprehension AND production with poor repetition is global aphasia. Normal fluency and comprehension with isolated impaired repetition is conduction aphasia. Pure articulation difficulty without language content errors is dysarthria, not aphasia; she has true language output impairment with agrammatism and naming difficulty, plus written language that is similarly nonfluent, confirming a language rather than purely motor problem.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: infarct in the left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca area) and adjacent frontal operculum, sparing the temporal lobe
MR angiography: left superior division MCA branch occlusion
Carotid ultrasound: 40% left internal carotid stenosis
Telemetry: normal sinus rhythm, no atrial fibrillation
Glucose: 102 mg/dL, LDL 142 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the MRI localization and exam, what is the diagnosis?
options: Wernicke aphasia as the primary diagnosis
Broca aphasia from a left inferior frontal infarct
Conduction aphasia as the primary diagnosis
Transcortical motor aphasia with preserved repetition
Global aphasia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Broca aphasia from a left inferior frontal (Broca area) infarct fits her nonfluent, effortful, agrammatic speech with preserved comprehension, impaired repetition, and accompanying right-sided weakness from adjacent motor cortex involvement. Wernicke aphasia is fluent with impaired comprehension. Global aphasia impairs both comprehension and production, but her comprehension is intact. Conduction aphasia spares fluency and comprehension with isolated repetition deficits. Transcortical motor aphasia resembles Broca aphasia but characteristically spares repetition; her repetition is impaired, making Broca aphasia the better fit. The frontal lesion location and clinical pattern together confirm Broca aphasia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach for her aphasia and stroke?
options: Early speech-language therapy, communication support for her frustration,
No specific intervention since comprehension is intact as initial therapy
Immediate carotid endarterectomy for the 40% stenosis as initial therapy
Sedation to reduce her frustration as initial therapy
Start anticoagulation for cardioembolic prevention as initial therapy
stages.explanation: She needs early speech-language therapy, supportive communication strategies to address her frustration, and aggressive secondary prevention with antiplatelet therapy, a high-intensity statin (LDL 142 is above goal), and blood-pressure control. Doing nothing wastes the high-yield early rehabilitation window even though comprehension is intact. Sedation is inappropriate and would impair participation in therapy. Carotid endarterectomy is indicated for symptomatic stenosis of 70-99% (and selectively 50-69%), not for a 40% non-flow-limiting stenosis. Anticoagulation is not indicated because telemetry shows no atrial fibrillation and there is no cardioembolic source; antiplatelet therapy is the correct prevention strategy here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement best guides communication and prognosis counseling for Broca aphasia?
options: Her frustration indicates a primary psychiatric disorder with appropriate follow-up
Recovery is impossible once a frontal lesion is present with appropriate follow-up
Because comprehension is preserved, address her directly
Speech therapy offers no benefit in Broca aphasia with appropriate follow-up
She cannot understand, so speak only to the family with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Because comprehension is preserved in Broca aphasia, clinicians should speak directly to the patient, allow extra time for responses, and use supportive communication; many patients make meaningful functional gains with speech-language therapy, especially with relatively focal lesions. Speaking only to the family is disrespectful and inaccurate given intact comprehension. Speech therapy is beneficial and central to recovery. Her frustration is an understandable reaction to a genuine output deficit, not a primary psychiatric disorder, though mood should be monitored as post-stroke depression is common. Recovery is not impossible; focal Broca aphasia often improves more than global aphasia.

cases.diagnosis: Broca (expressive) aphasia from a left inferior frontal (Broca area) infarct
pearls: Broca aphasia is nonfluent and effortful with preserved comprehension and impaired repetition; patients retain insight and become frustrated.
It localizes to the dominant inferior frontal gyrus and often co-occurs with contralateral arm/face weakness.
Preserved comprehension means clinicians should address the patient directly and allow time to respond.
Symptomatic carotid endarterectomy is for 70-99% (and selected 50-69%) stenosis, not low-grade lesions.

cases.title: Talkative but Nonsensical Patient Mistaken for Being Confused
cases.chiefComplaint: Fluent but nonsensical speech and apparent confusion noticed on the ward.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man was admitted overnight after his wife noted he was 'talking strangely.' The covering team initially documented him as 'confused' or 'delirious.' He speaks in long, fluent, well-articulated sentences that are full of made-up words and substitutions and convey little meaning, and he does not reliably follow commands. He seems unaware that his speech is abnormal. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a remote myocardial infarction. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily; he has no known drug allergies. He is right-handed and a retired engineer. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history includes hypertension and stroke. There is no fever, no recent infection, no new sedating medications, and no prior dementia. The nurse is concerned because he keeps trying to leave and becomes upset when redirected.
cases.exam: Alert with fluent, well-articulated, copious speech containing paraphasic errors and neologisms with poor meaning. Comprehension markedly impaired; cannot follow one- or two-step commands or point to named objects. Repetition and naming impaired. No insight into the deficit. Motor exam without obvious limb weakness; possible right superior quadrantanopia on confrontation. No facial droop appreciated. No neck stiffness. Reflexes symmetric. Attention testing limited by comprehension. Fingerstick glucose 142 mg/dL. NIHSS reflects severe language impairment.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Why might this presentation be mislabeled as delirium, and what is the key step to clarify it?
options: It cannot be confused with delirium; no further assessment is needed as the first step today
It represents psychiatric mania and warrants antipsychotics as the first step today
Fluent paraphasic speech with poor comprehension can mimic confusion
It is dementia and requires only outpatient cognitive testing as the first step today
The patient is simply uncooperative and should be sedated as the first step today
stages.explanation: Fluent, paraphasic, neologistic speech with impaired comprehension and lack of insight can be mistaken for delirium or psychiatric illness, so a focused language exam (assessing fluency, comprehension, naming, repetition) plus stroke imaging is the key step to identify an aphasia. Dismissing the possibility risks missing an acute stroke. Sedation is harmful and obscures the exam. Mania is fluent but with intact comprehension and other manic features, which are absent. Dementia develops gradually and would not present as an acute, isolated, fluent aphasia overnight. Recognizing the abrupt onset and pure language pattern points to a focal lesion, not a global confusional state.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: subtle hypodensity in the left posterior temporal region
MRI brain with DWI: acute infarct in the left superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke area), extending to the temporoparietal junction
MR angiography: left inferior division MCA branch occlusion
Telemetry: occasional premature atrial contractions, no sustained AF yet
Glucose: 142 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the imaging and exam, what is the diagnosis?
options: Broca aphasia as the primary diagnosis
Conduction aphasia as the primary diagnosis
Delirium from infection as the primary diagnosis
Global aphasia as the primary diagnosis
Wernicke aphasia from a left superior temporal infarct
stages.explanation: Wernicke aphasia from a left superior temporal (Wernicke area) infarct fits fluent, paraphasic, neologistic speech with impaired comprehension and repetition and lack of insight, with little or no limb weakness because the lesion spares motor cortex; a superior quadrantanopia can occur from involvement of the temporal optic radiations (Meyer loop). Broca aphasia is nonfluent with preserved comprehension. Global aphasia is nonfluent with impaired comprehension. Delirium from infection would show fluctuating attention, often a clear precipitant, and not a pure fluent aphasia with focal infarct on MRI. Conduction aphasia spares comprehension with isolated repetition deficits, unlike this patient's impaired comprehension.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient on the ward?
options: Immediate carotid endarterectomy as initial therapy
Antipsychotics and physical restraints for presumed delirium as initial therapy
Speech-language therapy, a safe
Discharge once he is 'less agitated' as initial therapy
Empiric antibiotics for presumed encephalitis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management includes early speech-language therapy, a safe structured environment recognizing that agitation stems from impaired comprehension (he cannot understand instructions), prolonged cardiac monitoring to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation given his embolic-appearing branch occlusion and atrial ectopy, and standard secondary prevention. Antipsychotics and restraints treat a misdiagnosis of delirium and can worsen outcomes; the agitation is driven by a comprehension deficit, not psychosis. Carotid endarterectomy is not indicated without significant symptomatic stenosis. Discharge is premature given an acute stroke needing workup and rehab. Empiric antibiotics for encephalitis are unwarranted without fever, CSF, or imaging evidence of infection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How should the team best communicate with a patient who has Wernicke aphasia and impaired comprehension?
options: Use short phrases, gestures, visual cues,
Communicate only in writing, since reading is always preserved as the immediate next step
Speak loudly, assuming the problem is hearing as the immediate next step
Avoid all interaction to prevent agitation as the immediate next step
Give long, detailed verbal explanations as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: With impaired comprehension, the team should use short phrases, gestures, visual cues, and demonstrations, minimize environmental stress, and involve speech-language therapy to build functional communication. Long, detailed verbal explanations exceed his comprehension and increase frustration. Avoiding all interaction is isolating and unhelpful. Communicating only in writing fails because reading comprehension is typically also impaired in Wernicke aphasia (the deficit affects language broadly, not just auditory input). Speaking loudly assumes a hearing problem, but the deficit is central language comprehension, not peripheral hearing, so volume does not help and may add stress.

cases.diagnosis: Wernicke (receptive) aphasia from a left superior temporal (Wernicke area) infarct, initially mistaken for delirium
pearls: Wernicke aphasia is fluent with paraphasias/neologisms, impaired comprehension and repetition, and poor insight.
It can be mislabeled as delirium or psychosis; abrupt onset and a pure language pattern point to a focal stroke.
Lesions in the left superior temporal gyrus spare motor cortex, so limb weakness is often minimal.
Communicate using short phrases, gestures, and visual cues; written communication often fails because reading is also impaired.

cases.title: Trouble Writing, Calculating, and Telling Right From Left
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficulty writing, doing simple math, and recurrent confusion of right and left.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman comes to the neurology clinic two weeks after a brief hospitalization for a small stroke. She reports lingering trouble signing her name and writing checks, difficulty with simple arithmetic when balancing her budget, and frequently confusing her right and left when following directions. Her daughter adds that she cannot reliably point to her own fingers when named. There is no significant weakness, no language fluency problem, and her comprehension is intact. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily started after the stroke; she has no known drug allergies. She is right-handed and a retired bookkeeper, which makes the calculation difficulty especially distressing. She does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history includes a mother with stroke. There is no headache, seizure, fever, or trauma.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, with intact comprehension and repetition. Marked agraphia (impaired writing) and acalculia (cannot perform simple subtraction or multiplication). Finger agnosia: cannot name or identify individual fingers on command. Right-left disorientation: inconsistently identifies right versus left on herself and the examiner. No aphasia, no neglect, no hemiparesis; visual fields full. Cranial nerves intact. Coordination normal; gait normal. Reflexes symmetric. No neck stiffness. Mini-mental testing notable for the tetrad above with otherwise preserved cognition. Fingerstick glucose 118 mg/dL.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her exam shows a specific tetrad of deficits. Which four findings constitute this recognizable syndrome?
options: Hemiparesis, hemianopia, aphasia, neglect
Apathy, abulia, incontinence, gait apraxia
Agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia,
Aphasia, apraxia, agnosia, amnesia as the first step today
Ataxia, nystagmus, vertigo, dysarthria
stages.explanation: The tetrad of agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and right-left disorientation defines Gerstmann syndrome, which localizes to the dominant (usually left) inferior parietal lobule, particularly the angular gyrus. Aphasia, apraxia, agnosia, and amnesia are the 'four A's' associated with Alzheimer dementia, not this focal vascular syndrome. Hemiparesis, hemianopia, aphasia, and neglect describe a large MCA territory syndrome. Ataxia, nystagmus, vertigo, and dysarthria suggest a posterior fossa/brainstem-cerebellar process. Apathy, incontinence, and gait apraxia point to a frontal or normal-pressure-hydrocephalus picture. Recognizing the specific tetrad localizes the lesion precisely.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: small chronic-appearing infarct in the left inferior parietal lobule involving the angular gyrus
MR angiography: no large-vessel occlusion; mild atherosclerosis
Carotid ultrasound: 30% bilateral stenosis
Telemetry/Holter: no atrial fibrillation
HbA1c: 6.8%, LDL 96 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the tetrad and MRI, what is the diagnosis and lesion localization?
options: Wernicke aphasia from a temporal lesion as the primary diagnosis
Cerebellar stroke as the primary diagnosis
Balint syndrome from bilateral parieto-occipital lesions
Gerstmann syndrome from a dominant angular gyrus/inferior parietal lesion
Frontal lobe syndrome as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Gerstmann syndrome from a dominant left inferior parietal lobule/angular gyrus lesion fits the tetrad of agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and right-left disorientation with otherwise preserved cognition and confirmatory MRI. Balint syndrome arises from bilateral parieto-occipital lesions and features optic ataxia, simultanagnosia, and oculomotor apraxia, none of which are present. Wernicke aphasia is a fluent aphasia with impaired comprehension, but her comprehension and fluency are intact. Frontal lobe syndrome causes executive, behavioral, and gait changes, not this parietal tetrad. A cerebellar stroke causes ataxia and coordination deficits, which she lacks. The localization to the angular gyrus is the classic teaching point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management focus at this clinic visit?
options: Start anticoagulation empirically as initial therapy
Schedule urgent carotid endarterectomy as initial therapy
Begin high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Reassure that no treatment or follow-up is needed as initial therapy
Optimize secondary stroke prevention
stages.explanation: Management focuses on optimizing secondary stroke prevention with antiplatelet therapy, a statin titrated to LDL goal, and control of blood pressure and diabetes, plus referral to occupational therapy and cognitive rehabilitation to address the functional impact of agraphia, acalculia, and right-left disorientation. Empiric anticoagulation is not indicated without atrial fibrillation or another cardioembolic source. Carotid endarterectomy is not warranted for only 30% stenosis. Corticosteroids have no role in an ischemic parietal stroke. Reassuring that no treatment or follow-up is needed ignores her ongoing risk and disability; cognitive rehabilitation can meaningfully improve daily function for these specific deficits.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which statement about the prognosis and rehabilitation of Gerstmann syndrome is most accurate?
options: It is best treated by surgical resection of the angular gyrus as the immediate next step
The deficits are always permanent and untreatable as the immediate next step
It requires lifelong anticoagulation to prevent worsening as the immediate next step
It inevitably progresses to global dementia as the immediate next step
Cognitive rehabilitation
stages.explanation: Cognitive rehabilitation and compensatory strategies can improve daily function in Gerstmann syndrome, and some component deficits partially recover over time, especially after a small stroke. The deficits are not necessarily permanent or untreatable. The syndrome does not inevitably progress to global dementia; it is a focal cortical syndrome, although vascular risk-factor control reduces future events. Lifelong anticoagulation is not a treatment for the syndrome itself and is reserved for specific cardioembolic indications. Surgical resection of the angular gyrus is nonsensical and would worsen deficits. Education, occupational therapy, and targeted compensation (e.g., calculators, writing aids) are the appropriate approach.

cases.diagnosis: Gerstmann syndrome from a dominant (left) angular gyrus/inferior parietal lobule infarct
pearls: Gerstmann syndrome tetrad: agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and right-left disorientation.
It localizes to the dominant (usually left) inferior parietal lobule, classically the angular gyrus.
Comprehension, fluency, and strength are typically preserved, distinguishing it from aphasia and large MCA strokes.
Cognitive rehabilitation and compensatory strategies plus vascular risk-factor control are the mainstays of care.

cases.title: Unable to Reach for Objects or See the Whole Scene
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficulty reaching for objects and inability to perceive more than one thing at a time after a hypotensive event.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is on the ward after an episode of profound hypotension during a complicated cardiac surgery and a presumed watershed/embolic insult. He now misreaches when trying to grab his cup or shake hands, repeatedly knocking items over, and seems unable to take in a whole scene, noticing only one object at a time even though he denies blindness. He has difficulty voluntarily directing his gaze to objects in his periphery on request. He is alert and converses normally. Past medical history includes coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and atrial fibrillation. Medications include apixaban (held perioperatively, now resumed), metoprolol, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, and aspirin; he has no known drug allergies. He is right-handed, a retired carpenter, a former smoker, and drinks rarely. Family history includes coronary disease. There is no headache, seizure, or fever. His family is alarmed because he 'acts blind' yet insists he can see.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, fluent speech with intact comprehension. Optic ataxia: misreaches for visually presented targets despite normal limb strength and proprioception. Simultanagnosia: describes only individual elements of a scene/picture, unable to integrate the whole. Oculomotor apraxia: difficulty voluntarily shifting gaze to peripheral targets on command, though spontaneous eye movements occur. Visual acuity grossly intact when fixating a single object; no dense hemianopia, no neglect to one side specifically. No hemiparesis. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Reflexes symmetric. Fingerstick glucose 108 mg/dL.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His exam shows a triad of higher-order visual deficits. Which triad is this?
options: Hemianopia, neglect, and anosognosia
Agraphia, acalculia, and finger agnosia
Aphasia, apraxia, and agnosia
Optic ataxia, simultanagnosia,
Ataxia, nystagmus, and vertigo
stages.explanation: The triad of optic ataxia (impaired visually guided reaching), simultanagnosia (inability to perceive more than one object at a time), and oculomotor apraxia (difficulty voluntarily directing gaze) defines Balint syndrome, which localizes to bilateral parieto-occipital cortex. Hemianopia, neglect, and anosognosia describe a right parietal stroke syndrome with unilateral inattention, not the symmetric visual integration failure here. Agraphia, acalculia, and finger agnosia belong to Gerstmann syndrome. Ataxia, nystagmus, and vertigo indicate cerebellar/vestibular pathology. Aphasia, apraxia, and agnosia are a dementia-associated grouping. Recognizing this specific visual-spatial triad localizes the lesions to bilateral parieto-occipital regions, often after watershed/embolic injury.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain with DWI: bilateral acute infarcts in the parieto-occipital regions (watershed distribution between MCA and PCA territories)
MR angiography: patent major vessels; findings consistent with hypotensive/embolic injury
Echo: atrial fibrillation with left atrial enlargement; no definite thrombus
Glucose: 108 mg/dL, INR therapeutic on resumed apixaban

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the triad and bilateral parieto-occipital infarcts, what is the diagnosis?
options: Gerstmann syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Anton syndrome (cortical blindness with denial)
Balint syndrome from bilateral parieto-occipital infarcts
Bilateral homonymous hemianopia alone
Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome
stages.explanation: Balint syndrome from bilateral parieto-occipital (dorsal visual stream) infarcts explains the triad of optic ataxia, simultanagnosia, and oculomotor apraxia following a hypotensive/watershed and embolic insult, with preserved basic acuity for single objects. Gerstmann syndrome is a dominant inferior parietal tetrad without these visuomotor deficits. Anton syndrome is cortical blindness with confabulated denial of blindness, but this patient can see single objects and has the specific Balint triad rather than total cortical blindness. Isolated bilateral homonymous hemianopia would cause field loss without the integrative reaching and gaze deficits. PRES presents with headache, seizures, vision changes, and characteristic posterior vasogenic edema, not discrete watershed infarcts with the Balint triad.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and rehabilitation plan?
options: Discharge home unsupervised as initial therapy
Immediate bilateral occipital surgery as initial therapy
No intervention since acuity is intact as initial therapy
Occupational therapy
High-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management centers on occupational therapy and visual/cognitive rehabilitation to compensate for impaired visually guided reaching and scene perception, environmental safety adaptations (reducing clutter, organizing single objects, assistance with reaching tasks), and secondary prevention with anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation given his cardioembolic risk. Doing nothing because acuity is intact ignores his profound functional disability and safety risk. Surgery has no role in ischemic cortical injury. Corticosteroids are not indicated for ischemic stroke. Discharge home unsupervised is unsafe given his severe visuospatial impairment and fall/injury risk; he needs supervised rehabilitation and caregiver support before any disposition decision.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which counseling point about Balint syndrome is most accurate?
options: It is a higher-order visual-processing disorder
It always fully resolves within days as the immediate next step
It indicates the patient is feigning visual loss as the immediate next step
It reflects ocular blindness that glasses will correct as the immediate next step
It is psychiatric and requires antipsychotics as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Balint syndrome is a higher-order visual-processing disorder of the dorsal stream: basic visual acuity for a single fixated object can be preserved, but integrating a whole scene, voluntarily shifting gaze, and reaching accurately under visual guidance are impaired. Rehabilitation emphasizes compensatory strategies, environmental safety, and caregiver education. It is not ocular blindness correctable with glasses, since the eyes and primary acuity may be intact. It does not reliably resolve within days; recovery is variable and often incomplete. It is neither psychiatric nor feigned; the deficits are genuine and localize to bilateral parieto-occipital cortex. Understanding the dorsal-stream basis guides realistic, safety-focused care.

cases.diagnosis: Balint syndrome from bilateral parieto-occipital (watershed/embolic) infarcts
pearls: Balint syndrome triad: optic ataxia, simultanagnosia, and oculomotor apraxia from bilateral parieto-occipital lesions.
It often follows hypotensive watershed injury (e.g., cardiac arrest/surgery) or bilateral embolic infarcts.
Basic visual acuity for a single object can be preserved despite profound failure to integrate and act on scenes.
Management centers on occupational therapy, safety adaptations, and treating the underlying stroke etiology.

cases.title: Leaning Hard to the Weak Side During Rehab
cases.chiefComplaint: She actively resists the therapist's attempts to bring her upright, pushing toward her paralyzed left side.
cases.history: A 73-year-old right-handed woman is on the inpatient stroke unit, day 5 after a large right middle cerebral artery (MCA) ischemic infarct. The acute presentation was sudden left hemiplegia and left-sided neglect; she received IV thrombolysis but had no large-vessel occlusion amenable to thrombectomy. Her course has been complicated by difficulty participating in physical therapy: when seated or stood, she actively pushes with her stronger right arm and leg toward her hemiplegic left side and resists passive correction toward the midline, becoming more forceful when the therapist tries to straighten her. PMH: hypertension, atrial fibrillation (now on apixaban 5 mg BID), hyperlipidemia, and remote tobacco use. Other meds: atorvastatin 40 mg, metoprolol 25 mg BID. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives with her daughter, previously fully independent and ambulatory. Family history: father had stroke at 80. She is alert and follows simple commands but tends to ignore stimuli on the left. There is no fever, chest pain, or new headache, and she has had no falls since admission.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented to person and place. Left homonymous visual inattention and left hemispatial neglect. Left central facial droop, dense left hemiplegia (0/5 arm, 1/5 leg), with left hemisensory loss. When sitting, she tilts toward the left and actively pushes herself further left using the right limbs; she resists examiner correction toward midline and perceives "upright" as tilted to the right. No nystagmus, no dysmetria in the right limbs, no dysarthria. Right-sided strength and sensation intact. Reflexes brisker on the left with a left Babinski. No neck rigidity. Skin over the left greater trochanter is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding best characterizes this patient's impaired postural control during rehabilitation?
options: Orthostatic intolerance from autonomic failure on standing as the first step today
Active pushing toward the hemiparetic side with resistance to passive correction to midline
Vestibular vertigo with horizontal nystagmus and a directional fast phase
Ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia causing a tendency to fall toward the lesion
stages.explanation: Pusher syndrome (contraversive pushing) is defined by active use of the non-paretic limbs to push toward the hemiparetic side, with resistance to passive correction toward the true vertical—exactly what is described. Patients misperceive their subjective postural vertical as tilted, so "upright" feels tilted toward the unaffected side. Cerebellar ataxia (option B) produces ataxia and dysmetria toward the lesion but not active pushing or resistance to correction, and her right-limb coordination is intact. Vestibular vertigo (option C) causes nystagmus and spinning, absent here. Orthostatic intolerance (option D) causes presyncope/BP drop on standing, not lateral pushing—her BP is stable. Recognizing pushing is essential because it changes therapy strategy and prolongs rehabilitation.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Damage to which structure is most classically associated with this contraversive pushing behavior?
options: Dorsal column of the spinal cord
Anterior cerebellar vermis
Medial longitudinal fasciculus
Posterolateral thalamus
stages.explanation: Contraversive pushing is classically linked to lesions of the posterolateral thalamus, which is thought to process the perception of the body's orientation with respect to gravity (subjective postural vertical). Insular and postcentral cortex lesions are also implicated, consistent with a large right MCA-territory infarct. The medial longitudinal fasciculus (option B) lesion causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia, not pushing. The cerebellar vermis (option C) governs truncal balance/gait ataxia but not the misperception of vertical that drives active pushing. Dorsal columns (option D) carry proprioception/vibration from the limbs and their lesion causes sensory ataxia with a positive Romberg, not contraversive pushing. Localization matters because pushing reflects a disturbed internal model of upright, distinct from weakness or sensory loss alone.

stages.label: IMAGING REVIEW
stages.content:
MRI brain (DWI): Large completed infarct in the right MCA territory involving frontoparietal cortex, insula, and posterolateral thalamus
No hemorrhagic transformation
No midline shift
MR angiography: No residual large-vessel occlusion; right M1 patent
CT head (day 5): Evolving hypodensity matching MRI, no new bleed

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate way to objectively grade and track the severity of this disorder over time?
options: Mini-Mental State Examination as the immediate next step
Serial National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale totals
Modified Rankin Scale at discharge only in this setting
Scale for Contraversive Pushing
stages.explanation: The Scale for Contraversive Pushing (SCP) and the Burke Lateropulsion Scale are validated, pushing-specific instruments that quantify spontaneous posture, use of the non-paretic extremities to push, and resistance to passive correction across sitting and standing. Serial scores let the rehab team track recovery, which typically occurs over weeks to a few months. The modified Rankin Scale (option A) measures global disability, not pushing severity, and is not serial-friendly. The NIHSS (option B) captures stroke deficits but has no pushing item. The MMSE (option D) screens cognition, not postural control. Choosing a pushing-specific scale guides therapy intensity, sets realistic prognosis, and documents the gradual resolution that most patients achieve.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation strategy is most appropriate for retraining her postural orientation?
options: Strict bed rest until pushing resolves spontaneously as initial therapy
High-dose benzodiazepines to suppress the pushing reflex as initial therapy
Bilateral lower-extremity casting to prevent lateral movement as initial therapy
Use of visual vertical cues to recalibrate upright orientation
stages.explanation: Pushing reflects a corrupted internal sense of vertical while visual perception of vertical is preserved. Therapy therefore uses external visual references—mirrors, vertical door frames, taped lines, and explicit feedback—so the patient consciously realigns to true vertical, combined with task-specific sitting/standing practice. This is the evidence-supported core of management, and most patients improve over weeks to months. Bed rest (option A) causes deconditioning, contractures, and pressure injury without treating the misperception. Benzodiazepines (option C) sedate, increase fall and aspiration risk, and do not correct postural orientation. Bilateral casting (option D) immobilizes rather than retrains and risks skin breakdown. Active visual-feedback retraining addresses the underlying perceptual deficit and accelerates the typically favorable recovery.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What should the team tell the family about the expected course of this postural disorder?
options: It is purely psychological and will resolve once she is motivated as the immediate next step
It typically resolves over weeks to a few months with targeted rehabilitation, though it may slow recovery
It is usually permanent and precludes independent ambulation as the immediate next step
It indicates the stroke is extending and warrants repeat thrombolysis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Contraversive pushing is usually a transient phenomenon: with targeted rehabilitation most patients improve substantially over several weeks and many resolve by about 6 months, though pushing does delay achievement of rehab milestones and lengthens stay. It is not generally permanent (option A), so a guarded but realistic, hopeful prognosis is appropriate. It does not signify stroke extension (option C)—her imaging shows a completed, non-progressing infarct, and thrombolysis days later is contraindicated and dangerous. It is a neurologically based disorder of vertical perception, not psychological or volitional (option D); attributing it to motivation is incorrect and undermines care. Accurate counseling supports adherence to the rehabilitation plan that drives recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Pusher syndrome (contraversive pushing/lateropulsion) after right MCA-territory stroke involving the posterolateral thalamus
pearls: Pusher syndrome = active pushing toward the hemiparetic side using the non-paretic limbs, with resistance to passive correction toward true vertical.
It reflects a disturbed subjective postural vertical, classically with posterolateral thalamic (also insular/parietal) lesions; visual perception of vertical is preserved.
Use external visual vertical cues (mirrors, door frames) for retraining; grade with the Scale for Contraversive Pushing or Burke Lateropulsion Scale.
Prognosis is generally favorable, resolving over weeks to months, but pushing prolongs rehabilitation and delays ambulation milestones.

cases.title: New Twitching of the Arm Months After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: He had a witnessed episode of rhythmic jerking of his left hand that spread up the arm, lasting about ninety seconds.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is admitted after a witnessed seizure on the medical ward, where he was being treated for a urinary tract infection. Four months ago he had a right frontoparietal cortical ischemic stroke (cardioembolic, from atrial fibrillation) leaving mild residual left arm weakness. Today a nurse witnessed rhythmic clonic jerking that began in the left hand, marched up to the shoulder, and lasted roughly 90 seconds; he was confused for several minutes afterward but did not lose awareness at onset. No tongue biting; there was brief urinary incontinence. PMH: atrial fibrillation (on apixaban 5 mg BID), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior stroke. Meds: apixaban, lisinopril 20 mg, metformin 1000 mg BID, atorvastatin 40 mg, plus ceftriaxone for the UTI. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: retired electrician, former smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He takes no recreational drugs. Between events he is at his neurologic baseline. He has had no prior seizures and no recent head trauma.
cases.exam: Postictally drowsy but rousable, oriented to name only initially, improving over 20 minutes to full orientation. Mild residual left pronator drift and 4+/5 left hand grip (baseline). No new focal deficit beyond baseline; no aphasia. Pupils equal and reactive. No neck stiffness, no photophobia. No tongue laceration; a small area of lateral tongue is intact. Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs. No focal abdominal tenderness; mild suprapubic discomfort. No rash. Reflexes symmetric except mildly brisk left biceps. Gait deferred acutely.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most accurate classification of the witnessed event?
options: Primary generalized tonic-clonic seizure as the first step today
Psychogenic nonepileptic event as the first step today
Focal motor seizure with a Jacksonian march
Convulsive syncope from a cardiac arrhythmia as the first step today
stages.explanation: The event began focally in the left hand with a Jacksonian march up the arm and progressed to postictal confusion—classic for a focal motor seizure arising from the contralateral (right) sensorimotor cortex, fitting his prior right frontoparietal cortical infarct. A primary generalized tonic-clonic seizure (option A) lacks a focal onset/march and begins with bilateral involvement and immediate loss of awareness. Psychogenic events (option C) are typically variable, with side-to-side movements, preserved awareness during bilateral activity, and no true postictal confusion; the stereotyped march and postictal state argue against this. Convulsive syncope (option D) follows loss of perfusion with brief myoclonic jerks and rapid recovery, not a 90-second focal march with prolonged confusion. Correct classification points to a structural cortical focus.

stages.label: DEFINITION
stages.question: Why is this best termed a late-onset (remote symptomatic) post-stroke seizure rather than an early/acute symptomatic seizure?
options: It occurred more than 7 days after the stroke, implying an established epileptogenic focus
It was generalized rather than focal in onset as the immediate next step
It occurred within 24 hours of the stroke as the immediate next step
It was provoked solely by the urinary tract infection as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: By convention, early (acute symptomatic) post-stroke seizures occur within 7 days of the stroke and reflect transient cortical irritability, whereas late (remote symptomatic) seizures occur after 7 days and reflect a stable, gliotic epileptogenic focus—this man is 4 months out, so it is late-onset. Option 1 describes the early window, which does not apply. The UTI/fever (option C) may lower seizure threshold but does not reclassify a remote symptomatic seizure into a purely provoked event in a patient with a known cortical scar. Option 3 is factually wrong—the seizure was focal in onset. This distinction matters because late-onset seizures carry a substantially higher recurrence risk, influencing the decision to start an antiseizure medication.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 137 mEq/L
Glucose 142 mg/dL
Ca 9.2 mg/dL
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Urinalysis: positive leukocyte esterase, many bacteria
CT head (non-contrast): Encephalomalacia in the right frontoparietal cortex; no acute hemorrhage or new infarct
EEG: Right centroparietal sharp waves; no ongoing electrographic seizures

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the seizure type, comorbidities, and current medications, which antiseizure medication is the most appropriate to start?
options: Phenytoin as initial therapy
Levetiracetam
Carbamazepine as initial therapy
Valproic acid as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Levetiracetam is preferred here: it has minimal cytochrome P450 interaction, so it will not undermine his apixaban, statin, or other medications, is effective for focal seizures, and is easy to titrate—making it a standard first choice after stroke. Phenytoin (option A) and carbamazepine (option B) are strong enzyme inducers that can lower anticoagulant and statin levels (a real hazard in this anticoagulated cardioembolic-stroke patient) and have less favorable side-effect/cognitive profiles. Valproic acid (option D) is an enzyme inhibitor with hepatotoxicity, weight gain, tremor, and platelet effects—less ideal in an anticoagulated, older patient. Lamotrigine is another reasonable non-inducing option but requires slow titration. Avoiding enzyme-interacting agents protects his stroke-prevention regimen while controlling seizures.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling and follow-up regarding recurrence and driving?
options: Start an antiseizure medication, counsel that late-onset post-stroke seizures have high recurrence risk,
Resume driving immediately since he is at baseline neurologically with appropriate follow-up
No medication or restrictions are needed because a single seizure never recurs with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue his anticoagulant to reduce hemorrhagic seizure risk with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Late-onset post-stroke seizures recur in a high proportion of patients (often cited above 50–70%), which justifies starting maintenance therapy after this first remote symptomatic seizure and counseling on recurrence. Patients must be advised not to drive, following the seizure-free interval required by local regulations, with neurology follow-up to monitor control and titration. Option 0 is wrong—recurrence is common in this setting. Resuming driving immediately (option C) endangers the patient and others and violates statutory seizure-free waiting periods. Stopping anticoagulation (option D) is dangerous: his seizure is from cortical scar, not hemorrhage (CT shows no bleed), and discontinuing apixaban would expose him to recurrent cardioembolic stroke. Appropriate counseling balances seizure control, stroke prevention, and public safety.

cases.diagnosis: Late-onset (remote symptomatic) focal post-stroke seizure following a right frontoparietal cortical infarct
pearls: Early (acute symptomatic) post-stroke seizures occur ≤7 days; late (remote symptomatic) seizures occur >7 days and carry a high recurrence risk warranting maintenance therapy.
Cortical (vs purely subcortical) infarcts and hemorrhagic strokes are the strongest risk factors for post-stroke epilepsy.
Prefer non-enzyme-inducing agents (levetiracetam, lamotrigine) to avoid lowering anticoagulant and statin levels in stroke patients.
A Jacksonian march and postictal confusion localize to a contralateral cortical focus; infection/fever can lower threshold but does not negate an established scar.

cases.title: Withdrawn and Tearful Since the Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Her family reports she has been persistently sad, unmotivated, and crying easily, and is no longer trying in therapy.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman returns to stroke clinic three months after a left MCA-territory ischemic stroke that left mild right-hand weakness and mild nonfluent aphasia, both improving. Her daughter reports that over the past two months she has become withdrawn, tearful, and apathetic; she has stopped doing her home exercises, sleeps poorly, has little appetite, and says she "isn't worth the trouble." She denies active suicidal plan but admits she sometimes feels life isn't worth living. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior stroke; no prior psychiatric diagnosis. Meds: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, lisinopril 20 mg. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, lives with her husband, formerly very socially active in her church; no alcohol or tobacco. Family history: mother had depression. She has not lost interest because of new neurologic decline—her motor and language deficits are stable or better. There are no symptoms of hypothyroidism reported, and she has not started any new sedating medications. Functionally, the change is most notable in her refusal to engage with rehabilitation.
cases.exam: Flat affect, poor eye contact, psychomotor slowing, tearful at times. Speech mildly nonfluent (baseline) but content coherent; no new word-finding regression. Mild right pronator drift and 4+/5 right grip, stable from prior visits. Cranial nerves intact, no new facial asymmetry. No tremor, no rigidity, normal tone. Gait steady without aid. No thyromegaly. She is oriented and attentive; cognition grossly intact aside from mild aphasia. No evidence of acute neurologic deterioration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for her tearfulness, apathy, and disengagement from rehabilitation?
options: Pseudobulbar affect alone
Post-stroke depression
Normal grief that requires no intervention
Recurrent stroke causing new deficits
stages.explanation: She meets criteria for a major depressive episode—persistent depressed mood, anhedonia/apathy, poor sleep and appetite, worthlessness, and passive death wishes—occurring after stroke, which defines post-stroke depression, the most common neuropsychiatric complication of stroke. Recurrent stroke (option A) is unlikely because her focal deficits are stable or improving, with no new findings on exam. Pseudobulbar affect (option C) involves involuntary, exaggerated crying/laughing incongruent with mood, but here the crying is congruent with pervasive low mood and is accompanied by full depressive symptoms—so PBA alone does not explain her presentation. Calling this normal grief (option D) and doing nothing is wrong and dangerous given functional decline and passive suicidality. Recognizing post-stroke depression is critical because it worsens rehabilitation outcomes and is treatable.

stages.label: SAFETY
stages.question: What is the most important immediate step before formulating a treatment plan?
options: Conduct a suicide risk assessment
Refer to physical therapy for more intensive exercises
Start a benzodiazepine for sleep
Order an MRI of the brain in this setting
stages.explanation: She has expressed passive suicidal ideation ("life isn't worth living"), so a focused suicide risk assessment—asking directly about ideation, intent, plan, access to means, and protective factors—takes priority over everything else and determines disposition and urgency. Imaging (option A) is not indicated without new neurologic signs and would not address the immediate safety concern. A benzodiazepine (option C) is inappropriate first-line: it can worsen depression and cognition, increase falls, and does not treat the underlying disorder. Intensifying physical therapy (option D) ignores the fact that untreated depression is the very reason she is not participating. Safety always comes first; once risk is assessed and addressed, treatment of the depression can proceed.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic treatment is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: An antipsychotic such as quetiapine as initial therapy
A tricyclic antidepressant such as amitriptyline
A psychostimulant such as methylphenidate as monotherapy
A selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor such as sertraline
stages.explanation: SSRIs (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram) are first-line for post-stroke depression—effective, generally well tolerated, and supported by guidelines, combined with psychotherapy and continued rehabilitation. A tricyclic (option B) carries anticholinergic, cardiac, orthostatic, and fall risks that are especially problematic in older stroke patients. An antipsychotic (option C) is not appropriate for uncomplicated depression and adds metabolic and extrapyramidal risk. A psychostimulant as monotherapy (option D) is not standard first-line treatment for post-stroke depression; while stimulants are sometimes considered for refractory apathy, they are not the initial choice and carry cardiovascular concerns. Starting an SSRI, with follow-up to monitor response and emergent suicidality, best supports both mood recovery and engagement in therapy.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Beyond medication, which combined approach best supports her recovery?
options: Psychotherapy plus continued structured rehabilitation
Discontinuing all stroke-prevention medications to reduce pill burden
Indefinite inpatient psychiatric admission regardless of risk level
Medication alone with no further follow-up as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Best practice combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy (such as cognitive behavioral therapy or problem-solving therapy), ongoing structured rehabilitation, caregiver support, and scheduled reassessment of mood and suicidality—an integrated model that improves both depressive symptoms and functional outcomes. Medication alone without follow-up (option A) misses the chance to monitor response, adjust dosing, and watch for emergent suicidality. Indefinite inpatient admission regardless of risk (option C) is excessive when she has only passive ideation without intent or plan; level of care should match assessed risk. Stopping stroke-prevention medications (option D) is harmful and unrelated to her depression, exposing her to recurrent stroke. A coordinated, multidisciplinary plan gives her the best chance to re-engage with rehabilitation.

cases.diagnosis: Post-stroke depression following a left MCA-territory infarct
pearls: Post-stroke depression is the most common neuropsychiatric complication of stroke and independently worsens rehabilitation and functional outcomes.
Always screen for and directly assess suicidal ideation; passive death wishes warrant a formal risk assessment.
SSRIs are first-line; avoid tricyclics in older stroke patients due to anticholinergic, cardiac, and fall risks.
Distinguish depression (mood-congruent crying with full depressive syndrome) from pseudobulbar affect (involuntary, mood-incongruent emotional outbursts).

cases.title: A Clenched, Painful Hand That Won't Open
cases.chiefComplaint: His left arm and hand have become increasingly stiff, painful, and curled inward since his stroke.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to rehabilitation clinic six months after a right MCA ischemic stroke with residual left hemiparesis. Over the past three months his left arm has become progressively stiff: the elbow, wrist, and fingers are increasingly flexed, hard to straighten, and painful, especially with passive stretch. Hygiene is difficult because the palm cannot be opened or cleaned, and the posture interferes with dressing. He has tried daily stretching with limited success. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior stroke, depression (in remission). Meds: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, amlodipine 5 mg, sertraline 50 mg. Allergies: none. Social: works part-time from home; nonsmoker; occasional alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He reports no fever, no skin breakdown, and no new weakness elsewhere. There is no red, hot, swollen joint to suggest septic arthritis, and no new sensory loss. The chief limitations are pain and the contracted posture impeding function and care.
cases.exam: Left upper limb held in adduction at the shoulder, flexion at the elbow, pronated forearm, flexed wrist, and clenched fingers. Increased tone with a velocity-dependent catch on passive elbow and wrist extension (Modified Ashworth 3). Hyperreflexia at the left biceps and brachioradialis with sustained clonus at the wrist; left Babinski. Strength: 2/5 in proximal left arm, 1/5 in hand. Skin in the palm intact but macerated-appearing. No joint erythema, warmth, or effusion. Right limbs normal. Sensation diminished but present in the left hand. Passive range of motion limited by tone and early contracture.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What best explains the increased tone with a velocity-dependent catch on passive stretch?
options: Parkinsonian rigidity (lead-pipe, velocity-independent)
Dystonia from a basal ganglia infarct
Paratonia (gegenhalten) as the first step today
Spasticity from an upper motor neuron lesion
stages.explanation: Spasticity is a velocity-dependent increase in tone with a catch (the "clasp-knife" phenomenon), accompanied by hyperreflexia, clonus, and an extensor plantar response—exactly this exam—reflecting loss of descending inhibition from his upper motor neuron (corticospinal) lesion. Parkinsonian rigidity (option A) is velocity-independent and uniform throughout the range (lead-pipe/cogwheel), not catch-like, and lacks hyperreflexia or Babinski. Paratonia (option C) is an involuntary variable resistance that fluctuates with the examiner's effort, typically in advanced dementia, without clonus or pyramidal signs. Dystonia (option D) produces sustained patterned twisting postures driven by co-contraction, not the velocity-dependent catch of spasticity. Correctly identifying spasticity directs treatment toward stretch, focal chemodenervation, and tone-reducing strategies.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which standardized tool is most appropriate to grade the severity of his increased tone?
options: Hunt and Hess grade
Glasgow Coma Scale in this setting
Modified Ashworth Scale
NIH Stroke Scale in this setting
stages.explanation: The Modified Ashworth Scale grades resistance to passive movement from 0 (no increase) to 4 (rigid in flexion/extension) and is the standard bedside measure for spasticity, allowing the team to track response to therapy. The Glasgow Coma Scale (option B) assesses level of consciousness, irrelevant here. The Hunt and Hess grade (option C) stratifies subarachnoid hemorrhage severity. The NIH Stroke Scale (option D) quantifies acute stroke deficits but does not specifically grade spasticity. Using a validated spasticity scale documents baseline severity, defines treatment goals, and measures the effect of interventions such as stretching, splinting, and botulinum toxin, which is central to managing his contracture.

stages.label: CLINIC ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Modified Ashworth Scale: Elbow flexors 3, wrist/finger flexors 3
Passive elbow extension limited to 40° short of full
Palmar skin intact but at risk for breakdown
Goniometry: progressive loss of range over 3 months
No radiographic evidence of heterotopic ossification

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this focal, painful upper-limb spasticity impairing hygiene, which is the most appropriate targeted treatment?
options: Long-term diazepam for sedation as initial therapy
Surgical tendon lengthening as the first intervention as initial therapy
Botulinum toxin injection into the spastic flexor muscles combined with physical/occupational therapy
High-dose oral baclofen as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For focal limb spasticity—especially when it is painful and impairs function or hygiene—intramuscular botulinum toxin into the targeted spastic muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum, flexor carpi, biceps) is first-line, and it works best combined with stretching, splinting, and occupational/physical therapy to maintain range and gains. High-dose oral baclofen as monotherapy (option A) acts diffusely, causes sedation and weakness, and is less suited to focal problems. Surgical tendon lengthening (option C) is reserved for fixed contractures refractory to conservative and chemodenervation therapy, not a first step. Long-term diazepam (option D) sedates, risks dependence and falls, and does not address the focal pattern. Botulinum toxin plus therapy directly relieves the targeted muscles and facilitates rehabilitation.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which adjunctive measure is most important to prevent a fixed contracture and skin breakdown while the toxin takes effect?
options: Application of heat alone without range-of-motion work in this setting
A daily stretching, splinting,
Complete avoidance of any movement to prevent pain in this setting
Strict immobilization of the limb in the flexed position
stages.explanation: A structured program of daily passive and active-assisted stretching, resting hand/wrist splints, proper limb positioning, and meticulous palmar skin and hygiene care is essential to maintain range of motion, prevent fixed contracture, and avoid maceration and pressure injury while botulinum toxin reaches effect over days to weeks. Immobilizing the limb in flexion (option A) accelerates contracture and skin breakdown—the opposite of the goal. Avoiding all movement (option C) leads to stiffness and contracture and does not even reliably reduce pain. Heat alone without range-of-motion work (option D) may transiently soothe but does not preserve joint mobility. Combining chemodenervation with active therapy and skin care gives the best functional and protective outcome.

cases.diagnosis: Post-stroke upper-limb spasticity with early flexor contracture
pearls: Spasticity is velocity-dependent (clasp-knife) with hyperreflexia, clonus, and extensor plantar—distinguishing it from velocity-independent parkinsonian rigidity.
Grade severity with the Modified Ashworth Scale to set goals and track therapy response.
Botulinum toxin is first-line for focal, function- or hygiene-limiting spasticity and works best combined with stretching, splinting, and therapy.
Reserve oral agents (baclofen, tizanidine) for diffuse spasticity and surgery for fixed contractures refractory to conservative care.

cases.title: Coughing With Every Sip After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: She coughs and her voice sounds wet whenever she tries to drink water on the stroke unit.
cases.history: An 80-year-old woman is on the stroke unit two days after an acute left hemispheric ischemic stroke presenting with right hemiparesis and slurred speech. The nursing staff note that she coughs when given sips of water and her voice sounds "gurgly" afterward. The team is asked to evaluate before she resumes eating and drinking. PMH: hypertension, atrial fibrillation, mild baseline cognitive impairment, and osteoporosis.
