Meds: apixaban 5 mg BID (held peri-stroke per protocol, plan to resume), amlodipine 10 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg. Allergies: none. Social: lives in assisted living, uses a walker, normally eats a regular diet without difficulty. Family history: noncontributory. She has no fever or productive cough at present and no chest pain. She is alert and cooperative but has dysarthria and a weak-sounding cough. Prior to this stroke she had no swallowing problems. The concern is safe initiation of oral intake without precipitating aspiration.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, dysarthric speech. Right central facial weakness and right pronator drift (4/5 arm). Wet, gurgly vocal quality after attempted sips; weak voluntary cough; delayed swallow with throat clearing. Gag reflex present but does not reliably predict swallow safety. Palate elevates symmetrically. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles. No accessory muscle use, no current respiratory distress. Right-sided hyperreflexia with a right Babinski. The remainder of the cranial nerve and sensory exam is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate action regarding her oral intake?
options: Keep her nil per os and perform a validated bedside swallow screen before any oral intake
Start a regular diet since she is alert and cooperative as the first step today
Place a nasogastric tube now without further assessment as the first step today
Give thickened liquids immediately without any screening as the first step today
stages.explanation: Every acute stroke patient should pass a validated dysphagia screen before receiving any food, fluids, or oral medications; until then she should remain NPO. She already shows red flags—coughing on sips, a wet voice, and a weak cough—so a formal bedside swallow screen (and likely a speech-language pathology evaluation) is the correct next step. Starting a regular diet (option A) risks aspiration despite her being alert—mental status alone does not ensure swallow safety. Empirically giving thickened liquids without screening (option C) skips the required assessment and may still be unsafe. Immediately placing a nasogastric tube (option D) is premature before a swallow evaluation defines whether and how she can take oral intake. Screening first prevents aspiration and its complications.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which finding most specifically raises concern for aspiration in this patient?
options: Wet/gurgly voice and cough after swallowing liquids
Symmetric palatal elevation in this setting
Presence of an intact gag reflex in this setting
Alert and oriented mental status in this setting
stages.explanation: A wet, gurgly voice and coughing after swallowing liquids indicate that material is penetrating to or below the level of the vocal cords—hallmark signs of aspiration risk. An intact gag reflex (option A) is reassuring to many clinicians but does not reliably predict swallow safety; patients can aspirate with a normal gag and protect the airway with an absent one. Symmetric palatal elevation (option C) reflects intact vagal motor function to the palate but says little about the coordinated swallow and airway protection during the pharyngeal phase. Alert mental status (option D) is necessary but not sufficient for safe swallowing. Recognizing post-swallow wet voice and cough as the key signs guides appropriate NPO status and further instrumental evaluation.

stages.label: SWALLOW EVALUATION
stages.content:
Bedside swallow screen: FAILED — cough and wet voice with 3-ounce water test
Speech-language pathology bedside eval: overt aspiration signs with thin liquids; better tolerance of pureed textures
SpO2 stable during assessment
Chest x-ray: no infiltrate at this time
Videofluoroscopic swallow study scheduled to define safe consistencies

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After she fails the bedside screen, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Discharge her with no dietary restrictions as initial therapy
Begin full oral feeding with a straw to speed intake as initial therapy
Resume a regular diet despite the failed screen as initial therapy
Maintain NPO, obtain a formal speech-language pathology evaluation,
stages.explanation: A failed bedside screen mandates keeping her NPO and obtaining a formal speech-language pathology evaluation; an instrumental study such as videofluoroscopy or fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing then defines which textures and strategies are safe before the diet is advanced. Resuming a regular diet (option A) directly contradicts the failed screen and invites aspiration pneumonia. Discharging her without restrictions (option C) is unsafe and ignores a documented swallowing deficit. Feeding by straw (option D) increases bolus speed and aspiration risk and is exactly the kind of strategy to avoid in dysphagia. Following a structured screen-then-instrumental-assessment pathway, with appropriate texture modification and aspiration precautions, is the standard approach to preventing aspiration pneumonia after stroke.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which bundle of measures best reduces her risk of aspiration pneumonia while swallowing recovers?
options: Routine prophylactic antibiotics for all dysphagic patients before discharge planning
Lying flat during and after meals to relax the throat as the immediate next step
Sedation to reduce coughing as the immediate next step
Upright positioning during
stages.explanation: Aspiration pneumonia prevention bundles include sitting the patient upright (about 90°) during and for a period after meals, meticulous oral hygiene to reduce the bacterial burden of any aspirated secretions, texture-modified diets and compensatory swallowing strategies prescribed by speech-language pathology, and supervised, unhurried feeding. Lying flat (option A) promotes reflux and aspiration—the opposite of what is needed. Sedation (option C) blunts the protective cough and worsens aspiration risk. Routine prophylactic antibiotics (option D) are not indicated and drive resistance; antibiotics treat established infection rather than prevent aspiration. A multimodal prevention bundle is the evidence-based way to protect dysphagic stroke patients.

cases.diagnosis: Post-stroke oropharyngeal dysphagia with aspiration risk
pearls: Keep every acute stroke patient NPO until a validated swallow screen is passed; failure mandates SLP evaluation and often an instrumental study.
Wet/gurgly voice and cough after swallowing signal aspiration; an intact gag reflex does NOT confirm a safe swallow.
Prevent aspiration pneumonia with upright positioning, oral hygiene, texture-modified diets, and supervised feeding—not routine prophylactic antibiotics.
Avoid straws and flat positioning in dysphagic patients, as both increase aspiration risk.

cases.title: Forgetful and Slowed Down, Step by Step
cases.chiefComplaint: His wife reports his memory and thinking have worsened in distinct downward steps over the past few years.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man comes to clinic with his wife, who describes a decline in his thinking over about three years that has happened in distinct steps rather than gradually—periods of stability punctuated by abrupt drops, sometimes coinciding with what may have been small strokes or transient spells. He has trouble with planning, organizing finances, and multitasking; he is slower and more apathetic, but his episodic memory is relatively better preserved early on. He has had at least one prior lacunar stroke with transient slurred speech. PMH: long-standing hypertension (often poorly controlled), type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, prior lacunar stroke, and tobacco use. Meds: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, lisinopril 20 mg, metformin 1000 mg BID, amlodipine 10 mg. Allergies: none. Social: retired accountant, former heavy smoker, lives with his wife. Family history: hypertension and stroke. There are no visual hallucinations, no early prominent resting tremor, and no REM sleep behavior disorder reported. His decline tracks with vascular risk and prior strokes rather than a smooth amnestic trajectory.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented to person and place, not to date. Slowed processing and reduced verbal fluency; difficulty with set-shifting and the clock-drawing/executive tasks, with relatively spared delayed recall when given cues. Mild left facial flattening and subtle left pronator drift (old). Mildly increased tone in the legs with brisk knee reflexes and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Short-stepped, slightly wide-based gait. No resting tremor, no cogwheeling. No prominent cortical signs such as aphasia or apraxia. Cardiac exam regular without murmurs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which cognitive profile best fits this patient's presentation?
options: Severe early visuospatial deficits with fluctuating attention and visual hallucinations
Predominantly executive dysfunction
Isolated, progressive nonfluent aphasia as the first step today
Early, prominent episodic memory loss with preserved executive function as the first step today
stages.explanation: Vascular cognitive impairment characteristically produces a dysexecutive, psychomotor-slowing pattern—impaired planning, set-shifting, and processing speed—with relatively preserved early episodic memory, often following a stepwise course tied to cerebrovascular events. Early prominent amnesia with preserved executive function (option A) is the classic signature of Alzheimer disease, not vascular dementia. Severe early visuospatial deficits with fluctuating attention and visual hallucinations (option C) describe dementia with Lewy bodies. Isolated progressive nonfluent aphasia (option D) points to a primary progressive aphasia/frontotemporal syndrome. His vascular risk burden, prior strokes, focal signs, and dysexecutive stepwise decline all converge on a vascular etiology, which directs the workup toward imaging of cerebrovascular disease and aggressive risk-factor control.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a vascular rather than a degenerative cause of his cognitive decline?
options: Prominent early visual hallucinations as the primary diagnosis
Gradual, smooth, continuous memory decline as the primary diagnosis
Stepwise deterioration temporally linked to cerebrovascular events with focal neurologic signs
Onset before age 60 with a strong autosomal-dominant family history as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Stepwise decline that correlates with strokes/TIAs, together with focal neurologic findings (his old facial droop, pronator drift, brisk reflexes, extensor plantars) and a heavy vascular risk profile, is the hallmark of vascular cognitive impairment. A gradual, smooth memory decline (option A) is more typical of Alzheimer disease. Prominent early visual hallucinations (option C) suggest Lewy body dementia. Early onset with autosomal-dominant inheritance (option D) suggests a familial degenerative dementia (or CADASIL specifically, though that is a distinct entity); it is not the typical sporadic late-onset vascular picture here. Identifying the vascular signature shifts management toward secondary stroke prevention and risk-factor optimization, which can slow further decline.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MoCA: 20/30 (losses in executive, attention, and delayed recall with cued improvement)
MRI brain: Multiple chronic lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia and pons; confluent periventricular and deep white matter hyperintensities (Fazekas 2–3)
HbA1c 8.4%
LDL 122 mg/dL
TSH 2.1 mIU/L; B12 410 pg/mL; RPR nonreactive
Carotid duplex: <50% stenosis bilaterally

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important management strategy to slow further cognitive decline?
options: Memantine to reverse the white matter disease as initial therapy
High-dose donepezil as the primary disease-modifying therapy as initial therapy
Long-term anticoagulation regardless of stroke mechanism as initial therapy
Aggressive control of vascular risk factors
stages.explanation: Because the disease is driven by cerebrovascular injury, the cornerstone is aggressive secondary prevention: optimizing blood pressure (his is 158/92 with a high HbA1c and LDL), tightening glycemic and lipid control, ensuring appropriate antiplatelet therapy, and smoking cessation—measures that reduce further ischemic injury and can slow decline. Cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil (option B) offer only modest, symptomatic benefit and are not disease-modifying for vascular dementia. Empiric long-term anticoagulation (option C) is not indicated without a specific cardioembolic source (he is in sinus rhythm with lacunar disease) and adds hemorrhage risk. Memantine (option D) does not reverse white matter disease and has limited evidence here. Risk-factor control is the highest-yield intervention for vascular cognitive impairment.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling for the patient and his wife about prognosis and treatment expectations?
options: Risk-factor control may slow progression but will not reverse existing damage,
Cognition will return to normal once blood pressure is controlled as the immediate next step
No treatment can influence the course, so nothing should be done as the immediate next step
The condition is fully reversible with the right medication as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Honest counseling is that established vascular injury cannot be reversed, but rigorous control of vascular risk factors can slow further decline and reduce new events; the course often remains stepwise, and supportive care, cognitive and functional support, and treatment of mood/apathy matter. Telling them it is fully reversible (option A) or that cognition will normalize with blood pressure control (option D) is false and sets up disappointment. Saying nothing can be done (option C) is both inaccurate and harmful—it abandons effective preventive measures and supportive interventions. A realistic, action-oriented message empowers adherence to the very interventions that best protect his remaining function.

cases.diagnosis: Vascular cognitive impairment/dementia from small-vessel disease and prior strokes
pearls: Vascular dementia classically shows dysexecutive deficits and slowed processing with relatively spared early memory and a stepwise course.
Focal neurologic signs, prior strokes, and heavy vascular risk distinguish it from the smooth amnestic decline of Alzheimer disease.
Aggressive vascular risk-factor control (BP, glucose, lipids, antiplatelet, smoking cessation) is the cornerstone to slow progression.
Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine offer only modest symptomatic benefit and are not disease-modifying in vascular dementia.

cases.title: Unsteady Walking and New Incontinence
cases.chiefComplaint: Over the past two years she has developed a shuffling, magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and slowed thinking.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman is brought to clinic for a progressive two-year decline in walking and thinking. Her family describes a gradually worsening, short-stepped, "stuck-to-the-floor" gait with frequent near-falls, new urinary urgency and incontinence, and slowed, apathetic thinking with emotional lability. The decline has been mostly gradual with occasional abrupt worsening. She has severe long-standing, poorly controlled hypertension. PMH: hypertension (multiple agents, poor adherence), type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, prior small subcortical strokes, and depression. Meds: amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg, lisinopril 40 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, metformin, aspirin 81 mg, sertraline. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives with her son, former smoker. Family history: hypertension, stroke. She has no resting tremor and no history suggesting normal-pressure hydrocephalus shunt evaluation yet. There are no cortical signs such as aphasia or apraxia of speech, and no early prominent episodic amnesia. The triad of gait disturbance, incontinence, and subcortical cognitive slowing dominates the picture.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented to person/place; markedly slowed processing, reduced fluency, perseveration, and emotional lability with easy tearfulness. Gait: short-stepped, wide-based, magnetic/apraxic with start hesitation and difficulty turning; positive pull test. Increased lower-extremity tone, brisk knee and ankle reflexes, bilateral extensor plantar responses, and a positive snout/grasp (frontal release) signs. No resting tremor, no cogwheel rigidity, no cerebellar dysmetria. Strength grossly preserved against the apraxic gait. Sensory exam intact. No papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical triad best characterizes this presentation?
options: Acute headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia as the first step today
Ataxia, dysarthria, and intention tremor as the first step today
Gait apraxia, urinary incontinence,
Resting tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of a magnetic/apraxic gait, urinary incontinence, and subcortical cognitive slowing—on a background of severe chronic hypertension and prior small strokes—is the classic picture of Binswanger disease (subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy), a form of subcortical vascular dementia. A parkinsonian triad (option A) features resting tremor and cogwheel rigidity, which she lacks. A cerebellar triad (option C) of ataxia, dysarthria, and intention tremor does not fit her frontal-subcortical signs. Acute meningitic features (option D) are absent and would be acute, not a two-year decline. Recognizing this triad focuses imaging on diffuse small-vessel white matter disease and shifts management to blood-pressure and vascular risk control.

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: Which condition is the most important mimic to distinguish from this disorder, given the overlapping triad?
options: Bell palsy in this setting
Essential tremor in this setting
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
stages.explanation: Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) shares the same triad—gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment—so it is the crucial mimic to exclude, because NPH may improve with ventricular shunting whereas Binswanger disease is treated by vascular risk control. The key differentiator is imaging: NPH shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy with relatively preserved white matter, whereas Binswanger shows extensive periventricular/deep white matter ischemic change and lacunes. Essential tremor (option B), Bell palsy (option C), and carpal tunnel syndrome (option D) are unrelated to this triad and do not explain her gait, incontinence, and cognition. Distinguishing NPH from subcortical vascular dementia changes both prognosis and the decision about invasive treatment.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI brain: Extensive confluent periventricular and deep white matter hyperintensities (severe leukoaraiosis, Fazekas 3); multiple lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia/thalamus; microbleeds on susceptibility imaging
Ventricles only mildly prominent, proportional to atrophy
B12 520 pg/mL; TSH 1.8 mIU/L; RPR nonreactive; HIV negative
HbA1c 7.9%; LDL 70 mg/dL
Neuropsych testing: dysexecutive, psychomotor slowing pattern

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding most strongly confirms the diagnosis over normal pressure hydrocephalus?
options: Extensive confluent periventricular/deep white matter ischemic change with lacunes
A solitary acute cortical infarct in the MCA territory as the primary diagnosis
Disproportionate ventriculomegaly with effacement of the high-convexity sulci and preserved white matter
Diffuse cerebral edema with loss of gray-white differentiation as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Binswanger disease is confirmed by extensive confluent periventricular and deep white matter ischemic disease (severe leukoaraiosis) with lacunar infarcts and microbleeds, while the ventricles are only mildly enlarged and proportional to atrophy—exactly this MRI. In NPH (option A), the hallmark is disproportionate ventriculomegaly with crowding/effacement of high-convexity sulci and relatively spared white matter, which would favor shunting. A solitary acute cortical infarct (option C) does not explain a chronic subcortical triad. Diffuse edema with loss of gray-white differentiation (option D) indicates an acute global insult (e.g., anoxia), not chronic small-vessel disease. The white matter burden with proportional ventricles cements subcortical vascular dementia over NPH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate cornerstone of management for this patient?
options: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids to reduce white matter inflammation as monotherapy
Strict long-term blood pressure control
Initiation of levodopa for the gait disorder as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of Binswanger disease centers on aggressive, sustained control of blood pressure (hers is 172/96 with poor adherence) and other vascular risk factors, antiplatelet therapy when appropriate, plus supportive measures—gait/falls and continence management, treating depression, and caregiver support—to slow progression of small-vessel disease. A shunt (option A) is the treatment for NPH, which her imaging excludes, and would not help leukoaraiosis. Corticosteroids (option C) are wrong because the white matter change is ischemic, not inflammatory, and steroids would worsen her hypertension and glycemia. Levodopa (option D) treats parkinsonism, which she does not have; her gait is apraxic/vascular, not hypokinetic-rigid. Vascular risk control is the highest-yield, evidence-based cornerstone.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most accurate statement about her expected course?
options: The condition is benign and self-limited as the immediate next step
Shunting will reliably reverse all three components of the triad as the immediate next step
Symptoms will fully resolve with blood pressure control as the immediate next step
The disorder typically progresses, but rigorous vascular risk control can slow decline
stages.explanation: Binswanger disease usually follows a progressive course with stepwise and gradual worsening; the established white matter injury is irreversible, but rigorous blood-pressure and vascular risk control can slow further accumulation of small-vessel damage and reduce new events, while supportive care addresses gait, continence, and mood. Claiming full resolution with blood pressure control (option A) or that it is benign and self-limited (option C) misrepresents a chronic progressive disease. Shunting (option D) treats NPH, not subcortical vascular dementia, and will not reverse her triad. An accurate, realistic prognosis supports adherence to the preventive and supportive measures that most benefit her.

cases.diagnosis: Binswanger disease (subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy / subcortical vascular dementia)
pearls: Binswanger disease presents with gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, and subcortical cognitive slowing in patients with severe chronic hypertension and small-vessel disease.
Normal pressure hydrocephalus is the key mimic; distinguish by imaging—NPH shows disproportionate ventriculomegaly, Binswanger shows extensive leukoaraiosis with lacunes.
Treatment centers on strict blood pressure and vascular risk-factor control plus supportive care; the white matter damage is not reversible.
Frontal release signs, magnetic gait, and bilateral extensor plantars reflect the diffuse frontal-subcortical disconnection of severe small-vessel disease.

cases.title: A Second Stroke in a Healthy Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: She has had a second stroke affecting the same side of her body despite a thorough negative workup.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman is referred to stroke clinic after a second ischemic stroke in the left MCA territory in eight months, each presenting with transient right arm weakness and word-finding difficulty, the latest leaving mild residual deficits. She has few vascular risk factors: no hypertension, no diabetes, nonsmoker, normal lipids, not on estrogen-containing contraception, and no migraine with aura. The first stroke workup—echocardiogram with bubble study, prolonged cardiac monitoring, hypercoagulable panel, and routine vessel imaging interpreted as unremarkable—was largely negative, and she was placed on aspirin, yet she had a recurrent event in the same territory. PMH: otherwise healthy; one uncomplicated pregnancy. Meds: aspirin 81 mg. Allergies: none. Social: works as a nurse, married, no alcohol or drugs. Family history: noncontributory; no early strokes or connective tissue disease. She has no neck pain, no recent trauma, and no fever. The striking feature is recurrent strokes confined to a single carotid territory in a young woman without conventional risk factors, raising suspicion for a focal arterial source.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, with subtle residual right-hand clumsiness and very mild word-finding pauses. Mild right pronator drift (4+/5), otherwise full strength. Right-sided fine-motor slowing; sensation intact. No carotid bruit appreciated. Cranial nerves otherwise normal; visual fields full. No nystagmus, no dysmetria. Cardiac exam normal, regular rhythm, no murmurs. No skin findings of connective tissue disease. Gait normal. The exam is notable for focal left-hemispheric signs with an otherwise unremarkable general and cardiac examination.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating recurrent same-territory strokes in this young woman with a negative cardiac and hypercoagulable workup?
options: Start empiric long-term warfarin without further imaging as the first step today
Repeat only a transthoracic echocardiogram as the first step today
Conclude the strokes are cryptogenic and simply continue aspirin as the first step today
Obtain dedicated high-resolution carotid imaging
stages.explanation: Recurrent strokes confined to one carotid territory in a young patient without conventional risk factors and with a negative cardiac/hypercoagulable evaluation should prompt dedicated arterial imaging focused on the ipsilateral carotid bulb. A carotid web—a shelf-like intraluminal projection (a variant of fibromuscular dysplasia) at the posterior bulb—can be missed on routine studies and is best seen on careful CTA/MRA, vessel-wall MRI, or catheter angiography. Simply labeling the strokes cryptogenic and continuing aspirin (option A) ignores a recurrent, territory-specific pattern and an identifiable, treatable source. Empiric warfarin without imaging (option C) is not evidence-based for arterial web disease and adds bleeding risk. Repeating only a transthoracic echo (option D) re-examines an already low-yield, negative cardiac source. Targeted vessel imaging is the key next step.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a shelf-like lesion at the posterior carotid bulb on imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Atherosclerotic carotid plaque
Takayasu arteritis as the primary diagnosis
Carotid web
Carotid artery dissection in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A thin, shelf-like, web-shaped projection arising from the posterior wall of the internal carotid bulb is the classic appearance of a carotid web, considered a focal intimal variant of fibromuscular dysplasia; it creates flow stasis distal to the shelf, promoting thrombus formation and recurrent embolic strokes in young patients. Atherosclerotic plaque (option A) is unlikely given her age and absent risk factors and looks like calcified/irregular wall thickening, not a thin shelf. Dissection (option C) shows an intimal flap, tapered stenosis, or intramural hematoma, usually with neck pain or trauma, which she lacks. Takayasu arteritis (option D) is a large-vessel vasculitis with long-segment stenoses, systemic inflammatory features, and elevated inflammatory markers, not a focal bulb shelf. The web explains her recurrent, same-territory events.

stages.label: VESSEL IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography neck: Thin shelf-like membrane projecting from the posterior wall of the left internal carotid bulb with focal contrast pooling distal to the shelf
No significant atherosclerotic calcification; contralateral carotid normal
Vessel-wall MRI: no intramural hematoma or dissection flap
ESR 10 mm/hr; CRP normal
No flow-limiting stenosis but features consistent with a carotid web

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has now had a recurrent stroke on aspirin attributable to the symptomatic carotid web. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue aspirin monotherapy unchanged as initial therapy
Begin high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Observation alone with no change in therapy as initial therapy
Definitive treatment of the symptomatic web with carotid endarterectomy or carotid stenting
stages.explanation: For a symptomatic carotid web with recurrent ipsilateral stroke despite antiplatelet therapy, definitive treatment of the lesion—carotid endarterectomy (resecting the web) or carotid artery stenting—is favored to remove the embolic source, since antiplatelet therapy alone has a substantial recurrence rate in symptomatic webs; if revascularization is deferred, escalating antithrombotic therapy is considered. Continuing unchanged aspirin (option A) is what already failed, leaving her at high recurrence risk. Corticosteroids (option C) treat vasculitis, but a web is not inflammatory—her ESR/CRP are normal. Observation alone (option D) ignores a recurrent, identified, treatable source. Targeting the web itself best prevents further events in this young patient with many years of stroke risk ahead.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most accurate point to convey about carotid webs?
options: They are a common cause of stroke in older patients with atherosclerosis in this setting
They never recur once a patient is on aspirin as the immediate next step
They are an under-recognized cause of recurrent cryptogenic stroke in young patients
They are always bilateral and require lifelong anticoagulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Carotid webs are an under-recognized but important cause of otherwise cryptogenic, recurrent ipsilateral stroke in young patients (often young women) and are frequently overlooked on routine imaging unless the carotid bulb is scrutinized—exactly the lesson of this case. They are not a typical mechanism of stroke in older atherosclerotic patients (option A). They are not always bilateral, and anticoagulation is not the universal answer (option C)—management favors revascularization for symptomatic webs. They clearly can recur on aspirin (option D), as this patient demonstrates. Recognizing the entity prompts the dedicated imaging and definitive treatment that prevent devastating repeat strokes in young people.

cases.diagnosis: Carotid web (focal intimal fibromuscular dysplasia variant) causing recurrent ipsilateral ischemic stroke
pearls: Suspect a carotid web in young patients (often women) with recurrent same-territory strokes and an otherwise negative cardioembolic/hypercoagulable workup.
A web is a thin shelf-like projection from the posterior carotid bulb—easily missed on routine imaging; scrutinize the bulb on CTA/MRA or vessel-wall imaging.
Symptomatic webs have a high recurrence risk on antiplatelet therapy alone; definitive treatment is endarterectomy or stenting.
Distinguish a web (shelf, flow stasis, normal inflammatory markers) from atherosclerosis, dissection, and vasculitis.

cases.title: Brief Arm Numbness With a Whooshing in the Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: She had a transient episode of left arm and face numbness and hears a rhythmic whooshing in her right ear.
cases.history: A 43-year-old woman presents after a transient episode of left face and arm numbness with mild clumsiness that resolved within 30 minutes. For months she has also noticed a rhythmic, pulse-synchronous whooshing sound in her right ear (pulsatile tinnitus) and intermittent headaches. She has few vascular risk factors. PMH: hypertension diagnosed unusually young (at age 35), otherwise healthy. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg, no estrogen contraception. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as an accountant. Family history: a sister with "renal artery problems" and early hypertension. She denies recent neck trauma or chiropractic manipulation, though she recalls turning her head sharply before symptoms began. No fever, no weight loss, no jaw claudication. The constellation of a young woman with early-onset hypertension, pulsatile tinnitus, transient focal neurologic symptoms, and a suggestive family history raises concern for an underlying arteriopathy complicated by an arterial injury. She is anxious but otherwise well-appearing.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, fully oriented; transient deficits have resolved and the neurologic exam is now normal—full strength, intact sensation, normal coordination and gait, full visual fields. A soft right cervical bruit is audible; a subtle right partial Horner syndrome (mild ptosis and miosis) is noted. No carotid tenderness. Pulses symmetric. No skin laxity, no joint hypermobility, no lens dislocation. Cardiac exam normal, regular rhythm without murmurs. Fundoscopy normal, no papilledema. The key positives are the cervical bruit and partial Horner, with an otherwise nonfocal exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which underlying arteriopathy best unifies her early-onset hypertension, pulsatile tinnitus, suggestive family history, and now a focal arterial event?
options: Fibromuscular dysplasia
Atherosclerosis
Giant cell (temporal) arteritis
Polyarteritis nodosa
stages.explanation: Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) is a non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic arteriopathy that predominantly affects young to middle-aged women and classically involves the renal arteries (early-onset hypertension, her sister's "renal artery problems") and the cervical carotid/vertebral arteries (pulsatile tinnitus, bruit, and predisposition to dissection). Atherosclerosis (option A) is unlikely given her age, sex, and minimal risk factors, and does not explain pulsatile tinnitus with renal involvement at a young age. Giant cell arteritis (option C) affects older patients with elevated inflammatory markers, jaw claudication, and visual loss, none present. Polyarteritis nodosa (option D) is a systemic necrotizing vasculitis with constitutional and multi-organ inflammatory features and abnormal markers. FMD ties the renal, cervical, and now ischemic findings together.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the partial Horner syndrome and transient hemispheric symptoms, what acute arterial complication should be suspected?
options: Cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
Lacunar infarct from chronic hypertension
Carotid artery dissection
stages.explanation: A partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis from disruption of the sympathetic plexus on the internal carotid) plus pulsatile tinnitus, a cervical bruit, and transient ipsilateral hemispheric symptoms after sharp head turning strongly suggests an internal carotid artery dissection—a well-known complication of underlying FMD. Cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation (option B) is unlikely in a young woman in sinus rhythm and would not cause Horner syndrome or pulsatile tinnitus. A lacunar infarct (option C) reflects chronic small-vessel disease and does not produce Horner syndrome or a bruit. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (option D) typically causes headache, seizures, papilledema, and venous infarcts, not a painful Horner with carotid territory TIA. The Horner-plus-TIA pattern points to carotid dissection.

stages.label: VASCULAR IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography head/neck: Right internal carotid artery with a long tapering stenosis and "string-of-beads" alternating dilations and constrictions in the mid-cervical segment
Focal eccentric intramural hematoma in the right ICA consistent with dissection
Renal artery imaging: multifocal beading of the mid-distal renal arteries
Brain MRI: small scattered right hemispheric DWI lesions (embolic pattern)
ESR/CRP normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMATION
stages.question: The angiographic "string-of-beads" appearance is most characteristic of which condition?
options: Atherosclerotic stenosis in this setting
Fibromuscular dysplasia
Vasospasm from subarachnoid hemorrhage
Moyamoya disease as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The "string-of-beads" pattern—alternating segments of stenosis and aneurysmal dilation, typically in the mid-to-distal portions of the renal and cervical arteries—is the hallmark of the medial fibroplasia type of fibromuscular dysplasia, confirming her underlying arteriopathy; the superimposed intramural hematoma confirms the dissection. Atherosclerosis (option A) causes focal, proximal, calcified, irregular plaques, not symmetric beading. Moyamoya (option C) shows progressive supraclinoid ICA stenosis with a basal collateral "puff of smoke," a different angiographic signature. Vasospasm after subarachnoid hemorrhage (option D) is diffuse arterial narrowing in the acute post-hemorrhage setting, not chronic beading with renal involvement. The string-of-beads plus renal beading nails FMD as the substrate for her dissection and TIA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of her carotid dissection with TIA and small embolic infarcts?
options: Thrombolysis with tissue plasminogen activator now that symptoms have resolved as initial therapy
Antithrombotic therapy for the dissection plus blood pressure control, with stenting reserved for failure/recurrence
High-dose corticosteroids and immunosuppression as initial therapy
Immediate carotid endarterectomy of the dissected segment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Extracranial carotid dissection causing TIA/minor stroke is managed medically with antithrombotic therapy—either antiplatelet or anticoagulation, which have shown comparable outcomes in trials—to prevent further embolism while the vessel heals, along with control of her hypertension; endovascular stenting is reserved for recurrent ischemia despite medical therapy or hemodynamic compromise. Immediate endarterectomy of a dissected segment (option A) is not first-line and is technically hazardous in friable FMD tissue. Corticosteroids/immunosuppression (option C) treat vasculitis, but FMD is non-inflammatory with normal ESR/CRP. Thrombolysis (option D) is contraindicated when symptoms have already resolved (no current deficit/no active stroke window) and is generally avoided in dissection-related TIA. Antithrombotic therapy plus risk-factor control is the appropriate, evidence-based approach.

cases.diagnosis: Fibromuscular dysplasia complicated by internal carotid artery dissection presenting with TIA and pulsatile tinnitus
pearls: FMD is a non-inflammatory arteriopathy of young-to-middle-aged women affecting renal (early HTN) and cervical (tinnitus, dissection) arteries; inflammatory markers are normal.
The angiographic 'string-of-beads' (alternating stenosis and dilation) is the hallmark of medial fibroplasia FMD.
A painful partial Horner syndrome with ipsilateral TIA suggests internal carotid artery dissection, a key FMD complication.
Treat dissection-related TIA/minor stroke with antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) plus BP control; reserve stenting for medical failure.

cases.title: Strokes in Different Places, Again and Again
cases.chiefComplaint: He has had several strokes affecting different parts of the brain over a few weeks despite being on aspirin.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man is admitted with his third ischemic stroke in six weeks. The events have involved different vascular territories—right hemiparesis from a left MCA branch, then a right visual field cut from a right PCA branch, and now left-arm weakness—suggesting a proximal embolic source showering multiple regions. He has been on aspirin since the first event. PMH: long-standing hypertension, hyperlipidemia, peripheral arterial disease, prior coronary stenting, and a 50-pack-year smoking history. Meds: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, lisinopril, metoprolol, clopidogrel (recently added). Allergies: none. Social: retired truck driver, current smoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: early coronary disease. He has no palpitations, and telemetry plus prior monitoring has shown sinus rhythm without atrial fibrillation. He has noted livedo-like mottling on his feet and a small painful toe lesion. The pattern of recurrent multi-territory embolic strokes in a heavy smoker with diffuse atherosclerosis and peripheral embolic phenomena points toward a proximal aortic source rather than a cardiac arrhythmia.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, oriented. New mild left-arm pronator drift (4/5) plus old right-sided weakness and a right homonymous field defect. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; speech fluent. Cardiac: regular rhythm, no murmurs, no irregularly irregular pulse. Carotid upstrokes intact, faint bilateral carotid bruits. Peripheral: diminished distal pulses, livedo reticularis over the feet, and a small tender violaceous ("blue") toe lesion. No splinter hemorrhages elsewhere, no fever. Abdomen soft. Multi-territory deficits with peripheral embolic stigmata are the salient findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What does the pattern of recurrent strokes in multiple vascular territories most strongly suggest about the mechanism?
options: Primary CNS vasculitis confined to small vessels
A proximal embolic source showering multiple territories
A single fixed carotid stenosis as the first step today
Small-vessel (lacunar) disease as the first step today
stages.explanation: Strokes spanning multiple, non-contiguous vascular territories (left MCA, right PCA, now another) point to a proximal embolic source launching emboli to different regions—classically the heart or the aortic arch—rather than disease of a single vessel or small vessels. Small-vessel/lacunar disease (option A) produces deep, single-territory lacunes with classic syndromes, not scattered cortical infarcts across territories. A single fixed carotid stenosis (option C) would cause repeated events in one territory only, not bilateral/multi-territory infarcts. Primary CNS vasculitis (option D) is rare, usually causes headache and encephalopathy with abnormal CSF/angiography, and would be a diagnosis of exclusion. The multi-territory pattern mandates a search for a proximal embolic source, especially after telemetry has excluded atrial fibrillation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Telemetry shows sinus rhythm and prior monitoring found no atrial fibrillation. What is the best next diagnostic test to identify the embolic source?
options: Transthoracic echocardiogram alone as the primary diagnosis
Repeat noncontrast head CT as the primary diagnosis
Transesophageal echocardiography to evaluate the aortic arch
Lumbar puncture as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is the test of choice to visualize the aortic arch (and the left atrium/appendage and interatrial septum), where complex or mobile atheromatous plaque—a recognized cause of recurrent multi-territory embolic stroke—is best seen; transthoracic imaging poorly visualizes the arch. Transthoracic echo alone (option A) cannot adequately assess the proximal aorta and may miss the culprit plaque. A repeat head CT (option C) characterizes brain injury but does not reveal the embolic source. Lumbar puncture (option D) is for suspected infection, inflammation, or subarachnoid hemorrhage—not embolic source evaluation. Given his diffuse atherosclerosis, peripheral embolic signs, and absence of atrial fibrillation, TEE targeting the arch is the highest-yield study.

stages.label: TEE & LABS
stages.content:
Transesophageal echocardiography: Complex atheroma in the aortic arch measuring 5.5 mm in thickness with a mobile, pedunculated component
No left atrial/appendage thrombus; no patent foramen ovale; normal LV function
Carotid duplex: 40–50% bilateral stenosis (non-flow-limiting)
LDL 96 mg/dL; HbA1c 6.1%
ESR/CRP mildly elevated; eosinophilia noted (peripheral embolic/cholesterol phenomena)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which TEE finding is most strongly associated with a high risk of recurrent embolic stroke?
options: A complex aortic arch atheroma ≥4 mm thick and/or with mobile/ulcerated components
Aortic arch plaque <2 mm thick without mobile components before discharge planning
Mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy as the immediate next step
Trace mitral regurgitation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Aortic arch atheromas that are large (commonly cited at ≥4 mm thick) and/or have mobile, pedunculated, or ulcerated components carry a markedly elevated risk of recurrent embolic stroke—his 5.5 mm plaque with a mobile element is exactly such a high-risk lesion and explains the multi-territory infarcts and peripheral cholesterol emboli (blue toe, livedo, eosinophilia). Thin plaque <2 mm without mobile features (option A) confers much lower risk. Mild concentric LV hypertrophy (option C) reflects chronic hypertension, not an embolic source. Trace mitral regurgitation (option D) is a benign incidental finding. Identifying the high-risk arch plaque confirms the mechanism and frames management around aggressive medical secondary prevention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy for recurrent stroke from this high-risk aortic arch atheroma?
options: Surgical endarterectomy of the aortic arch as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Aggressive medical therapy
Long-term warfarin for all aortic atheromas as the preferred therapy as initial therapy
No change in management since he is already on aspirin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Aortic arch atheroma is managed primarily with aggressive medical therapy: high-intensity statins (which stabilize/regress plaque and reduce events), antiplatelet therapy, strict blood-pressure control, and smoking cessation—central for this current smoker with diffuse atherosclerosis. Aortic arch endarterectomy (option A) is highly invasive, not first-line, and reserved for rare refractory cases. Routine long-term warfarin (option C) has not proven superior to antiplatelet therapy for aortic atheroma and may even precipitate cholesterol embolization, so it is not the preferred default. Making no change (option D) is unacceptable given recurrent strokes and peripheral emboli on his current regimen. Intensive risk-factor modification and statin-based plaque stabilization offer the best protection against further embolic events.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent multi-territory embolic stroke from a complex, mobile aortic arch atheroma
pearls: Strokes in multiple, non-contiguous vascular territories suggest a proximal embolic source (cardiac or aortic), not single-vessel or small-vessel disease.
Transesophageal echocardiography is the test of choice to visualize aortic arch atheroma, which transthoracic echo poorly sees.
Arch plaques ≥4 mm thick and/or with mobile/ulcerated components carry high recurrent embolic risk; peripheral signs include blue toe, livedo, and eosinophilia.

Treat aortic arch atheroma with aggressive medical therapy (high-intensity statin, antiplatelet, BP control, smoking cessation); warfarin is not routinely preferred and may worsen cholesterol embolization.

cases.title: Lightheaded When He Paints the Ceiling
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodic dizziness and blurred vision when using his left arm overhead.
cases.history: A 65-year-old retired house painter presents with several months of brief dizziness, blurred vision, and occasional unsteadiness that occur only when he works with his left arm raised, such as painting overhead or hanging curtains. Episodes last under a minute and resolve when he rests the arm. He denies chest pain, palpitations, or loss of consciousness. He has noticed the left arm tires and feels cool and achy with sustained use. PMH includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and a 40-pack-year smoking history (quit 2 years ago). Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily. No allergies. He lives alone, drinks alcohol socially, and is a former smoker. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease in his father and a brother with peripheral arterial disease. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable; no dysarthria, no facial droop, no leg weakness, and no symptoms at rest.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact; no nystagmus at rest. Strength 5/5 in all limbs at rest; left arm fatigues with repetitive elevation. The left radial pulse is diminished compared to the right, and the left arm is slightly cooler. A bruit is auscultated over the left supraclavicular fossa. No carotid bruits. Romberg negative at baseline. Sensation intact. Reflexes symmetric. Gait normal at rest. No dysmetria. Repeating overhead left-arm exercise reproduces transient lightheadedness with no fixed focal deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single bedside finding most strongly points toward the underlying diagnosis?
options: A carotid bruit on the right as the first step today
Brief dizziness that resolves with rest as the first step today
An interarm systolic blood pressure difference of 36 mmHg with a diminished left radial pulse
A 40-pack-year smoking history as the first step today
stages.explanation: A large interarm systolic BP difference (here 36 mmHg) with a diminished ipsilateral radial pulse is the hallmark of proximal subclavian artery stenosis, which underlies subclavian steal. A difference >15 mmHg is abnormal and >20 mmHg is highly suggestive of subclavian disease. A right carotid bruit is wrong because symptoms localize to posterior circulation and the left arm, not a right carotid lesion. Exertional dizziness that resolves with rest is supportive but nonspecific and does not localize. The smoking history is a risk factor but not a localizing physical finding. The interarm gradient plus pulse asymmetry directly implicates a left subclavian lesion proximal to the vertebral origin.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best initial noninvasive test to confirm the suspected pathophysiology?
options: Transthoracic echocardiogram in this clinical context
Duplex ultrasound of the vertebral
CT angiography of the head with perfusion
24-hour Holter monitor as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Duplex ultrasound of the vertebral and subclavian arteries is the best first noninvasive study: it demonstrates the characteristic reversed (retrograde) vertebral artery flow and can show a to-and-fro or fully reversed waveform, often accentuated by arm exercise or a hyperemia cuff maneuver. CTA head with perfusion evaluates intracranial parenchyma and large vessels but is less ideal as the first step for confirming flow reversal and adds contrast/radiation. Echocardiography evaluates a cardioembolic source, which is not suggested by the arm-exertional, positional pattern. Holter monitoring assesses arrhythmia and would not explain reproducible arm-induced symptoms with an interarm gradient.

stages.label: DUPLEX ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Left vertebral artery: retrograde (reversed) flow at rest
Flow reversal accentuated after left-arm hyperemia cuff maneuver
Left subclavian artery: elevated peak systolic velocity >300 cm/s proximal to vertebral origin, consistent with severe stenosis
Right vertebral artery: normal antegrade flow
Bilateral internal carotid arteries: no significant stenosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has bothersome but non-disabling symptoms. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Urgent subclavian-to-carotid bypass as initial therapy
Start therapeutic warfarin as initial therapy
Immediate carotid endarterectomy as initial therapy
Optimize vascular risk factors
stages.explanation: Most patients with subclavian steal have mild, activity-related, non-disabling symptoms and are managed medically: aggressive risk-factor modification with an antiplatelet agent, high-intensity statin, blood pressure and glucose control, and smoking cessation, plus reassurance and activity counseling. Carotid endarterectomy treats carotid bifurcation disease, not a proximal subclavian lesion. Warfarin/anticoagulation is not indicated for atherosclerotic subclavian stenosis without another indication. Surgical bypass or endovascular stenting is reserved for disabling posterior-circulation symptoms refractory to medical therapy, critical arm ischemia, or coronary-subclavian steal after internal mammary CABG. Revascularization (usually subclavian angioplasty/stenting) is considered only when conservative measures fail.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this patient had previously undergone CABG using the LEFT internal mammary artery, which complication would most concern you?
options: Renal artery stenosis with flash pulmonary edema
Acute mesenteric ischemia as the immediate next step
Coronary-subclavian steal causing angina with left-arm exertion
Aortic dissection extending to the carotids in this setting
stages.explanation: When the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) is used for CABG and a proximal left subclavian stenosis is present, blood can be 'stolen' retrograde down the LIMA graft, producing coronary-subclavian steal and exertional angina, classically provoked by left-arm activity. This is an important indication for subclavian revascularization. Mesenteric ischemia involves the celiac/SMA and presents with postprandial pain and weight loss, unrelated to subclavian flow. Renal artery stenosis causes resistant hypertension and flash pulmonary edema, not arm-induced angina. Aortic dissection is an acute catastrophic event with tearing pain and pulse deficits, not the chronic exertional pattern described.

cases.diagnosis: Subclavian steal syndrome from severe proximal left subclavian artery stenosis with retrograde left vertebral flow
pearls: Check blood pressure in BOTH arms: an interarm systolic difference >15-20 mmHg with a diminished radial pulse and supraclavicular bruit suggests proximal subclavian stenosis.
Subclavian steal = retrograde vertebral flow distal to a proximal subclavian/innominate stenosis; symptoms are typically arm-exertional posterior-circulation spells.
Duplex ultrasound showing reversed vertebral flow (accentuated by arm exercise) confirms the diagnosis noninvasively.
Most cases are managed medically; revascularization is reserved for disabling symptoms, limb ischemia, or coronary-subclavian steal after LIMA CABG.

cases.title: Fleeting Right-Hand Weakness That Keeps Coming Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated brief spells of right-hand clumsiness and word-finding trouble despite taking aspirin.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents with three episodes over the past two weeks of sudden right-hand weakness and difficulty finding words, each lasting 5 to 15 minutes with full recovery. He was started on aspirin 81 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily after a similar spell one month ago, yet symptoms recurred. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 8.6%), hyperlipidemia, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. He is of East Asian descent. Additional medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. No allergies. He drinks minimal alcohol and is sedentary. Family history includes a father with stroke at 70. He denies headache, vision loss, vertigo, palpitations, or chest pain. He reports the spells sometimes occur when he stands up quickly or after large meals. He has not had any falls or seizures. Blood pressure logs from home show variability with some readings in the 150s-160s systolic. He is adherent to medications and has no recent illness.
cases.exam: Alert and fully oriented, fluent speech at baseline with no current aphasia. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields full. Motor exam 5/5 throughout at rest with no pronator drift; subtle slowing of right finger taps. Sensation intact to all modalities. Reflexes symmetric, plantar responses flexor. No carotid bruits. Cardiac exam regular rhythm without murmur. Gait normal, no ataxia. No neglect or extinction. Coordination intact. The transient nature of prior deficits and current near-normal exam are consistent with recurrent TIAs in the left middle cerebral artery territory.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: These recurrent left-MCA-territory TIAs occurred despite aspirin and a statin. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin
Vessel imaging with MRA or CTA of the head
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for CNS vasculitis in this clinical context
Reassure and continue current therapy with outpatient follow-up in 3 months
stages.explanation: Recurrent TIAs in a single arterial territory warrant urgent vascular and parenchymal imaging: MRI brain (for infarcts) and MRA/CTA of head and neck to identify a culprit lesion such as intracranial stenosis. Reassurance and routine follow-up are unsafe given recurrent events and high short-term stroke risk. Lumbar puncture for vasculitis is premature without imaging or systemic features. Empiric warfarin is not first-line for atherothrombotic intracranial disease and exposes the patient to bleeding risk without a diagnosis; anticoagulation has not shown benefit over antiplatelets for intracranial atherosclerotic stenosis (WASID trial). Imaging defines the mechanism and guides targeted secondary prevention.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain (DWI): two small punctate acute infarcts in the left MCA borderzone (deep watershed) territory
Intracranial MRA: severe focal stenosis of the proximal left middle cerebral artery (M1), estimated 75% luminal narrowing
Carotid duplex and neck CTA: no significant extracranial carotid stenosis
No aneurysm or dissection identified
No cardioembolic source on initial review

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms 75% symptomatic intracranial left M1 stenosis. Based on SAMMPRIS, what is the best management?
options: Switch to anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant as initial therapy
Immediate carotid endarterectomy as initial therapy
Aggressive medical therapy: dual antiplatelet for 90 days, high-intensity statin,
Intracranial angioplasty and stenting of the M1 lesion now as initial therapy
stages.explanation: SAMMPRIS demonstrated that aggressive medical therapy was superior to percutaneous angioplasty/stenting for symptomatic 70-99% intracranial stenosis: stenting carried a high periprocedural stroke/death rate (~14.7% at 30 days) versus ~5.8% in the medical arm. The regimen is aspirin 325 mg plus clopidogrel 75 mg for 90 days, then single antiplatelet, with high-intensity statin (LDL goal <70), systolic BP target <140, glycemic and lifestyle optimization. Stenting now is wrong because it increased early stroke/death. Anticoagulation (WASID) showed no benefit over antiplatelets and more bleeding. Carotid endarterectomy treats extracranial carotid disease, which this patient does not have. Medical therapy is the evidence-based choice.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which physiologic principle best explains why this patient's deep watershed infarcts and TIAs may worsen with overaggressive blood pressure lowering?
options: Hypertension directly dissolves the atherosclerotic plaque as the immediate next step
Lower blood pressure increases platelet aggregation as the immediate next step
Statins raise cerebral blood flow independent of pressure as the immediate next step
Loss of cerebral autoregulation distal to a fixed stenosis makes perfusion pressure-dependent
stages.explanation: Distal to a hemodynamically significant fixed stenosis, the cerebral vasculature is already maximally dilated and autoregulation is impaired, so distal perfusion becomes directly pressure-dependent. Borderzone (watershed) territory is most vulnerable, and overaggressive BP lowering can precipitate hypoperfusion infarcts or TIAs. This is why BP is lowered carefully (target <140 systolic over time) rather than abruptly, and why orthostatic and postprandial drops provoked his spells. Hypertension does not dissolve plaque. Lower BP does not increase platelet aggregation. Statins have pleiotropic effects but do not raise cerebral blood flow independent of perfusion pressure; the key mechanism is pressure-dependent flow through the stenosis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 90 days of dual antiplatelet therapy, what is the appropriate long-term antithrombotic plan if he has had no further events?
options: Begin triple therapy with aspirin, clopidogrel, and an anticoagulant
Stop all antiplatelets since he is asymptomatic with appropriate follow-up
Transition to single antiplatelet therapy
Continue lifelong aspirin plus clopidogrel together with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After the 90-day course of dual antiplatelet therapy used in SAMMPRIS, patients should step down to a single antiplatelet agent while continuing high-intensity statin, BP control (<140 systolic), glucose management, and lifestyle changes long-term. Prolonged dual antiplatelet therapy beyond ~90 days increases bleeding risk without added benefit for this indication. Stopping all antiplatelets is unsafe given symptomatic intracranial atherosclerosis and ongoing stroke risk. Triple therapy markedly increases hemorrhage risk and has no role here absent a separate anticoagulation indication. Single antiplatelet plus aggressive medical management is the durable secondary-prevention strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic stenosis of the left M1 segment causing recurrent MCA-territory TIAs and deep watershed infarcts
pearls: SAMMPRIS: for symptomatic 70-99% intracranial stenosis, aggressive medical therapy beats angioplasty/stenting due to high periprocedural stroke/death.
Regimen: aspirin + clopidogrel for 90 days, then single antiplatelet, plus high-intensity statin (LDL <70) and systolic BP <140.
Distal to a fixed stenosis, perfusion is pressure-dependent (impaired autoregulation); lower BP carefully to avoid watershed hypoperfusion.
Intracranial atherosclerosis is more common in Asian, Black, and Hispanic patients and often presents as recurrent same-territory TIAs or borderzone infarcts.

cases.title: Worst Headache and a Seizure in a College Athlete
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe headache followed by a witnessed seizure and right-sided weakness.
cases.history: A 22-year-old previously healthy college soccer player is brought to the ED after a sudden, severe occipital-parietal headache while studying, described as the worst of his life, followed minutes later by a witnessed generalized seizure. On arrival he is postictal and confused with new right arm weakness. He has no history of hypertension, illicit drug use, or trauma. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends and denies cocaine, amphetamines, or sympathomimetic supplements. He recalls intermittent left-sided 'pulsing' headaches and a faint whooshing sound over the past year that he ignored. No prior seizures. Family history is unremarkable for aneurysms, but an uncle had a 'brain bleed' in his 40s. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fever, neck stiffness preceding the event, or recent illness. He is a nonsmoker. He has been under exam stress and sleeping poorly. There is no history of bleeding disorders or anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: Postictal, drowsy but arousable, oriented to self only. Pupils equal and reactive. There is a mild right facial droop and right pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the right arm and 4+/5 in the right leg. Mild expressive language difficulty. Reflexes brisker on the right with an equivocal right plantar response. No neck rigidity initially. No papilledema seen. A faint bruit is auscultated over the left orbit/temporal region. No external head trauma. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. The combination of thunderclap headache, seizure, and focal deficit in a young patient raises concern for an intracranial hemorrhage.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Electroencephalogram to characterize the seizure
Outpatient MRI in 48 hours
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Noncontrast head CT
stages.explanation: A young patient with thunderclap headache, seizure, and a focal deficit needs an emergent noncontrast head CT to detect intracranial hemorrhage rapidly. CT is fast, sensitive for acute blood, and guides immediate management. Lumbar puncture before imaging is dangerous when a space-occupying hemorrhage or mass effect is possible and is reserved for suspected SAH with a negative CT. Outpatient MRI delays diagnosis of a potentially life-threatening bleed. EEG characterizes seizure activity but does not address the acute structural emergency. CT first is the standard initial step in suspected acute intracranial hemorrhage.

stages.label: NONCONTRAST HEAD CT
stages.content:
Acute lobar intraparenchymal hemorrhage in the left parietal-occipital region, approximately 28 mL
Mild surrounding vasogenic edema with local mass effect
Serpiginous hyperdense structures adjacent to the hematoma suggesting abnormal vessels
No subdural or epidural collection
Ventricles patent without hydrocephalus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a lobar hemorrhage with serpiginous vessels in a 22-year-old, what is the best next imaging study to define the underlying lesion?
options: Repeat noncontrast CT in 24 hours
Catheter cerebral angiography
PET scan of the brain in this clinical context
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard to characterize a suspected arteriovenous malformation, defining the nidus, feeding arteries, draining veins, and any associated aneurysm, which is critical for treatment planning. CTA/MRA can screen, but DSA gives the dynamic, high-resolution detail needed. A repeat noncontrast CT only tracks the hematoma and cannot delineate the vascular architecture. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid disease, irrelevant to an intracranial AVM. PET assesses metabolism/tumor and has no role in defining AVM angioarchitecture. In a young patient with lobar hemorrhage and abnormal vessels, AVM must be excluded with angiography.

stages.label: DIGITAL SUBTRACTION ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Left parieto-occipital arteriovenous malformation with a compact nidus ~3 cm
Feeding arteries from branches of the left middle and posterior cerebral arteries
Single deep draining vein into the internal cerebral vein
No associated flow-related aneurysm identified
Spetzler-Martin grade estimated at 3

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is hemodynamically stable with a GCS of 14 after the bleed. What is the most appropriate acute management priority?
options: Start therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent venous thrombosis as initial therapy
Administer intravenous alteplase for the focal deficit as initial therapy
Immediate craniotomy for AVM resection during the acute bleed as initial therapy
Blood pressure control, seizure management, neurosurgical/neuro-IR consultation,
stages.explanation: Acute management of a ruptured AVM focuses on stabilization: controlled blood pressure lowering, seizure treatment, reversal of any coagulopathy, ICU monitoring, and multidisciplinary neurosurgery/neurointerventional consultation to plan definitive obliteration (microsurgery, embolization, and/or stereotactic radiosurgery) after the patient stabilizes. Emergent resection during the acute hemorrhage is generally avoided unless there is life-threatening mass effect requiring hematoma evacuation, because acute-phase resection of the nidus is high risk. Anticoagulation is contraindicated in active intracranial hemorrhage. Alteplase is absolutely contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke and would be catastrophic. Stabilize first, then plan staged definitive AVM treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After recovery, how should the long-term risk and treatment of this ruptured AVM be framed when counseling him?
options: A ruptured AVM carries a meaningful annual re-bleed risk, so definitive obliteration is generally recommended in a young patient
Lifelong aspirin is the definitive treatment for AVMs with appropriate follow-up
AVMs always resolve spontaneously within a year with appropriate follow-up
Once it has bled, an AVM essentially never bleeds again, so observation is sufficient with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: An AVM that has already ruptured carries a higher annual hemorrhage risk than an unruptured one (overall AVM hemorrhage risk is roughly 2-4% per year, higher after a first bleed and with deep venous drainage or associated aneurysms). In a young patient with long life expectancy and a treatable lesion, definitive obliteration via microsurgery, embolization, and/or stereotactic radiosurgery is generally recommended to reduce cumulative lifetime bleed risk. Saying it never re-bleeds is false and dangerous. Aspirin does not treat AVMs and could worsen bleeding risk. AVMs do not spontaneously resolve. Counseling centers on cumulative re-hemorrhage risk and the rationale for definitive treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Hemorrhagic stroke from rupture of a Spetzler-Martin grade 3 left parieto-occipital arteriovenous malformation
pearls: Lobar hemorrhage in a young, normotensive patient should prompt a search for a vascular malformation (AVM, cavernoma, dural fistula) or aneurysm.
Digital subtraction angiography is the gold standard to define AVM nidus, feeders, and draining veins and to guide treatment.
A previously ruptured AVM and deep venous drainage raise future hemorrhage risk; alteplase and anticoagulation are contraindicated in acute hemorrhagic stroke.
Acute care = stabilize (BP control, seizures, reverse coagulopathy, ICU); definitive obliteration (surgery, embolization, radiosurgery) is usually staged after recovery.

cases.title: Recurrent Spells of Twitching and Tingling on One Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodes of right-hand twitching and numbness with occasional headaches.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents with several months of stereotyped episodes of right-hand and forearm tingling followed by rhythmic twitching of the fingers lasting 1 to 2 minutes, sometimes with brief speech arrest. She remains aware during the events. She has had intermittent dull left-sided headaches. One year ago she had a transient episode of right-arm weakness attributed to 'stress' that fully resolved. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; she takes only an oral contraceptive and a daily multivitamin. No allergies. She works as a graphic designer, drinks occasional wine, does not smoke, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for a sister and mother with similar 'spells' and one relative with a brain hemorrhage in middle age, raising suspicion for a familial condition. She denies fever, weight loss, night sweats, or recent infection. There is no history of head trauma. She has had no generalized convulsions or loss of consciousness. She is otherwise healthy and physically active, with no visual symptoms or vertigo.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact, visual fields full, no papilledema. Motor strength 5/5 throughout with no drift. Sensation intact between events; she reports transient tingling only during spells. Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor. No dysmetria, normal gait, Romberg negative. No neck stiffness. Skin exam without neurocutaneous stigmata. Between episodes the neurologic exam is normal. The stereotyped sensorimotor focal events with preserved awareness localize to the left frontoparietal cortex and are concerning for focal seizures from a structural cortical lesion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial imaging study to evaluate these focal seizures?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound as the first step today
Brain MRI with susceptibility-weighted sequences
Noncontrast head CT only as the first step today
Lumbar puncture as the first step today
stages.explanation: Brain MRI, including susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) or gradient-echo (GRE) sequences, is the best initial study for focal seizures because it detects small structural lesions and is exquisitely sensitive to hemosiderin from prior microhemorrhages, the signature of cavernous malformations. Noncontrast CT often misses small cavernomas and old blood. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial atherosclerosis, not a cortical seizure focus. Lumbar puncture is indicated for suspected infection or subarachnoid hemorrhage, neither of which fits this stereotyped, recurrent, afebrile picture. MRI with blood-sensitive sequences is the key test to identify a cavernous malformation as the seizure substrate.

stages.label: BRAIN MRI
stages.content:
Left frontal cortical-subcortical lesion ~1.4 cm with mixed signal intensity and a 'popcorn' or mulberry appearance
Complete hypointense hemosiderin rim on gradient-echo/SWI sequences
Small focus of recent blood products at the margin, suggesting a prior microhemorrhage
Two additional small SWI hypointense foci elsewhere consistent with multiple lesions
No surrounding large hematoma or significant mass effect

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The popcorn lesion with a complete hemosiderin rim and multiple SWI foci, plus a strong family history, most supports which diagnosis?
options: Cavernous malformation
Arteriovenous malformation
Metastatic tumor as the primary diagnosis
Multiple sclerosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A 'popcorn' or mulberry lesion with a complete hemosiderin rim on GRE/SWI is the classic appearance of a cavernous malformation. Multiple lesions plus a family history of similar spells and hemorrhage strongly suggest familial (autosomal dominant) cavernous malformation syndrome (CCM1/KRIT1 and related genes). AVMs show flow voids and enlarged feeding/draining vessels with a nidus, not a popcorn hemosiderin pattern, and are angiographically occult cavernomas are the opposite. Metastatic tumor would show enhancement, edema, and a different clinical context. Multiple sclerosis produces periventricular/white-matter demyelinating plaques without hemosiderin rims and a different clinical syndrome. The imaging and family history point to multiple cavernous malformations.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has focal aware seizures localizing to the left frontal cavernoma. What is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Start an antiseizure medication
Immediate whole-brain radiation as initial therapy
Start high-dose aspirin as initial therapy
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent further bleeds as monotherapy
stages.explanation: The symptomatic cavernoma is causing focal seizures, so the next steps are to start an antiseizure medication to control seizures and refer to neurosurgery to consider resection of the accessible symptomatic lesion, which can improve seizure control and reduce future bleeding from that lesion. Anticoagulation is contraindicated because it increases hemorrhage risk from cavernomas. Whole-brain radiation is not standard; stereotactic radiosurgery is only occasionally considered for surgically inaccessible lesions and is controversial. Aspirin/antiplatelets are generally avoided when feasible because of bleeding concern. Seizure control plus surgical evaluation of the symptomatic, surgically accessible cavernoma is the appropriate plan.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks about her family and her oral contraceptive. Which counseling point is most accurate?
options: Multiple cavernomas with a positive family history suggest a heritable form
Cavernomas are detectable only by catheter angiography as the immediate next step
Cavernomas are never inherited, so family screening is unnecessary as the immediate next step
She must immediately stop all hormonal contraception because it causes cavernomas as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Multiple cavernous malformations with a positive family history indicate a likely autosomal dominant familial form (CCM genes), so genetic counseling and consideration of MRI screening for symptomatic first-degree relatives are appropriate. Saying cavernomas are never inherited is incorrect, as familial multiple cavernomatosis is well established. Hormonal contraception does not cause cavernomas; while pregnancy and hormones can theoretically influence lesions, blanket cessation is not mandated and decisions are individualized. Cavernomas are angiographically occult, meaning catheter angiography typically does NOT show them, MRI with blood-sensitive sequences is the diagnostic test of choice. Family screening and genetic counseling are the accurate counseling points.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic cavernous malformation (familial multiple cerebral cavernous malformations) causing recurrent microhemorrhage and focal seizures
pearls: A 'popcorn/mulberry' lesion with a complete hemosiderin rim on GRE/SWI is the classic cavernous malformation appearance; cavernomas are angiographically OCCULT.
Multiple cavernomas plus family history suggest autosomal dominant familial CCM; offer genetic counseling and consider MRI screening of relatives.
Symptomatic lesions causing seizures or hemorrhage warrant antiseizure therapy and neurosurgical evaluation for resection; avoid anticoagulation/antiplatelets when possible.
MRI with blood-sensitive sequences (SWI/GRE) is the diagnostic test of choice; CT and catheter angiography often miss these lesions.

cases.title: A Whooshing in the Ear That Won't Stop
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent pulsatile whooshing in the right ear with new headaches.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of relentless pulsatile tinnitus in the right ear, a rhythmic whooshing synchronous with his heartbeat. It is louder when he lies down and bends over, and he can sometimes diminish it by pressing on his neck. Over the past two months he has developed dull holocranial headaches and intermittent blurred vision. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He recalls a head injury from a fall about 3 years ago and had an episode of sinus thrombosis treated years prior. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No allergies. He drinks moderately, does not smoke, and works as an accountant. No family history of vascular malformations. He denies vertigo, hearing loss, fever, or weight loss. He notes the noise is objective at times, his wife can occasionally hear it when close to his ear. He has had no seizures or focal weakness, but reports mild cognitive 'fogginess' recently that concerns him.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented, fluent speech. An audible bruit is appreciated on auscultation over the right mastoid/retroauricular region and diminishes with ipsilateral light carotid compression.
